title: Failed Intubation — Can't Intubate Can't Oxygenate (CICO)
topic: Failed Intubation - Can't Intubate Can't Oxygenate (CICO)
source: DAS Failed Intubation Guidelines 2015 · Frerk et al., BJA
demographics: 58M, 95 kg, BMI 33, OT for emergency laparotomy
presentation: RSI with propofol 200 mg and suxamethonium 120 mg for perforated viscus. Direct laryngoscopy grade 4 view. Video laryngoscope also failed. Patient now apnoeic 4 minutes. SpO2 falling from 98% to 78% and continuing to drop. BMV generating high airway pressures with minimal chest rise. Cricoid pressure applied and maintained throughout.

options.text: Declare CICO emergency to the theatre team and call for senior help immediately
options.rationale: Team declaration activates the CICO drill and summons additional skilled help; solo management of CICO carries near-certain mortality.

options.text: Release cricoid pressure and attempt two-person bag-mask ventilation with oropharyngeal and nasopharyngeal airways
options.rationale: Cricoid pressure impairs mask ventilation and SGA insertion; releasing it is the first DAS CICO management step — trainees routinely miss this.

options.text: Insert i-gel supraglottic airway (size 4) — maximum 2 attempts
options.rationale: SGA achieves rescue oxygenation in the majority of CICO scenarios; limiting to 2 attempts prevents time-wasting per DAS guidance.

options.text: Prepare front-of-neck access equipment at the neck now — scalpel, 10-blade, bougie, cuffed 6.0 ETT
options.rationale: eFONA preparation must occur in parallel with SGA attempts, not after SGA failure — the hypoxic window permits no sequential delays.

options.text: Perform emergency front-of-neck access (scalpel-finger-bougie technique) if SGA fails to oxygenate
options.rationale: eFONA is the definitive CICO rescue when SGA fails; further delay beyond this point is fatal.

options.text: Request surgeon to stand by for open surgical tracheostomy as final rescue
options.rationale: Surgical tracheostomy is the last-resort definitive airway if eFONA fails; the request must be made early but executed last.

management_logic: CICO management follows the DAS 2015 failed intubation drill. The non-obvious critical step is releasing cricoid pressure before SGA insertion — trainees commonly maintain it throughout, impairing both BMV and SGA placement. eFONA preparation must begin in parallel with SGA placement, not sequentially after SGA failure.
learning_point: Cricoid pressure release is mandatory at the start of the CICO drill, not an afterthought. eFONA preparation must be simultaneous with SGA attempts — parallel, not sequential — because the window for reversible hypoxia is approximately 60 seconds.
evidence_basis: Difficult Airway Society Failed Intubation Guidelines 2015 (Frerk et al., BJA 2015; PMID 26556848)

title: Malignant Hyperthermia — Intraoperative Trigger
topic: Malignant Hyperthermia - Intraoperative Trigger
source: Miller's Anaesthesia 9th Ed, Ch 38 · MHAUS Protocol
presentation: 25 minutes into sevoflurane anaesthesia. ETCO2 rising from 35 to 68 mmHg despite increased minute ventilation. Temperature 38.9°C and rising (was 37.1°C at induction). Masseter rigidity noted at intubation, not escalated at the time. HR 138/min irregular. Generalised muscle rigidity developing. Capnograph shows sustained exponential ETCO2 rise.
constraints: Dantrolene sodium not stocked in OT — urgent pharmacy dispatch required, estimated 12-minute delay

options.text: Declare MH emergency, activate MH protocol, call for all available help
options.rationale: Team activation summons additional hands and initiates the systematic MH response; MH is rapidly fatal without coordinated multi-person management.

options.text: Discontinue sevoflurane immediately — remove vaporiser from circuit, switch to TIVA with propofol infusion
options.rationale: Removing the triggering agent is the single most important step; continued volatile exposure accelerates the hypermetabolic crisis exponentially.

options.text: Dispatch runner to pharmacy urgently for dantrolene sodium — 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat as needed
options.rationale: Given the 12-minute delay, dantrolene must be dispatched in the first 60–90 seconds — in parallel with trigger removal — to minimise time to definitive treatment.

options.text: Hyperventilate with 100% O2 at maximum fresh gas flow (≥10 L/min) to wash out volatile and treat hypercarbia
options.rationale: High-flow O2 and hyperventilation reduce ETCO2 and accelerate volatile washout while awaiting dantrolene.

options.text: Treat metabolic complications — sodium bicarbonate for acidosis, calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia, amiodarone for arrhythmias
options.rationale: Symptomatic treatment of metabolic derangements is necessary but secondary to trigger removal and dantrolene dispatch.

options.text: Active cooling — ice packs to axillae and groins, cold IV crystalloid, cooling blanket
options.rationale: Active cooling addresses hyperthermia but is supportive; definitive treatment is dantrolene and trigger removal.

management_logic: The constraint — dantrolene not immediately available — moves pharmacy dispatch to step 3, before it would normally rank. A 12-minute delay means requesting at minute 1 versus minute 5 saves 4 minutes of dantrolene-free crisis. Trainees commonly complete all other steps before requesting dantrolene, losing critical time.
learning_point: When dantrolene is not immediately available, pharmacy dispatch must occur within the first 60–90 seconds of MH declaration — in parallel with trigger removal, not after. The constraint changes the priority hierarchy.
evidence_basis: MHAUS Treatment Protocol; Rosenberg et al., Orphanet J Rare Diseases 2007 (PMID 17456235)

title: Intraoperative Anaphylaxis with Beta-Blocker Resistance
topic: Intraoperative Anaphylaxis with Beta-Blocker Resistance
source: AAGBI Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2009 · Stoelting's Anaesthesia and Co-existing Disease
demographics: 35F, 68 kg, 8 weeks pregnant, OT for emergency appendicectomy
presentation: 3 minutes after IV cefazolin 1g at induction. Sudden cardiovascular collapse. Generalised erythema and urticaria developing rapidly. Angioedema of lips and tongue. Bilateral wheeze. SpO2 falling despite 100% O2. Regular medications include propranolol 80 mg BD for essential hypertension.
constraints: Propranolol 80 mg BD — beta-blockade blunts adrenaline response

options.text: Declare anaphylaxis, call for help, stop cefazolin infusion immediately
options.rationale: Removing the trigger and summoning help are simultaneous first steps; continuing the causative agent prolongs antigenic stimulus.

options.text: Adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.1–0.5 mg IV bolus titrated in 1:10,000 dilution — anticipate higher doses required due to beta-blockade
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is first-line in the anaesthetised patient; beta-blockade blunts chronotropic and inotropic response, requiring higher repeated doses than standard.

options.text: Position supine with legs elevated and 15° left lateral tilt for aortocaval decompression
options.rationale: Left lateral tilt is mandatory at 8 weeks gestation to prevent aortocaval compression by the gravid uterus; omission worsens haemodynamic collapse.

options.text: Rapid IV crystalloid bolus 1–2 L Hartmann's solution via large-bore cannula
options.rationale: Anaphylactic shock has a significant distributive component from capillary leak; aggressive fluid resuscitation is a key parallel intervention.

options.text: Glucagon 1–2 mg IV bolus followed by infusion — specific treatment for beta-blocker resistant anaphylaxis
options.rationale: Glucagon bypasses the beta-adrenoceptor via glucagon receptors, restoring inotropy and chronotropy when adrenaline fails due to beta-blockade — the key non-obvious step.

options.text: Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV and hydrocortisone 200 mg IV
options.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids prevent biphasic reaction and treat urticaria but have no role in acute haemodynamic rescue.

management_logic: Beta-blockade blunts adrenaline's cardiovascular effects, necessitating glucagon — a step invisible in standard anaphylaxis management. The pregnancy mandates left lateral tilt immediately on positioning. Trainees commonly omit glucagon entirely and fail to tilt, missing both constraint-driven modifications.
learning_point: Glucagon is the specific pharmacological rescue for beta-blocker-resistant anaphylaxis. It is not a standard anaphylaxis step — only required when beta-blockade is present and adrenaline response is inadequate. If a patient on beta-blockers fails adequate adrenaline doses, glucagon must be given immediately.
evidence_basis: AAGBI Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2009; Simons et al., WAO Journal 2011 (PMID 21960707)

title: Laryngospasm — Immediate Post-Extubation
topic: Laryngospasm - Immediate Post-Extubation
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Airway Management
presentation: Immediately after tracheal extubation, complete inspiratory obstruction. Initial stridor replaced by silence with paradoxical chest-abdominal movement. SpO2 96% and falling rapidly. Patient semi-conscious, partially emerging from anaesthesia. No obvious secretions or blood in oropharynx.

options.text: Apply CPAP via tight-fitting facemask with 100% O2 and jaw thrust
options.rationale: CPAP is first-line for laryngospasm; positive pressure at the glottis helps force adducted cords apart and maintains oxygenation.

options.text: Jaw thrust and head extension to optimise upper airway patency
options.rationale: Airway positioning is immediate and simultaneous with CPAP — reduces soft tissue apposition and improves the geometry for mask ventilation.

options.text: Larson manoeuvre — firm bilateral pressure in the notch between the mastoid process and posterior ramus of the mandible
options.rationale: Pressure at Larson's point stimulates the facial nerve branch and reliably breaks laryngospasm; this step is underused and often omitted by trainees in favour of immediate drug treatment.

options.text: Suxamethonium 0.1–0.5 mg/kg IV (or 4 mg/kg IM if no IV access) for refractory laryngospasm
options.rationale: Succinylcholine is the definitive pharmacological treatment when CPAP and Larson manoeuvre fail; it abolishes the spasm within 30–60 seconds.

options.text: Sub-anaesthetic propofol 0.5 mg/kg IV if laryngospasm is partial and patient still has some airflow
options.rationale: Small propofol bolus can deepen anaesthesia and break partial laryngospasm but is inappropriate for complete obstruction where CPAP and succinylcholine are required.

options.text: Reintubation with RSI if SpO2 <85% and not responding to all above measures
options.rationale: Reintubation is the rescue for refractory laryngospasm unresponsive to pharmacological management; preparing for this runs parallel to steps 4 and 5.

management_logic: Laryngospasm management proceeds from non-pharmacological to pharmacological. CPAP must be maintained throughout. The Larson manoeuvre — firm digital pressure bilaterally at the notch between the mastoid and posterior mandibular ramus — reliably breaks laryngospasm and must precede succinylcholine. Trainees skip directly to drug treatment and omit step 3.
learning_point: The Larson manoeuvre is a highly effective physical technique for laryngospasm that trainees routinely omit. CPAP plus Larson must be applied before succinylcholine. Succinylcholine is definitive but is step 4, not step 1.
evidence_basis: Larson CP Jr. Laryngospasm — the best treatment. Anesthesiology 1998 (PMID 9757578); Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16

title: High-Risk Extubation — Morbid Obesity and Proven Difficult Airway
topic: High-Risk Extubation - Morbid Obesity and Proven Difficult Airway
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Endotracheal Extubation
demographics: 72M, 145 kg, BMI 49 · PACU after laparotomy for bowel resection
presentation: Ready for extubation assessment. Original intubation required video laryngoscope plus bougie after two failed direct laryngoscopy attempts (grade 3 view). Patient has OSA on home CPAP. SpO2 95% on 40% O2. Responding to commands. TOF ratio 1.0 after sugammadex 200 mg. RR 14/min with adequate tidal volumes. Concerns: morbid obesity, proven difficult airway, OSA, residual opioid effect from intraoperative remifentanil.
constraints: Grade 3 laryngoscopy view at original intubation — VL + bougie required
Morbid obesity BMI 49 — reduced FRC and rapid desaturation
Home CPAP for OSA — airway tends to collapse post-extubation

options.text: Confirm all extubation criteria met: TOF 1.0, sustained head-lift >5 seconds, adequate minute ventilation, SpO2 acceptable, cooperative
options.rationale: Rigorous confirmation of extubation criteria in a high-risk patient is mandatory before any airway device is removed — one failed criterion is a reason to delay.

options.text: Pre-oxygenate to SpO2 100% — administer 100% O2 for 3–5 minutes before extubation
options.rationale: Maximising SpO2 before extubation extends the apnoeic window; morbid obesity markedly reduces FRC and time to desaturation.

options.text: Insert 14 Fr Cook airway exchange catheter (AEC) through the ETT before extubation — leave AEC in situ for 20–30 minutes post-extubation
options.rationale: AEC provides a conduit to reintubation over a guide if extubation fails in a proven difficult airway; this step is the key non-obvious intervention trainees omit in high-risk extubation.

options.text: Position sitting upright or 30–45° head-up immediately before and after extubation
options.rationale: Upright positioning maximises FRC and reduces the risk of airway collapse in morbid obesity; supine extubation in a BMI 49 patient worsens respiratory mechanics significantly.

options.text: Apply CPAP or high-flow nasal oxygen immediately after extubation
options.rationale: Home CPAP patient with OSA requires airway support post-extubation to prevent obstructive episodes; high-flow nasal O2 also provides apnoeic oxygenation if needed.

options.text: Document difficult airway alert, arrange 1:1 PACU nursing, and prepare reintubation equipment at bedside for minimum 2 hours
options.rationale: Extended monitoring with documented difficult airway alert and immediately available reintubation equipment is required; AEC removal after confirmed stability.

management_logic: In a morbidly obese patient with a proven difficult airway and OSA, extubation over an AEC converts a potentially catastrophic airway loss to a manageable situation. All three constraints alter management: the difficult original intubation mandates AEC, morbid obesity mandates upright positioning and pre-oxygenation, and OSA mandates immediate post-extubation airway support. Trainees perform standard extubation and omit the AEC.
learning_point: In any patient where the original intubation was difficult, extubation must be planned with the same rigour. Extubation over an airway exchange catheter is not optional in morbid obesity with a proven difficult airway — it is the minimum safe standard.
evidence_basis: DAS Extubation Guidelines 2012 (Popat et al., Anaesthesia; PMID 22765843); Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16

title: Video Laryngoscopy — Can See But Cannot Intubate
topic: Video Laryngoscopy - Can See But Cannot Intubate
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Video Laryngoscopy
demographics: 48M, 88 kg · OT for elective laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication
presentation: Routine RSI. Direct laryngoscopy grade 3 view. Video laryngoscope (GlideScope) inserted: excellent grade 1 view of glottis on screen. ETT with preshaped 60° stylet advancing well, but unable to pass tube through cords despite clear visualisation. Multiple attempts with stylet manipulation unsuccessful. SpO2 98% on bag-mask ventilation between attempts.

options.text: Remove the video laryngoscope and ventilate the patient — do not persist with further attempts at current technique
options.rationale: Repeated failed attempts cause airway trauma, oedema, and bleeding that progressively worsen the situation; oxygenation must be maintained before optimising technique.

options.text: Recheck stylet curve — reshape to match the GlideScope blade angle (60°), ensure stylet tip does not protrude beyond ETT
options.rationale: With angulated VL blades, the ETT stylet must precisely match the blade curvature; a sub-optimal stylet shape is the most common cause of cannot-intubate-despite-good-view.

options.text: Reattempt with video laryngoscope: advance ETT under screen guidance, withdraw stylet during tube advancement as tip passes cords, rotate ETT 90° anticlockwise if encountering resistance
options.rationale: Withdrawing the stylet during tube advancement and rotating the ETT 90° anticlockwise when caught on the arytenoid are established techniques for successful tube delivery with angulated VL blades.

options.text: If VL still unsuccessful: insert bougie under direct or video view and railroad ETT ≥6.0 mm over bougie
options.rationale: A bougie can be placed under VL guidance when an ETT cannot be delivered directly; ETT is then railroaded over the bougie with laryngoscope still in place.

options.text: Insert supraglottic airway as rescue oxygenation device and call for senior help before further intubation attempts
options.rationale: If two optimised VL attempts have failed, the situation is escalating to a failed intubation; SGA for oxygenation and senior input are required before further attempts.

options.text: Document all attempts, communicate airway findings to PACU team and document in patient notes
options.rationale: Accurate documentation of the airway findings and techniques used is essential for future anaesthetic management and patient safety.

management_logic: The cannot-intubate-despite-good-VL-view scenario is a recognised VL pitfall specific to angulated blades. The ETT must follow the same curve as the blade; an incorrectly shaped stylet will direct the tube anteriorly into the anterior tracheal wall rather than through the cords. Rotating 90° anticlockwise and withdrawing the stylet as the tip passes the cords are the key technical manoeuvres. Trainees persist with the same technique repeatedly without diagnosing the stylet geometry problem.
learning_point: An excellent view on video laryngoscopy with an angulated blade does not guarantee easy intubation. The ETT stylet must precisely match the blade angle. When the tube cannot pass despite a good view, the problem is almost always stylet geometry — not laryngeal anatomy.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Video Laryngoscopy; Aziz et al. Use of video laryngoscopy, BJAA 2012

title: Awake Fibreoptic Intubation — Technique and Preparation
topic: Awake Fibreoptic Intubation - Technique and Preparation
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Awake Fibreoptic Endotracheal Intubation
demographics: 56M, 78 kg · Pre-op for anterior cervical discectomy C4–C5, unstable cervical spine
presentation: Pre-operative. MRI shows C4-5 disc prolapse with myelopathy. Neck extension and flexion both restricted and painful. Mallampati III, mouth opening 3 cm, reduced neck mobility. Surgical team requests awake fibreoptic intubation. Patient alert and cooperative but anxious. No known difficult airway history. Neurological baseline: mild weakness both hands.
constraints: Unstable cervical spine — no neck movement permitted during intubation
Anxious patient — cooperation essential for awake intubation technique

options.text: Administer glycopyrrolate 0.2 mg IV — antisialagogue to reduce secretions before airway topicalisation
options.rationale: Secretions are the primary enemy of fibreoptic intubation; glycopyrrolate 15–20 minutes before the procedure dries the airway and dramatically improves visualisation — this must come first.

options.text: Topicalise the airway: nebulised 4% lidocaine (4 mL) for 15 minutes, then additional spray-as-you-go lidocaine through the fibreoptic scope — total dose not to exceed 7 mg/kg
options.rationale: Adequate airway anaesthesia is essential for patient cooperation and prevents coughing that could displace the cervical spine; insufficient topicalisation is the most common cause of awake FOI failure.

options.text: Administer careful sedation — midazolam 1–2 mg + remifentanil 0.025–0.05 mcg/kg/min infusion, maintaining verbal contact throughout
options.rationale: Light sedation improves patient cooperation without obtunding airway reflexes; remifentanil blunts the intubation response and suppresses cough while maintaining consciousness.

options.text: Perform nasal fibreoptic intubation — nasal route preferred as ETT angle naturally approximates upper airway anatomy
options.rationale: Nasal route is easier for awake FOI as the curvature of the nasopharynx guides the scope; requires vasoconstriction of nasal mucosa with oxymetazoline first. Oral route if nasal is contraindicated.

options.text: Confirm intubation via fibreoptic view of tracheal rings and carina before advancing ETT — confirm ETCO2 after tube placement
options.rationale: Fibreoptic confirmation of tracheal position before advancing the ETT, and ETCO2 confirmation after, prevents oesophageal placement — the most dangerous complication of awake FOI.

options.text: Induce general anaesthesia only after confirmed tracheal intubation — neurological assessment if possible before full GA
options.rationale: GA induction before confirmed intubation defeats the purpose of awake FOI in an unstable cervical spine; neurological assessment post-intubation before GA documents any change from manipulation.

management_logic: Awake FOI success depends on systematic preparation: antisialagogue first (glycopyrrolate before topicalisation), thorough airway anaesthesia second, appropriate sedation third. The most common failures are inadequate drying (secretions obscure view) and inadequate topicalisation (patient moves/coughs). In an unstable cervical spine, the nasal route is preferred and neck movement must be zero throughout.
learning_point: Glycopyrrolate must be given 15–20 minutes before airway topicalisation — it is not an optional step. A wet airway with secretions makes fibreoptic intubation nearly impossible regardless of technique quality.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Awake Fibreoptic Endotracheal Intubation; DAS Difficult Airway Guidelines 2015

title: Epidural Haematoma — Recognition and Emergency Management
topic: Epidural Haematoma - Recognition and Emergency Management
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Epidural Haematoma
demographics: 58F, 72 kg · Post-major gynaecological surgery, thoracic epidural in situ
presentation: Day 1 post-operative. Thoracic epidural (T8-9) with 0.1% levobupivacaine + fentanyl infusion running at 8 mL/hour providing good analgesia. Patient reports sudden onset severe back pain — different from surgical pain. Unexpectedly prolonged motor block — cannot lift either leg. Unable to void urine for 4 hours. No fever. Neurological deficit progressing over the past 90 minutes.
constraints: Prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC — last dose given 6 hours ago

options.text: Stop epidural infusion immediately — this is a neurological emergency, not analgesic failure
options.rationale: Unexpected motor block + new back pain + bladder dysfunction = epidural haematoma until proven otherwise; continuing infusion masks neurological deterioration and delays diagnosis.

options.text: Urgent MRI spine without delay — do not wait for blood results before imaging
options.rationale: MRI is the investigation of choice and must be obtained immediately; surgical decompression within 6–8 hours of symptom onset preserves neurological function — every minute of imaging delay costs neurological outcome.

options.text: Simultaneous urgent spinal/neurosurgical consultation — parallel to MRI, not after it
options.rationale: Surgical decompression is the only effective treatment; the surgical team must be contacted simultaneously with MRI to prevent sequential delays — theatre prep begins while images are being acquired.

options.text: Check anti-Xa level and coagulation screen — determine anticoagulation status before surgical consent
options.rationale: Enoxaparin 6 hours ago has significant residual anti-Xa activity; reversal with protamine sulphate may be required before theatre — but this result must not delay imaging or surgical consultation.

options.text: Catheterise bladder for urinary retention
options.rationale: Urinary retention causes additional discomfort and bladder injury; catheterisation is needed regardless of surgical outcome and can be done while awaiting MRI.

options.text: Document onset time of symptoms precisely and preserve epidural catheter — notify departmental lead and complete incident documentation
options.rationale: Precise symptom onset documentation is critical for surgical decision-making and medico-legal purposes; incident reporting is important but is the last priority.

management_logic: Epidural haematoma is a time-critical diagnosis. The classic triad is new back pain + unexpectedly prolonged motor block + bladder dysfunction. Every hour between symptom onset and surgical decompression directly determines neurological outcome. MRI must be obtained urgently regardless of anticoagulation status — anticoagulation affects surgical planning, not the imaging decision. Trainees order blood tests first, then imaging, then consult — sequential delays are catastrophic.
learning_point: In suspected epidural haematoma, MRI must be obtained immediately regardless of anticoagulation status. Anticoagulation affects surgical planning — it does not delay imaging. The window for useful surgical decompression is 6–8 hours from symptom onset.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17; ASRA Practice Advisory on Neurological Complications of Regional Anesthesia 2018 (PMID 29200598)

title: Total Spinal After Epidural Top-Up for Caesarean Section
topic: Total Spinal After Epidural Top-Up for Caesarean Section
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Total Spinal Anaesthesia
presentation: Established labour epidural (L3-4) providing good analgesia. Decision for emergency caesarean section (category 2). Epidural catheter aspirated — no blood, no CSF. Registrar injects 15 mL 2% lidocaine + 1:200,000 adrenaline. Four minutes later: patient reports difficulty breathing and arm weakness. Rapidly loses consciousness. BP 58/32 mmHg, HR 44/min. Apnoeic.
constraints: Gravid uterus — aortocaval compression risk in supine position
Full stomach — aspiration risk; RSI mandatory

options.text: Call for emergency help — declare crisis, activate obstetric and neonatal emergency teams
options.rationale: Total spinal in a pregnant patient is a combined anaesthetic and obstetric emergency requiring multiple teams simultaneously; delayed team activation is a recognised contributor to maternal mortality.

options.text: Left lateral tilt 15° — wedge under right hip immediately to prevent aortocaval compression
options.rationale: In a supine hypotensive pregnant patient, aortocaval compression prevents adequate venous return regardless of vasopressors; left tilt must be applied before or simultaneously with all other interventions — trainees omit this step in the emergency.

options.text: RSI with cricoid pressure — secure airway immediately; patient is unconscious with full stomach
options.rationale: Loss of consciousness with impending apnoea in a pregnant patient (full stomach) mandates immediate RSI; bag-mask ventilation alone risks aspiration and does not protect the airway.

options.text: Vasopressor bolus — phenylephrine 100 mcg IV or metaraminol 0.5–1 mg IV; atropine 0.6 mg IV for bradycardia
options.rationale: Total spinal causes complete sympathetic blockade to cervical levels causing profound vasodilation and bradycardia; vasopressors and atropine must be given simultaneously with airway management.

options.text: Rapid IV crystalloid bolus — 500–1000 mL Hartmann's to support vasopressor effect
options.rationale: Volume resuscitation supports vasopressor effect; however vasopressors take precedence — fluids alone are inadequate for the magnitude of vasodilation from total spinal.

options.text: Assess fetal heart rate and coordinate with obstetric team for emergency caesarean if fetal compromise confirmed
options.rationale: Once maternal resuscitation is underway, fetal wellbeing must be assessed; emergency CS may be required for fetal salvage but maternal stability is the prerequisite.

management_logic: Total spinal in pregnancy presents three simultaneous threats: maternal airway loss, cardiovascular collapse, and fetal compromise. Left lateral tilt for aortocaval decompression is mandatory before any other intervention — a supine pregnant patient cannot be resuscitated from any cause without tilt. RSI is mandatory given full stomach risk. Vasopressors must be given immediately; fluids alone are insufficient.
learning_point: In total spinal in a pregnant patient, left lateral tilt must precede all other interventions or be applied simultaneously. A supine pregnant patient in cardiovascular collapse cannot respond to vasopressors or resuscitation due to complete aortocaval obstruction by the gravid uterus.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17; OAA/DAS Guidelines for Management of Failed Tracheal Intubation in Obstetrics 2015

title: Post-Dural Puncture Headache with Cranial Nerve Involvement
topic: Post-Dural Puncture Headache with Cranial Nerve Involvement
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Post-Dural Puncture Headache
demographics: 24F, 62 kg · Postnatal ward, day 2 after emergency caesarean under spinal anaesthesia
presentation: Worsening bifrontal-occipital headache since day 1. Severe when upright (NRS 9/10), relieved by lying supine. Nausea and neck stiffness reported. New diplopia today — difficulty looking to the right. Afebrile. No meningism on formal examination. BP 118/74 mmHg. SpO2 99%.

options.text: Urgent MRI brain with contrast to exclude subdural haematoma — cranial nerve involvement changes the management algorithm
options.rationale: Diplopia after PDPH may represent 6th nerve palsy from intracranial hypotension or, more critically, subdural haematoma from CSF depletion; MRI is mandatory before epidural blood patch when cranial nerve palsy is present.

options.text: Urgent neurology or ophthalmology review for 6th nerve palsy assessment
options.rationale: Cranial nerve involvement is an atypical PDPH presentation requiring specialist input to determine whether this is abducens palsy from intracranial hypotension or a sign of more serious intracranial pathology.

options.text: Epidural blood patch (EBP) — 20 mL autologous blood at the same lumbar level once imaging excludes contraindication
options.rationale: EBP is definitive treatment for PDPH with cranial nerve involvement and should not be delayed once imaging is clear; this is not a conservative management case.

options.text: Paracetamol 1g QDS and caffeine 300 mg PO — safe in breastfeeding; avoid NSAIDs and codeine
options.rationale: Non-opioid adjuncts provide symptomatic relief and are safe in breastfeeding; NSAIDs have restricted use in breastfeeding and codeine is contraindicated due to unpredictable neonatal morphine exposure.

options.text: Maintain oral hydration 2–3 L per day
options.rationale: Hydration is standard conservative PDPH management but is adjunctive, not primary treatment, especially with cranial nerve involvement.

options.text: Obtain informed consent for EBP including risks of epidural haematoma, infection, back pain, and incomplete relief
options.rationale: Consent for EBP is essential before the procedure but follows the clinical decisions at steps 1–5.

management_logic: PDPH with cranial nerve involvement (6th nerve palsy causing diplopia) is an atypical presentation that changes the management hierarchy. Standard PDPH management begins conservatively — this case does not. Imaging must precede EBP to exclude subdural haematoma, which can complicate severe PDPH from CSF depletion. Breastfeeding restricts analgesic options: paracetamol and caffeine are safe; codeine is contraindicated.
learning_point: Cranial nerve palsy in PDPH mandates MRI before epidural blood patch — it may represent subdural haematoma rather than simple intracranial hypotension. This is not a conservative management scenario.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — PDPH; Amorim et al., EBP for PDPH — systematic review, Cochrane 2012

title: Inadvertent Intravascular Epidural Catheter — Positive Test Dose
topic: Inadvertent Intravascular Epidural Catheter - Positive Test Dose
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Epidural Anaesthesia Technique
demographics: 34F, 71 kg, 39 weeks gestation · Labour suite, epidural siting for labour analgesia
presentation: Epidural catheter placed at L3-4. Aspiration of catheter shows no blood and no CSF. Test dose administered: 3 mL 2% lidocaine + adrenaline 15 mcg (1:200,000). Sixty seconds later: maternal HR increases from 82 to 118/min. Patient reports palpitations and feels 'anxious'. No neurological symptoms.
constraints: Active labour — patient requesting pain relief urgently

options.text: Do NOT administer the planned epidural loading dose — the test dose is positive for intravascular placement
options.rationale: A HR increase of ≥20 bpm within 60 seconds of adrenaline-containing test dose indicates intravascular catheter placement; administering a full epidural dose intravenously will cause LAST — this must not happen.

options.text: Withdraw the epidural catheter completely and remove from the epidural space
options.rationale: An intravascular catheter must be removed; repositioning is not safe as the catheter tip is in a vessel and cannot be reliably redirected.

options.text: Reassure the patient, monitor for signs of LAST from the test dose — HR, BP, neurological observations for 5 minutes
options.rationale: The 15 mcg adrenaline + 3 mL 2% lidocaine test dose may cause transient symptoms but is unlikely to cause LAST at this dose; monitoring confirms resolution before reattempt.

options.text: Reattempt epidural at L2-3 or L4-5 after catheter removal and clinical assessment
options.rationale: Labour epidural analgesia can be reattempted at a different level once the intravascular catheter is removed and the patient is stable — address the patient's pain after safe management of the complication.

options.text: Re-aspirate the new catheter and repeat test dose before any loading dose — confirm negative at new site
options.rationale: Standard safety protocol must be repeated at the new site; aspiration and test dose confirmation must precede every loading dose regardless of confidence in placement.

options.text: Document the event, notify the obstetric team, and record in the patient notes
options.rationale: Documentation of the inadvertent intravascular placement, the positive test dose finding, and management is mandatory for patient safety and medico-legal purposes.

management_logic: A positive epidural test dose (HR increase ≥20 bpm with adrenaline-containing test dose) reliably identifies intravascular catheter placement. The correct response is immediate cessation — not repositioning the catheter in the same space. The test dose adrenaline 15 mcg causes transient tachycardia but not LAST; the danger is administering the full epidural bolus through an intravascular catheter. Trainees sometimes ignore a modest HR increase and proceed with the loading dose.
learning_point: A heart rate increase of ≥20 bpm within 60 seconds of an adrenaline-containing test dose is a positive result indicating intravascular catheter placement. The loading dose must not be given. The catheter must be withdrawn completely.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17; Guay J. The epidural test dose: a review. Anesth Analg 2006 (PMID 16717327)

title: LAST — Seizure and Cardiac Arrest After Interscalene Block
topic: LAST - Seizure and Cardiac Arrest After Interscalene Block
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Regional Anaesthesia Complications; Advanced Anaesthesia Review Ch 22
presentation: 25 mL ropivacaine 0.5% injected under ultrasound guidance for interscalene brachial plexus block. Three minutes after injection: sudden loss of consciousness and tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds. Seizure terminates spontaneously then immediately: no palpable pulse, ECG shows broad complex irregular rhythm (VF). Resuscitation commenced.
constraints: Intralipid 20% not immediately available — urgent pharmacy dispatch, estimated 5-minute delay
Patient on atenolol 50 mg OD — beta-blockade may blunt adrenaline response

options.text: Call cardiac arrest team and commence CPR immediately — 30:2, 100% O2
options.rationale: Cardiac arrest from LAST requires immediate CPR; without circulatory support no drug or defibrillation can be effective.

options.text: Dispatch urgent runner to pharmacy for Intralipid 20% NOW — given 5-minute delay, request must be made in first 60 seconds of arrest
options.rationale: Intralipid is the definitive treatment for LAST cardiac arrest; a 5-minute delay means dispatching at arrest minute 1 vs minute 5 saves 4 minutes of Intralipid-free cardiac arrest — the constraint makes this step 2, not step 5.

options.text: Defibrillate VF — 200J biphasic if shockable rhythm confirmed
options.rationale: Defibrillation for shockable rhythm runs simultaneously with CPR; LAST-induced VF can be shock-responsive, though prolonged refractory VF is the hallmark of severe LAST.

options.text: Adrenaline — use small doses 10–100 mcg IV rather than standard ALS 1 mg bolus in LAST context with beta-blockade
options.rationale: Standard 1 mg adrenaline in LAST may worsen arrhythmia; AAGBI guidelines recommend small doses in LAST; beta-blockade requires careful titration — 100 mcg is a reasonable starting dose.

options.text: When Intralipid arrives: 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus over 1 minute, then infusion 0.25 mL/kg/min — repeat bolus if VF recurs
options.rationale: Intralipid lipid sink sequesters free ropivacaine away from cardiac sodium channels; definitive treatment that may restore spontaneous circulation when CPR and defibrillation cannot.

options.text: Notify cardiothoracic team early for CPB/ECMO if refractory — LAST arrest can be prolonged
options.rationale: LAST cardiac arrest can be refractory for 30–60 minutes; CPB/ECMO maintains perfusion until drug redistribution allows recovery — early notification is required.

management_logic: LAST cardiac arrest management follows AAGBI/ASRA guidelines with two constraint-driven modifications. The Intralipid delay mandates immediate dispatch in parallel with CPR — not after defibrillation. Beta-blockade from atenolol changes the adrenaline dosing strategy — standard 1 mg boluses may exacerbate LAST-induced arrhythmia. Trainees use standard ALS adrenaline doses and fail to dispatch Intralipid until after other resuscitation steps.
learning_point: In LAST cardiac arrest, adrenaline should be used in small doses (10–100 mcg) rather than standard ALS 1 mg — standard doses may worsen LAST-induced arrhythmia. Intralipid dispatch must occur within the first 60 seconds when not immediately available.
evidence_basis: AAGBI Guidelines: Management of Severe Local Anaesthetic Toxicity 2010; ASRA Checklist for LAST Treatment; Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e Ch 17

title: Residual Neuromuscular Block — Post-Operative Hypoxia
topic: Residual Neuromuscular Block - Post-Operative Hypoxia
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Endotracheal Extubation
demographics: 64M, 96 kg · PACU after 3-hour laparotomy for bowel resection
presentation: 45 minutes after extubation in PACU. SpO2 falling from 96% to 87%. RR 22/min but shallow with paradoxical breathing. Patient agitated but unable to cooperate fully. Received vecuronium 10 mg at induction + 4 mg at 90 min. Sugammadex not given at end of case. TOF not checked prior to extubation. Facemask O2 applied by PACU nurse but SpO2 not recovering.
constraints: Sugammadex not available on PACU — used last dose earlier in list, not restocked

options.text: Apply jaw thrust and 100% O2 via tight-fitting mask — support ventilation manually if required
options.rationale: Immediate airway support with supplemental O2 and positioning is the first response to post-extubation hypoxia regardless of cause; residual NMB must be on the differential.

options.text: Quantify residual block with peripheral nerve stimulator — assess TOF ratio at adductor pollicis
options.rationale: TOF ratio must be measured before giving reversal; neostigmine is ineffective if no twitches are present (PTC 0), and may cause cholinergic crisis if block is minimal — you must know the depth before reversing.

options.text: Neostigmine 50 mcg/kg IV + glycopyrrolate 10 mcg/kg IV if TOF shows 1 or more twitches
options.rationale: Neostigmine reverses partial NMB when twitches are present; must be given with glycopyrrolate to prevent bradycardia; given after TOF assessment confirms partial block.

options.text: If no twitches on TOF (complete block): reintubate and ventilate until block resolves spontaneously
options.rationale: Complete NMB (TOF = 0, PTC = 0) cannot be reversed with neostigmine; reintubation and ventilation are the only safe options until the block wears off — usually 30–60 minutes for vecuronium.

options.text: Restock sugammadex for next case and arrange ongoing monitoring — SpO2, RR, TOF at 15-minute intervals until full reversal confirmed
options.rationale: Once reversal is initiated or reintubation performed, ongoing monitoring confirms adequacy of reversal; TOF >0.9 at adductor pollicis is the standard for safe extubation.

options.text: Complete critical incident form and debrief — failure to reverse and failure to confirm reversal before extubation
options.rationale: Failure to administer reversal and failure to confirm TOF ratio before extubation is a system and individual error requiring incident documentation for learning.

management_logic: Residual NMB is managed in sequence: airway support first, then quantify block depth with TOF, then choose reversal method based on TOF result. The critical non-obvious step is that neostigmine cannot be given until TOF is assessed — if TOF = 0, neostigmine is ineffective and reintubation is required. Sugammadex unavailability changes the algorithm to neostigmine-or-reintubation.
learning_point: Neostigmine reversal requires at least one TOF twitch to be effective. Administering neostigmine without assessing TOF depth is incorrect — if the block is complete (TOF = 0), reintubation is the only safe option. TOF ratio must be confirmed before extubation.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16; Brull SJ & Kopman AF. Current status of neuromuscular reversal and monitoring. Anesthesiology 2017 (PMID 27941428)

title: Acute Intraoperative Bronchospasm
topic: Acute Intraoperative Bronchospasm
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16; Miller's Basics of Anaesthesia 8th Ed
presentation: 20 minutes into general anaesthesia. Progressive increase in peak airway pressure from 18 to 42 cmH2O. SpO2 falling from 99% to 91%. Audible wheeze on auscultation bilaterally. Capnograph shows characteristic shark-fin ETCO2 waveform. Patient has known asthma — on salbutamol PRN. Last used inhaler 3 days ago. Was not given any preoperative bronchodilator.
constraints: Laparoscopy ongoing — cannot deepen anaesthesia with volatile agent easily due to high gas leak around pneumoperitoneum

options.text: Exclude other causes: check ETT position (not endobronchial), exclude tube kinking, check circuit connections, confirm no pneumothorax
options.rationale: High airway pressure must first exclude mechanical causes — endobronchial intubation, kinked tube, or pneumothorax — before treating as bronchospasm; treating the wrong diagnosis is dangerous.

options.text: Deepen anaesthesia with IV propofol bolus 50–100 mg — reduce airway reactivity
options.rationale: Deepening anaesthesia is the most important immediate treatment for light-anaesthesia-triggered bronchospasm; propofol is preferred as volatile deepening is limited by pneumoperitoneum gas leak.

options.text: Administer salbutamol via MDI through the ETT — 4–8 puffs (400–800 mcg) via ETT adaptor
options.rationale: Inhaled salbutamol via MDI adaptor delivers bronchodilator directly to the airways with minimal systemic effects — most effective bronchodilator route in intubated patients.

options.text: Ventilate with prolonged expiratory time — I:E ratio 1:3 to 1:4 to allow full exhalation and prevent air trapping
options.rationale: Bronchospasm causes expiratory flow limitation; prolonged expiratory time prevents dynamic hyperinflation and intrinsic PEEP that worsen cardiovascular compromise.

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg and magnesium sulphate 2 g IV over 20 minutes for refractory bronchospasm
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids and magnesium are second-line bronchodilators for bronchospasm not responding to anaesthesia deepening and inhaled salbutamol.

options.text: If severe refractory bronchospasm: IV adrenaline 10–50 mcg boluses, convert to manual ventilation, consider abandoning the surgical procedure
options.rationale: Refractory bronchospasm with severe hypoxia may require IV adrenaline and surgical abandonment; this is a last resort after all other measures.

management_logic: Intraoperative bronchospasm must first exclude mechanical causes before treatment. The constraint (ongoing pneumoperitoneum limiting volatile deepening) makes IV propofol the preferred agent for anaesthesia deepening. Inhaled salbutamol via ETT adaptor is more effective than IV salbutamol. Prolonged expiratory time is a ventilator adjustment that prevents dynamic hyperinflation — a step trainees often omit.
learning_point: Intraoperative bronchospasm management: first exclude mechanical causes, then deepen anaesthesia, then inhaled bronchodilator, then ventilator adjustment for expiratory flow limitation. Inhaled salbutamol via MDI adaptor is the most effective bronchodilator route in intubated patients.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e; GINA Asthma Management Guidelines; Dewachter P et al. Anaphylaxis and severe bronchospasm under anaesthesia. Anesthesiology 2009

title: Venous Air Embolism — Sitting Craniotomy
topic: Venous Air Embolism - Sitting Craniotomy
source: Advanced Anaesthesia Review 2023, Monitoring section; Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e
demographics: 52M, 74 kg · OT under GA, sitting position for posterior fossa craniotomy
presentation: 60 minutes into sitting craniotomy for cerebellar tumour. Sudden fall in ETCO2 from 35 to 18 mmHg, SpO2 94% and falling, BP 88/52 mmHg. Doppler probe over heart detects classic mill-wheel murmur. Surgeon notes venous bleeding from bone edge. Central venous catheter in situ (right internal jugular).
constraints: Sitting position — cannot easily reposition without neurosurgical input
Ongoing open craniotomy — surgeon must be involved in management decisions

options.text: Notify surgeon immediately — pack and flood the surgical field with saline to prevent further air entrainment
options.rationale: The surgical team must be notified and the source of air entry must be immediately controlled; saline flooding prevents further entrainment while the source is identified and sealed.

options.text: Discontinue nitrous oxide if in use and ventilate with 100% O2
options.rationale: N2O diffuses into air bubbles and dramatically expands their volume, worsening haemodynamic compromise; discontinuation is mandatory in VAE.

options.text: Aspirate central venous catheter — attempt to aspirate air from the right atrium/ventricle via CVC
options.rationale: A properly positioned multi-orifice CVP catheter in the right atrium can aspirate intracardiac air; this is the only direct treatment for intravascular air and should be attempted while other measures are implemented.

options.text: Lower the operative site — tilt table or lower head of bed to reduce the hydrostatic gradient driving air entrainment
options.rationale: Reducing the height of the surgical site relative to the heart reduces the negative pressure that drives air entrainment; must be coordinated with the surgical team.

options.text: Vasopressors — phenylephrine or metaraminol to treat hypotension while source is controlled
options.rationale: Haemodynamic support with vasopressors maintains perfusion pressure while the source of VAE is controlled and air is aspirated; fluid bolus alone will not treat VAE.

options.text: If cardiac arrest from paradoxical embolism: CPR in modified position, left lateral tilt if possible
options.rationale: Massive VAE can cause cardiac arrest from outflow obstruction; CPR may temporarily fragment the air lock; left lateral positioning may shift air away from the outflow tract.

management_logic: VAE management requires simultaneous actions: stop air entrainment (flood field), remove N2O (prevents air expansion), aspirate from CVC (direct treatment), and lower the surgical site (reduces driving pressure). Vasopressors support haemodynamics. The non-obvious step is CVC aspiration — trainees commonly omit this when a CVP catheter is in place. N2O discontinuation is mandatory and often delayed.
learning_point: In venous air embolism, CVP catheter aspiration is the only direct treatment for intravascular air and must be attempted immediately when a central line is in place. N2O must be discontinued immediately — it expands air emboli and worsens haemodynamic compromise.
evidence_basis: Advanced Anaesthesia Review 2023; Mirski et al. Diagnosis and treatment of vascular air embolism. Anesthesiology 2007 (PMID 17122583)

title: Acute Intraoperative ICP Crisis During Craniotomy
topic: Acute Intraoperative ICP Crisis During Craniotomy
source: Advanced Anaesthesia Review 2023, Ch 19 — Increased Intracranial Pressure and Herniation
demographics: 62M, 80 kg · OT under GA, elective craniotomy for GBM resection
presentation: 45 minutes into craniotomy under GA (propofol-remifentanil TIVA). Surgeon reports 'brain is tight' with significant cerebral oedema at dural opening. Intracranial pressure monitor reads 38 mmHg. CPP 52 mmHg. MAP 90 mmHg. ETCO2 38 mmHg. Patient positioned 15° head-up. FiO2 0.35.
constraints: GBM with significant peritumoral oedema — steroids already started 48h pre-op
No mannitol on OT trolley — must be requested from pharmacy

options.text: Optimise head position — confirm head truly elevated 30° with neck in neutral position (no compression of jugular veins)
options.rationale: Head elevation and neutral neck position maximise venous drainage from the cranium; jugular vein compression from head turning is a common overlooked cause of elevated ICP that is immediately correctable.

options.text: Hyperventilate — reduce ETCO2 to 30–33 mmHg to induce cerebral vasoconstriction and reduce CBF
options.rationale: Hyperventilation reduces PaCO2, causes cerebral vasoconstriction, and reduces cerebral blood volume — the fastest available method to reduce ICP; target ETCO2 30–33 mmHg (not lower to avoid ischaemia).

options.text: Request mannitol 1 g/kg IV over 15 minutes from pharmacy immediately — dispatch runner now
options.rationale: Mannitol is the primary osmotic agent for raised ICP; given the pharmacy delay, request must be dispatched immediately in parallel with other interventions — not after hyperventilation has been tried.

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 1.0 — ensure cerebral oxygen delivery is maximised
options.rationale: Maximising PaO2 ensures cerebral oxygen delivery is not limited by inspired oxygen concentration; simple, immediate intervention with no downside in the short term.

options.text: Review anaesthetic depth — ensure propofol TIVA is providing adequate anaesthesia (reduce cerebral metabolic demand)
options.rationale: Adequate depth of anaesthesia with IV agents reduces cerebral metabolic rate and consequently CBF and ICP; volatile agents should be avoided as they may increase ICP.

options.text: Consider furosemide 20 mg IV to augment osmotic gradient established by mannitol once it arrives
options.rationale: Furosemide maintains the osmotic gradient created by mannitol by removing the fluid drawn into the intravascular space; it is adjunctive to mannitol, not a primary treatment.

management_logic: Intraoperative ICP crisis management proceeds: optimise venous drainage first (positioning), then hyperventilate (immediate, no equipment needed), then osmotic therapy (mannitol — but pharmacy delay mandates early dispatch). The sequence of physical and pharmacological interventions is not arbitrary: positioning and hyperventilation are immediate and equipment-free, while waiting for mannitol. Volatile agents should be replaced by TIVA to reduce cerebral vasodilation.
learning_point: In intraoperative ICP crisis, positioning and hyperventilation are the fastest interventions — they require no drug or equipment and work within minutes. Mannitol must be requested early given pharmacy delays, but hyperventilation buys time. Target ETCO2 30–33 mmHg — over-hyperventilation causes cerebral ischaemia.
evidence_basis: Advanced Anaesthesia Review 2023, Ch 19; Patel PM et al. in Miller's Anesthesia 8th Ed; Brain Trauma Foundation Guidelines for ICP Management

title: Epidural Abscess — Late Presentation
topic: Epidural Abscess - Late Presentation
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Epidural Abscess
demographics: 68M, 81 kg · Surgical ward, day 5 post-major colorectal surgery, thoracic epidural removed day 3
presentation: Referred by surgical team. Progressive back pain over 48 hours, initially attributed to surgical pain. Now with fever 38.9°C, severe localised tenderness at T10-11 catheter site, and new bilateral leg weakness. Diabetic, on insulin. Was on epidural catheter for 3 days.
constraints: Diabetes mellitus — increased infection risk and impaired healing

options.text: Urgent MRI spine with contrast immediately — do not delay for blood results or antibiotics before imaging
options.rationale: MRI with contrast is the investigation of choice for epidural abscess; neurological outcome from surgical decompression is time-dependent — imaging must not be delayed for any reason.

options.text: Blood cultures × 2 sets and send CRP, ESR, WCC, blood glucose — drawn simultaneously with MRI arrangements
options.rationale: Blood cultures must be taken before antibiotics to maximise culture yield; the epidural site should also be swabbed; CRP will guide monitoring of treatment response.

options.text: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus — after blood cultures taken
options.rationale: S. aureus is the most common causative organism in epidural abscess; empirical IV antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin + gentamicin or vancomycin if MRSA risk) must be started after cultures but without further delay.

options.text: Urgent neurosurgical consultation — surgical decompression if neurological deficit present or MRI confirms abscess
options.rationale: Surgical decompression is the definitive treatment for epidural abscess with neurological compromise; neurosurgery must be consulted simultaneously with imaging rather than after.

options.text: Strict blood glucose control — insulin infusion if glucose >10 mmol/L
options.rationale: Hyperglycaemia impairs neutrophil function and worsens infection outcomes; tight glucose control is a proven intervention in infectious complications in diabetic patients.

options.text: Escalate to critical care if systemic sepsis develops — SIRS criteria, evolving haemodynamic compromise
options.rationale: Epidural abscess with systemic sepsis may require ICU-level care; this is a safety net escalation that runs in the background of the primary neurological and infectious management.

management_logic: Epidural abscess management is time-critical — neurological outcome correlates directly with time from neurological deficit onset to surgical decompression. Imaging must not be delayed for blood results or antibiotics. Blood cultures are drawn simultaneously with MRI arrangements. Antibiotics start after cultures but without further delay. The diabetes constraint increases both infection risk and the urgency of glycaemic control.
learning_point: In epidural abscess with neurological deficit, MRI must be obtained immediately — it takes priority over everything except blood culture collection. Neurosurgery must be consulted in parallel with, not after, imaging. Do not wait for blood results before imaging.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17; Sendi P et al. Spinal epidural abscess — a systematic review. BMJ 2008 (PMID 18832521)

title: Awareness Under Anaesthesia — Intraoperative Recognition and Post-Event Management
topic: Awareness Under Anaesthesia - Intraoperative Recognition and Post-Event Management
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e; Advanced Anaesthesia Review 2023
demographics: 38F, 62 kg · OT for emergency laparotomy (perforated viscus)
presentation: 75 minutes into emergency laparotomy. Circulating nurse reports the patient's eyes are wet (tears) and notes intermittent movement. ETCO2 is rising. Propofol infusion alarm shows — pump has been running on keep-vein-open rate for 22 minutes following accidental disconnection noticed by scrub nurse. Rocuronium was given 40 minutes ago. No BIS or depth of anaesthesia monitor in use.
constraints: No depth of anaesthesia monitor (BIS) in use
Emergency surgery — cannot stop for risk of haemorrhage

options.text: Immediately restore IV anaesthesia — propofol bolus 100 mg then restart infusion at appropriate rate; check all connections
options.rationale: The patient is almost certainly aware; restoring anaesthetic delivery is the most urgent action — every second of continued awareness causes psychological trauma.

options.text: Administer benzodiazepine — midazolam 2–5 mg IV for anterograde amnesia
options.rationale: Midazolam provides anterograde amnesia for the period of awareness and blunts psychological impact; it must be given as soon as anaesthesia is restored, not delayed.

options.text: Administer opioid analgesia — fentanyl 100 mcg IV; if surgical stimulus explains the haemodynamic response, analgesia may be primarily needed
options.rationale: Awareness during surgery includes pain experience; adequate opioid analgesia addresses both the afferent stimulus and cardiovascular response.

options.text: Add volatile anaesthetic agent — sevoflurane or desflurane as supplemental agent to prevent recurrence
options.rationale: Addition of volatile agent provides a second route of anaesthetic delivery independent of IV pump function; prevents recurrence if propofol delivery fails again.

options.text: Inform the patient as soon as possible post-operatively — explain what happened before they leave the recovery room
options.rationale: Early open disclosure of anaesthetic awareness is ethically mandatory and psychologically beneficial; patients who are not informed often suffer greater PTSD than those who receive early explanation.

options.text: Complete clinical incident report, refer for psychological support, arrange follow-up, document in notes
options.rationale: System-level response and patient psychological support are essential post-awareness but follow the acute management and initial disclosure.

management_logic: Suspected intraoperative awareness requires immediate restoration of anaesthesia and concurrent benzodiazepine for anterograde amnesia. Volatile supplementation prevents recurrence if IV delivery fails again. Post-operatively, early honest disclosure before discharge from recovery is ethically mandatory and psychologically beneficial — patients informed early show less PTSD than those who discover awareness independently.
learning_point: When intraoperative awareness is suspected: restore anaesthesia immediately, give midazolam for anterograde amnesia, and add a second anaesthetic route to prevent recurrence. Post-operatively, disclose early — before the patient leaves recovery — honest and empathic disclosure is the most protective intervention against long-term psychological harm.
evidence_basis: Royal College of Anaesthetists. NAP5 — Fifth National Audit Project on Anaesthetic Awareness 2014; Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e

title: Neuraxial Opioid-Induced Respiratory Depression
topic: Neuraxial Opioid-Induced Respiratory Depression
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Respiratory Depression Associated with Neuraxial Procedures
demographics: 58M, 84 kg · Orthopaedic ward, 18 hours after total knee replacement under spinal anaesthesia
presentation: Ward nurse calls: patient unresponsive, snoring. Spinal anaesthesia with hyperbaric bupivacaine and intrathecal morphine 300 mcg administered 18 hours ago. Pain well controlled (NRS 2/10). Patient is sedated but arousable to sternal rub. RR 6/min with long pauses. SpO2 84% on room air.

options.text: Apply high-flow O2 via facemask (15 L/min) and stimulate — call patient's name, sternal rub
options.rationale: Immediate oxygenation support and stimulation are first-line; hypoxia is the immediate threat and stimulation may be sufficient to restore adequate ventilation in mild cases.

options.text: Naloxone 0.1–0.2 mg IV bolus, repeat every 2–3 minutes to restore respiratory rate — titrate to effect, not to full reversal
options.rationale: Naloxone is the specific reversal agent for opioid-induced respiratory depression; titrated dosing avoids acute reversal causing severe pain and sympathetic surge in a post-operative patient.

options.text: Prepare naloxone infusion (2 mg in 500 mL normal saline at 60 mL/hour) — intrathecal morphine's duration exceeds naloxone's half-life (60–90 min)
options.rationale: Intrathecal morphine can cause respiratory depression up to 18–24 hours post-injection (hydrophilic opioid — delayed rostral spread); naloxone's half-life is 60–90 min, far shorter than morphine's — a naloxone infusion is essential to prevent re-narcotisation after bolus reversal.

options.text: Call for anaesthetic support and transfer to high-dependency unit for monitoring
options.rationale: Neuraxial opioid respiratory depression requires anaesthetic team involvement and HDU-level monitoring for a minimum of 12–24 hours given the duration of intrathecal morphine effect.

options.text: Bag-mask ventilation if RR <4/min or SpO2 remains <90% despite O2 and naloxone bolus
options.rationale: If opioid reversal does not rapidly restore adequate ventilation, manual bag-mask ventilation bridges to definitive management — this is the safety net for non-responders.

options.text: Review and document the intrathecal morphine dose, timing, and monitoring protocol — root cause analysis
options.rationale: A 300 mcg intrathecal morphine dose in an 84 kg patient 18 hours post-operatively without adequate respiratory monitoring represents a system issue requiring review.

management_logic: Delayed respiratory depression from intrathecal hydrophilic opioids (morphine) can occur up to 24 hours post-injection due to rostral spread in CSF. The critical non-obvious step is the naloxone infusion — naloxone's 60–90 minute half-life is shorter than the duration of intrathecal morphine effect, making re-narcotisation after a single bolus a serious risk. Trainees give a bolus, patient improves, and they leave — patient re-narcoticises within 90 minutes.
learning_point: After naloxone bolus reversal of intrathecal opioid-induced respiratory depression, a continuous naloxone infusion is mandatory. Naloxone's half-life (60–90 min) is shorter than intrathecal morphine's duration (18–24 h) — re-narcotisation after a single bolus is common and potentially fatal.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17; Ko S et al. Intrathecal morphine respiratory depression — Anesth Analg 2003 (PMID 12564954)

title: Anaphylaxis During Rapid Sequence Induction — Multiple Drug Exposure
topic: Anaphylaxis During Rapid Sequence Induction - Multiple Drug Exposure
source: Advanced Anaesthesia Review 2023, Ch 22 — Regional Anaesthesia Complications; AAGBI Anaphylaxis Guidelines
demographics: 47F, 74 kg · OT for emergency bowel obstruction, RSI with propofol + suxamethonium + cefuroxime
presentation: 90 seconds after RSI induction (propofol 140 mg, suxamethonium 100 mg, cefuroxime 1.5g given simultaneously). Attempted intubation successful. Immediate cardiovascular collapse: BP unrecordable, HR 148/min. Generalised flushing and erythema. Bronchospasm: SpO2 falling, very high airway pressures on ventilator. Multiple drugs given simultaneously — causative agent unknown.
constraints: Multiple causative agents possible — propofol, suxamethonium, cefuroxime all given simultaneously
Emergency surgery — bowel obstruction, cannot cancel case
Full stomach — aspiration risk managed by intubation but constrains positioning
Mast cell tryptase must be timed correctly for diagnostic value

options.text: Declare anaphylaxis, call for help, stop all potentially causative agents — do not give further cefuroxime
options.rationale: Anaphylaxis management begins with stopping all causative agents; in multi-drug exposure, all suspected agents are withheld pending allergy investigation.

options.text: Adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.1–0.5 mg IV titrated — first-line vasopressor and bronchodilator in anaphylaxis
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is the only drug that addresses all components of anaphylaxis simultaneously: vasopressor, bronchodilator, and mast cell stabiliser — it must be given immediately.

options.text: 100% O2 and treat bronchospasm — manual ventilation with high flow O2, salbutamol via ETT
options.rationale: Anaphylactic bronchospasm contributes to hypoxia alongside cardiovascular collapse; simultaneous treatment of both is required.

options.text: Rapid IV crystalloid bolus 1–2 L for volume resuscitation
options.rationale: Anaphylactic shock has a large distributive component from capillary leak; aggressive fluid resuscitation supports vasopressor effect.

options.text: Take mast cell tryptase sample at 1 hour post-reaction and arrange repeat at 24 hours — time-critical for diagnostic confirmation
options.rationale: Mast cell tryptase peaks at 1 hour post-reaction and falls within 6 hours; correct timing is essential for diagnostic confirmation and allergy investigation — trainees often fail to take timed samples.

options.text: Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV and hydrocortisone 200 mg IV — secondary prevention of biphasic reaction
options.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts that prevent biphasic reaction and treat skin manifestations but have no role in acute haemodynamic rescue.

management_logic: Multi-drug anaphylaxis at RSI is a complex scenario where the causative agent cannot be identified acutely — all suspected agents are withheld and allergy referral is arranged post-operatively. The management hierarchy follows: stop agent, adrenaline, oxygenation, fluids. Mast cell tryptase timing is the key non-obvious step — trainees fail to take timed samples, losing the only objective diagnostic confirmation of anaphylaxis.
learning_point: In anaphylaxis, mast cell tryptase samples must be taken at 1 hour post-reaction (peak) and 24 hours (baseline). Failure to take timed samples means the diagnosis cannot be confirmed biochemically and allergy testing may be inconclusive. Tryptase sampling is a time-critical step, not a post-stabilisation afterthought.
evidence_basis: AAGBI Suspected Anaphylactic Reactions Associated with Anaesthesia Guidelines 2009; Harper et al. RCUK Anaphylaxis Algorithm 2021

title: Inadvertent Intrathecal Catheter — Management After Detection
topic: Inadvertent Intrathecal Catheter - Management After Detection
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Inadvertent Intrathecal Catheter
presentation: Epidural catheter placed at L3-4 by trainee anaesthetist. Aspiration of the catheter yields clear fluid — uncertain whether it is saline flush or CSF. Glucose test strip on the fluid is positive (glucose >10 mmol/L, consistent with CSF). Test dose of 3 mL 2% lidocaine + adrenaline given. Patient develops immediate dense motor block of both legs within 3 minutes.
constraints: Pregnant — fetal monitoring ongoing, risk of haemodynamic compromise

options.text: Do NOT give any further drug through this catheter — declare inadvertent intrathecal placement to entire team
options.rationale: Any epidural dose given through an intrathecal catheter will cause total spinal; immediate cessation of all drug administration and team notification are mandatory.

options.text: Monitor for haemodynamic compromise and apply left lateral tilt — a 3 mL 2% lidocaine spinal dose will produce a significant sensory and motor block
options.rationale: 3 mL 2% lidocaine intrathecally will produce dense bilateral block to mid-thoracic levels; hypotension and fetal compromise are imminent — left tilt prevents aortocaval compression.

options.text: IV vasopressor — phenylephrine 100 mcg bolus if BP falls >20% from baseline; titrate to maintain uterine perfusion
options.rationale: Spinal-induced hypotension reduces uterine perfusion and causes fetal distress; phenylephrine is preferred in obstetrics as it maintains uteroplacental flow better than ephedrine.

options.text: Remove the intrathecal catheter after haemodynamic stability confirmed — resit epidural at a different level with fresh sterile equipment
options.rationale: The intrathecal catheter must be removed before further epidural insertion; the new catheter must be sited at a different level and tested with a formal test dose.

options.text: Inform the patient of the inadvertent intrathecal placement — explain the denser-than-expected block
options.rationale: Informed consent principles and open disclosure require that the patient understands what occurred; this supports trust and allows the patient to report any new symptoms accurately.

options.text: Document the event, incident report, consultant notification, and arrange formal review with the patient post-delivery
options.rationale: System-level reporting and post-event review are essential for learning and patient follow-up; they follow acute management.

management_logic: An inadvertent intrathecal catheter discovered before a large dose has been given requires immediate cessation, haemodynamic monitoring, and management of the spinal block from the test dose. In pregnancy, left lateral tilt and phenylephrine prevent haemodynamic compromise. The catheter is removed only after stability is confirmed — removing it immediately risks an uncontrolled situation if further intervention is needed.
learning_point: An intrathecal catheter is identified by positive glucose on aspirated fluid and by unexpectedly dense, rapid block after test dose. No further drug must be given through this catheter. Left lateral tilt and phenylephrine prevent the obstetric consequences of the spinal block from the test dose.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17; Royal College of Anaesthetists Patient Safety Guidelines

title: Transtracheal Jet Ventilation — Rescue in CICV Before eFONA
topic: Transtracheal Jet Ventilation - Rescue in CICV Before eFONA
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Invasive Airway Access; DAS Guidelines 2015
demographics: 54M, 91 kg · OT for emergency debridement of neck abscess
presentation: CICV scenario developing. Neck abscess has caused significant swelling and trismus — mouth opening 2 cm only. Direct laryngoscopy failed. Video laryngoscope failed (blood and secretions from abscess). SGA insertion failed (unable to achieve seal). SpO2 now 72% and falling. eFONA being prepared but kit not yet assembled. Jet ventilator available and assembled. Cricothyroid membrane palpable.
constraints: Neck abscess — normal anatomical landmarks potentially distorted
Jet ventilator assembled and available — eFONA kit being prepared

options.text: Confirm cricothyroid membrane location with finger palpation — if palpable, proceed to emergency transtracheal access
options.rationale: With SpO2 72% and falling, any delay is fatal; confirming CTM location is the first step regardless of which rescue technique is used.

options.text: Perform needle cricothyroidotomy — 14G IV cannula through CTM at 90° then directed 30–45° caudally, aspirate air to confirm tracheal placement
options.rationale: Needle cricothyroidotomy with jet ventilation is faster to perform than scalpel eFONA when the jet ventilator is already assembled; in this scenario, jet ventilation is available NOW while eFONA kit is being assembled.

options.text: Connect needle to jet ventilator — drive pressure 1 bar initially, reduce if pneumothorax risk is high; allow full passive expiration between jets
options.rationale: Jet ventilation must allow full passive expiration between insufflations to prevent barotrauma and air trapping; high-pressure oxygen delivery will provide critical oxygenation within seconds.

options.text: Complete eFONA kit assembly while jet ventilation oxygenates the patient — scalpel, 10-blade, bougie, 6.0 cuffed ETT
options.rationale: Jet ventilation through needle buys time to properly assemble and perform scalpel eFONA, which provides a definitive airway — it is a bridge, not a definitive solution.

options.text: Perform scalpel-finger-bougie eFONA to convert to definitive cuffed airway once oxygenation is restored
options.rationale: Jet ventilation is a temporising measure; eFONA with a cuffed ETT is the definitive airway — it should be performed after oxygenation has been restored and kit is properly assembled.

options.text: Summon ENT surgeon for emergency surgical tracheostomy if eFONA fails or is anatomically impossible
options.rationale: Surgical tracheostomy is the definitive fallback if all other techniques fail; ENT must be called early while other rescue attempts are underway.

management_logic: When eFONA kit is not yet assembled but a jet ventilator is available, needle cricothyroidotomy with jet ventilation is the fastest route to oxygenation — it buys time for eFONA assembly. Jet ventilation is a bridge, not a definitive airway; eFONA to a cuffed tube remains the goal. Full passive expiration between jet insufflations is mandatory to prevent barotrauma. The non-obvious decision is choosing jet ventilation NOW over waiting 60 additional seconds for eFONA kit assembly during critical hypoxia.
learning_point: In CICV with critical hypoxia, any available airway rescue should be used immediately without waiting for a preferred technique. If a jet ventilator is assembled and the CTM is palpable, needle cricothyroidotomy with jet ventilation provides immediate oxygenation while eFONA is being prepared.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 16 — Transtracheal Jet Ventilation; DAS CICV Guidelines 2015 (Frerk et al., BJA PMID 26556848)

title: Bupivacaine Cardiotoxicity — Prevention and Management
topic: Bupivacaine Cardiotoxicity - Prevention and Management
source: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Local Anaesthetic Pharmacology; Advanced Anaesthesia Review Ch 22
demographics: 61M, 86 kg · Pre-op area, shoulder surgery, interscalene block planned
presentation: Pre-operative block planning. Surgeon requests a long-acting interscalene block for shoulder arthroplasty. Suggests using bupivacaine 0.5% 30 mL for maximum duration. Anaesthetist is selecting between bupivacaine 0.5%, levobupivacaine 0.5%, and ropivacaine 0.5% for the block. Patient has known left bundle branch block on ECG.
constraints: Known left bundle branch block — increased vulnerability to local anaesthetic cardiac toxicity

options.text: Select ropivacaine 0.5% or levobupivacaine 0.5% — NOT racemic bupivacaine 0.5% — for this cardiac-vulnerable patient
options.rationale: Racemic bupivacaine is more cardiotoxic than levobupivacaine or ropivacaine at equipotent concentrations; in a patient with LBBB, the added cardiac conduction system vulnerability makes bupivacaine selection inappropriate.

options.text: Perform block under ultrasound guidance with real-time needle visualisation
options.rationale: Ultrasound-guided nerve block reduces the volume of local anaesthetic required and reduces the risk of intravascular injection — both directly relevant to LAST risk reduction in a cardiac-vulnerable patient.

options.text: Inject in 5 mL increments with aspiration before each aliquot — not as a single bolus
options.rationale: Fractionated injection with aspiration between each aliquot detects intravascular placement before a toxic dose is administered; single bolus injection of 30 mL will cause LAST if intravascular.

options.text: Confirm Intralipid 20% is immediately available in the block area before starting
options.rationale: Intralipid must be available at the site of block performance for all high-volume peripheral nerve blocks; checking availability before starting is a mandatory safety step.

options.text: 12-lead ECG monitoring throughout block performance and 30 minutes post-block
options.rationale: ECG monitoring detects cardiac toxicity early (PR prolongation, widened QRS, arrhythmia); mandatory in a patient with pre-existing conduction disease receiving a high-volume block.

options.text: Brief patient on LAST symptoms — tingling around mouth, metallic taste, visual disturbance — and ask them to report immediately
options.rationale: Early patient-reported LAST symptoms (CNS prodrome) precede cardiovascular toxicity by seconds to minutes; patient education may allow detection before cardiac arrest.

management_logic: The selection of local anaesthetic agent is the most important decision in this case. Racemic bupivacaine carries higher cardiotoxicity than levobupivacaine or ropivacaine at equivalent doses — this is a pharmacological fact that directly changes the management in a patient with LBBB. All subsequent steps (ultrasound, fractionation, Intralipid availability, monitoring) reduce the risk of LAST regardless of agent chosen.
learning_point: Bupivacaine is more cardiotoxic than levobupivacaine and ropivacaine at clinically used concentrations. In patients with pre-existing cardiac conduction disease, levobupivacaine or ropivacaine must be selected for high-volume peripheral nerve blocks. This is an agent-selection decision, not purely a technique decision.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia Review 4e, Ch 17 — Local Anaesthetic Pharmacology; Knudsen K et al. Levobupivacaine vs bupivacaine cardiovascular toxicity, Acta Anaesthesiol Scand 1997 (PMID 9069558)

title: Cardiac Arrest in the Prone Position
topic: Cardiac Arrest in the Prone Position
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Adult Advanced Life Support
demographics: 52M, 84 kg · OT under GA, prone for posterior spinal fusion L3–S1
presentation: 60 minutes into prone spinal surgery. ECG shows sudden VF. Pulseless. SpO2 probe losing signal. Airway secured with ETT, ventilated. Surgical site open, surgeon scrubbed at the field. Two operating theatre nurses present. Anaesthetic registrar alone with the patient.
constraints: Prone position — cannot immediately be turned supine

options.text: Call cardiac arrest team and all available help immediately — declare arrest
options.rationale: A single anaesthetic registrar cannot manage cardiac arrest alone; team activation is the first mandatory step regardless of position.

options.text: Begin CPR in prone position — compress between the scapulae at T7 level with the patient on their chest; same rate and depth as standard CPR
