options.rationale: Oxford Handbook 5th Ed: 'Chest compressions can be started by compressing between the scapulae for patients in the prone position' — do not wait to flip the patient before starting CPR.

options.text: Apply defibrillator pads in anteroposterior position (one pad on sternum area of back, one on left lateral chest) and charge for defibrillation
options.rationale: AP pad placement allows defibrillation in prone position; charge the defibrillator in parallel with compressions — do not interrupt CPR to place pads first.

options.text: Coordinate team to turn patient supine as quickly and safely as possible for standard ALS
options.rationale: Prone CPR is a temporising measure; the patient must be repositioned supine for definitive ALS once sufficient team members are present to do so safely.

options.text: Follow standard ALS algorithm once supine — defibrillate VF, adrenaline 1mg IV every 3–5 min, amiodarone 300mg after 3rd shock
options.rationale: Standard Resuscitation Council UK ALS algorithm proceeds once the patient is supine and the shockable rhythm is confirmed on pads.

options.text: Treat reversible causes — identify and address 4Hs and 4Ts; consider anaesthetic-specific causes including gas embolism, anaphylaxis, LA toxicity
options.rationale: Reversible cause identification runs throughout resuscitation; anaesthetic-specific causes must be on the differential in any intraoperative arrest.

management_logic: The key non-obvious step is starting prone CPR immediately by compressing between the scapulae — not waiting to turn the patient supine. Prone CPR is haemodynamically effective and must begin at arrest, not after repositioning. Team assembly is needed to safely turn the patient; a single anaesthetist cannot manage both CPR and repositioning simultaneously.
learning_point: In prone cardiac arrest, begin CPR immediately by compressing between the scapulae. Do not wait to turn the patient supine before starting compressions — prone CPR is haemodynamically effective and buys time for team assembly.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — ALS Modifications During Anaesthesia; Resuscitation Council UK

title: Post-Resuscitation Care After Intraoperative Cardiac Arrest
topic: Post-Resuscitation Care After Intraoperative Cardiac Arrest
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Post-Resuscitation Care
demographics: 64M, 78 kg · PACU/OT after ROSC from 12-minute intraoperative VF arrest during laparotomy
presentation: ROSC achieved after 12 minutes CPR. Cause: haemorrhagic shock with concurrent myocardial ischaemia. Remains comatose (GCS 3T). Intubated and ventilated. Haemorrhage controlled surgically. SpO2 99% on 100% O2. MAP 88 mmHg on noradrenaline infusion. Blood glucose 16 mmol/L. ETCO2 28 mmHg (mild hyperventilation).

options.text: Reduce FiO2 to target SpO2 94–98% — titrate inspired O2 down from 100%
options.rationale: Post-ROSC hyperoxia causes secondary cerebral injury from oxygen free radicals; Oxford Handbook: 'titrate inspired O2 to achieve saturation 94–98%' — 100% O2 must not be continued after ROSC.

options.text: Correct ventilation — increase ETCO2 to 35–40 mmHg by reducing minute ventilation
options.rationale: ETCO2 28 mmHg indicates hyperventilation causing hypocarbia; cerebral vasoconstriction from low PaCO2 compounds post-arrest cerebral ischaemia — normocarbia is mandatory post-ROSC.

options.text: Start insulin infusion to maintain blood glucose 4–10 mmol/L — treat glucose of 16 mmol/L
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'treat blood glucose with insulin if it exceeds 10mmol/L; maintain in range 4–10mmol/L' — post-arrest hyperglycaemia independently worsens neurological outcome.

options.text: Initiate targeted temperature management — target core temperature 32–36°C for minimum 24 hours in comatose post-arrest patient
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'treat patients remaining comatose after ROSC with targeted temperature management, maintain at 32–36°C for at least 24h' — TTM reduces neurological injury.

options.text: 12-lead ECG and early echocardiography — identify ST elevation, myocardial dysfunction, underlying structural cause
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'ST-elevation on ECG is indication for urgent coronary angiography and PCI'; early echo detects underlying cause and quantifies dysfunction.

options.text: Transfer to cardiac ICU with continuous monitoring — plan prognostication assessment no earlier than 72–96h after ROSC
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'prognostication in unconscious patients is unreliable for at least 72–96h after ROSC' — premature withdrawal is dangerous; ICU admission for at least 3–7 days.

management_logic: Two immediate constraints both worsen neurological outcome and must be corrected in parallel: hyperoxia (FiO2 100%) and hypocarbia (ETCO2 28 mmHg). Both are iatrogenic and both cause cerebral injury. Hyperglycaemia and hypothermia are the next priorities. Trainees commonly leave 100% O2 running post-ROSC and over-ventilate — both are harmful.
learning_point: Post-ROSC hyperoxia and hypocarbia are iatrogenic causes of secondary cerebral injury that must be corrected within minutes of ROSC. Target SpO2 94–98% and ETCO2 35–40 mmHg. Prognostication cannot be performed reliably until 72–96 hours post-ROSC.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Post-Resuscitation Care; ERC/ESICM Post-Resuscitation Care Guidelines 2021 (PMID 33773834)

title: Intraoperative Unstable Tachycardia — Broad Complex
topic: Intraoperative Unstable Tachycardia - Broad Complex
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Tachycardia
demographics: 71M, 82 kg · OT under GA, elective TURP, anaesthesia 55 minutes in
presentation: Sudden broad complex tachycardia — HR 178/min, irregular. BP 74/42 mmHg and falling. Patient anaesthetised, ETT in situ, SpO2 93%. ECG shows broad QRS >0.12s, irregular rhythm. Underlying AF likely. No pulse. Surgeon currently irrigating the prostate.
constraints: Ongoing TURP — risk of TURP syndrome, potential hyponatraemia
Patient on digoxin for AF — at baseline

options.text: Call for help — declare emergency, ask surgeon to stop irrigation and assess fluid absorption
options.rationale: Broad complex tachycardia in TURP with haemodynamic instability may be partly from TURP syndrome (hyponatraemia); stopping irrigation prevents further absorption while management proceeds.

options.text: Check pulse and consciousness — if pulseless, follow ALS algorithm; if pulse present, proceed to DC cardioversion
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'For all tachycardias, assess for life-threatening adverse features: syncope, shock, myocardial ischaemia and heart failure. If present, perform synchronised DC cardioversion' — pulseless goes to ALS.

options.text: Synchronised DC cardioversion 120–150J biphasic for unstable broad complex tachycardia — patient already anaesthetised
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'use 120–150J for broad complex tachycardia and AF' — patient is already anaesthetised, no sedation required; synchronised mode must be selected to avoid VF induction.

options.text: If cardioversion fails: amiodarone 300mg IV over 10–20 minutes, then reattempt cardioversion
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'If direct current cardioversion fails, give 300mg IV amiodarone over 10–20min, then reattempt DC cardioversion' — amiodarone preconditions before repeat shock.

options.text: Do NOT give verapamil — contraindicated with digoxin and in haemodynamically unstable broad complex tachycardia
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'Do not use verapamil and a beta-blocker together'; verapamil in haemodynamically unstable broad complex tachycardia is dangerous and is contraindicated with digoxin.

options.text: Check serum sodium and TURP fluid absorption — treat hyponatraemia if TURP syndrome confirmed
options.rationale: TURP syndrome (hypo-osmolar hyponatraemia from glycine absorption) can cause cardiac arrhythmias; correction with hypertonic saline if sodium <120 mmol/L is required once haemodynamics are stabilised.

management_logic: Unstable broad complex tachycardia in an anaesthetised patient requires immediate synchronised DC cardioversion — the patient does not require additional sedation. The TURP context adds a second diagnosis (TURP syndrome) that must be addressed in parallel. Verapamil is contraindicated in unstable broad complex tachycardia and specifically in combination with digoxin. 'Synchronised' mode is the non-obvious selection — trainees sometimes deliver an unsynchronised shock in AF causing VF.
learning_point: In unstable tachycardia in an anaesthetised patient, synchronised DC cardioversion is the immediate treatment — no additional sedation is needed. Select synchronised mode carefully; an unsynchronised shock delivered during the vulnerable period can induce VF.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Tachycardia; Resuscitation Council UK Tachycardia Algorithm

title: Severe Intraoperative Bradycardia — Beta-Blocker Overdose
topic: Severe Intraoperative Bradycardia - Beta-Blocker Overdose
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Bradycardia
demographics: 58M, 76 kg · A&E resus, intubated after propranolol overdose (suicide attempt), taken to OT for nasogastric lavage
presentation: Under GA. HR 28/min, BP 58/32 mmHg. Broad QRS on ECG. Sinus bradycardia with first-degree block. Atropine 3 mg total already given — no response. Isoprenaline not available. Temporary pacing not yet set up.
constraints: Refractory to atropine — maximum dose already given

options.text: Glucagon 3–10 mg IV bolus — specific antidote for beta-blocker-induced bradycardia and hypotension
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'Beta-blocker overdose: give glucagon 3–10mg IV, then 3–5mg/h IVI' — glucagon bypasses the beta-adrenoceptor and is the only effective treatment after atropine failure in beta-blocker toxicity.

options.text: High-dose insulin euglycaemic therapy — insulin 1 unit/kg IV bolus, then 0.5 units/kg/h infusion with glucose supplementation
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'Consider high-dose insulin 1 unit/kg bolus, followed by 0.5 units/kg/h' — insulin-glucose therapy improves myocardial metabolism in beta-blocker toxicity and is the second-line after glucagon.

options.text: Adrenaline infusion 2–10 mcg/min IV to provide chronotropic and inotropic support while specific treatments take effect
options.rationale: High-dose catecholamines can partially overcome beta-receptor blockade; adrenaline infusion provides bridge support while glucagon and insulin take effect.

options.text: Arrange urgent transcutaneous pacing — apply pads, set rate 70/min, increase current until electrical capture confirmed on ECG with associated pulse
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'If transcutaneous pacing, check for electrical capture on ECG and that there is an associated pulse; patient may need analgesia/sedation' — external pacing provides guaranteed rate while pharmacological reversal proceeds.

options.text: Urgent cardiology consultation for transvenous temporary pacing if external pacing does not achieve capture
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'Discuss early with cardiology regarding temporary transvenous pacing' — transvenous pacing provides reliable ventricular capture when external pacing fails or causes discomfort.

options.text: Notify critical care and toxicology — lipid emulsion (Intralipid) 1.5 mL/kg if refractory; ECMO as rescue if all else fails
options.rationale: Intralipid has been used for lipid-soluble beta-blocker toxicity (propranolol is highly lipid-soluble); ECMO maintains perfusion in refractory cardiovascular collapse from toxin overdose.

management_logic: Beta-blocker overdose refractory to atropine requires glucagon as the specific antidote — it is the most important pharmacological step and must come before transcutaneous pacing. Glucagon bypasses the blocked beta-receptor directly. High-dose insulin euglycaemic therapy is second-line. External pacing provides mechanical rate support while pharmacological reversal proceeds.
learning_point: Glucagon is the specific antidote for beta-blocker-induced refractory bradycardia. It bypasses the beta-adrenoceptor and is the first-line after atropine failure. High-dose insulin euglycaemic therapy is second-line. External pacing provides mechanical rate support but does not treat the underlying toxicity.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Bradycardia Specific Antidotes; Engebretsen KM et al., beta-blocker toxicity, Clin Toxicol 2011 (PMID 21275637)

title: Intraoperative Aspiration — Management and Sequelae
topic: Intraoperative Aspiration - Management and Sequelae
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Aspiration
demographics: 72F, 68 kg · OT for emergency bowel obstruction, RSI planned
presentation: LMA inserted instead of ETT in error at induction (trainee case). Vomiting occurred immediately after induction — gastric contents visible in circuit and LMA. SpO2 falling from 98% to 84%. High airway pressures. Wheeze audible. Patient not yet intubated.
constraints: LMA used instead of ETT — airway not secured

options.text: Immediately apply cricoid pressure if not already applied and secure the airway with RSI and ETT — remove the LMA
options.rationale: An LMA does not protect against aspiration; the airway must be secured with an ETT immediately. Oxford Handbook: 'consider securing the airway with ETT' — this is the most urgent intervention.

options.text: Turn patient into the recovery position if ETT cannot be immediately placed — head down, left lateral, suction
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'if light anaesthesia, suction and put into recovery position' — if immediate intubation is not possible, positioning limits further aspiration and facilitates suction.

options.text: 100% O2 and aggressive suctioning of the pharynx and trachea via ETT once secured
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'administer 100% O2 and minimise risk of further aspirate contaminating airway' — once ETT placed, suction clears material from trachea.

options.text: Pass large-bore nasogastric tube and empty the stomach before any further airway manipulation or extubation attempt
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'empty the stomach with a large-bore NGT prior to extubation' — emptying the stomach reduces further aspiration risk from the large volume obstruction.

options.text: Arrange CXR and ABG post-intubation — assess extent of pulmonary injury and need for ongoing respiratory support
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'monitor respiratory function and arrange CXR; look for evidence of oedema, collapse or consolidation' — baseline imaging guides ongoing management.

options.text: Do NOT give prophylactic antibiotics or corticosteroids routinely — neither alter outcome
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook explicitly states: 'prophylactic antibiotics are not generally given routinely' and 'corticosteroids may modify inflammatory response early but do not alter the outcome except by potentially interfering with normal immune response.'

management_logic: Aspiration through an LMA requires immediate ETT placement — LMA provides no aspiration protection. Recovery position with suction is the bridge if intubation is temporarily not possible. The two common clinical errors are: (1) administering prophylactic antibiotics, which Oxford Handbook explicitly states do not alter outcome; and (2) giving corticosteroids, which also do not alter outcome and may harm immunity.
learning_point: Prophylactic antibiotics and corticosteroids are NOT indicated after aspiration and do not alter outcome per Oxford Handbook 5th Ed. Empty the stomach with a large-bore NGT before extubation. The LMA does not protect against aspiration and must be replaced with an ETT.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Aspiration; Cook T et al., NAP4 Audit — Aspiration, RCoA 2011

title: Severe Intraoperative Hypotension — Differential Diagnosis
topic: Severe Intraoperative Hypotension - Differential Diagnosis
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension in Theatre
demographics: 66M, 80 kg · OT for elective laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy, 90 minutes into procedure
presentation: Sudden drop in BP from 118/72 to 64/38 mmHg. Simultaneous fall in ETCO2 from 34 to 18 mmHg. SpO2 94%. HR 118/min. No obvious surgical bleeding. Pneumoperitoneum at 15 mmHg. Patient on lisinopril 10 mg/day preoperatively (dose not withheld).
constraints: Lisinopril not withheld pre-op — ACE inhibitor vasodilatation

options.text: Simultaneously check ETCO2 and pulse — fall in both ETCO2 and BP together = gas embolism or massive PE until proven otherwise
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'Suspect embolic event if sudden decrease in ETCO2, decrease in SpO2, loss of palpable pulse' — the ETCO2 drop is the diagnostic key that distinguishes embolism from haemorrhagic or drug-related hypotension.

options.text: Ask surgeon to deflate pneumoperitoneum immediately and check for diaphragm breach — CO2 capnothorax or gas embolism from intraabdominal pressurisation
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'Raised intrathoracic pressure can be caused by a capnothorax (during laparoscopic surgery where the diaphragm is breached)' — pneumoperitoneum is the most likely cause of the ETCO2 fall and hypotension in this context.

options.text: Give 100% O2 and reduce volatile agent — check depth of anaesthesia is not excessive
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook lists 'excessive depth of anaesthesia' as an anaesthetic cause of severe hypotension in theatre; volatile reduction is simple and immediate.

options.text: Fluid challenge 10 mL/kg crystalloid and vasopressor — metaraminol 1–2 mg IV or phenylephrine 0.25–0.5 mg IV
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: fluid challenge + vasoconstrictors are standard first-line haemodynamic support; ACE inhibitor pre-operatively potentiates induction hypotension and vasodilatory response.

options.text: Check for tension pneumothorax — needle decompression at 2nd ICS MCL if trachea deviated and hyperresonant
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: if tension pneumothorax suspected — 'treat immediately by decompressing the pleural cavity with a large-bore cannula placed in the 2nd ICS MCL' — rare but rapidly fatal if missed.

options.text: 12-lead ECG and point-of-care echocardiography to assess RV function and exclude PE or RWMA
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'ECG, ABGs, echocardiography, troponin, CXR' are investigations for severe intraoperative hypotension; ECHO distinguishes ventricular failure from distributive causes.

management_logic: The simultaneous fall in ETCO2 and BP is the diagnostic key — it distinguishes embolism from haemorrhagic or drug-related hypotension. Oxford Handbook specifically links 'sudden decrease in ETCO2, decrease in SpO2, loss of palpable pulse' to suspect embolic event. In a laparoscopic case, the first response is to ask the surgeon to deflate the pneumoperitoneum. ACE inhibitor pre-operatively potentiates hypotension.
learning_point: A simultaneous fall in ETCO2 and BP is the haemodynamic signature of a gas embolism or PE — not of haemorrhage or vasodilation. In laparoscopic surgery, capnothorax from diaphragmatic breach is the most common cause. Deflating the pneumoperitoneum is the first diagnostic and therapeutic step.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension in Theatre

title: Severe Intraoperative Bronchospasm — Drug Doses and Sequencing
topic: Severe Intraoperative Bronchospasm - Drug Doses and Sequencing
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Bronchospasm (Table 39.1)
demographics: 38M, 74 kg · OT under GA for inguinal hernia repair, known asthma
presentation: 30 minutes into GA (sevoflurane, LMA, spontaneous ventilation). Progressive difficulty ventilating: airway pressures rising, plateau on capnograph, audible wheeze. SpO2 falling from 99% to 91%. ETT or circuit obstruction excluded. Bilateral wheeze confirmed on auscultation.

options.text: Exclude mechanical causes first — pass suction catheter through ETT/LMA, check circuit connections and ETT position
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'severe bronchospasm is a diagnosis of exclusion; quickest method is to connect a self-inflating bag directly to the ETT and manually ventilate' — kinked tube, endobronchial intubation, and circuit obstruction must be excluded before treating as bronchospasm.

options.text: Increase sevoflurane concentration — sevoflurane is the least irritant volatile and is the first-line bronchodilator in the anaesthetised patient
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'increase the volatile agent concentration; sevoflurane is the least irritant and is less likely to precipitate dysrhythmias in the presence of hypercapnia' — deepening anaesthesia is the first specific treatment.

options.text: Salbutamol 250 mcg IV slow bolus OR salbutamol 10 puffs via MDI adaptor on ETT/LMA
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook Table 39.1: 'salbutamol IV 250 mcg (adult); salbutamol nebulised 2.5–5 mg; salbutamol puffs 10 puffs' — beta-2 agonist is first-line pharmacological bronchodilator.

options.text: Adjust ventilator to I:E ratio 1:3 or 1:4 with low respiratory rate — allow full expiration to prevent gas trapping
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'ventilator settings in bronchospasm: low RR and prolonged expiration (I:E ratio 1:3 or 1:4); accept increased ETCO2 provided SpO2 adequate (permissive hypercapnia)' — gas trapping is life-threatening.

options.text: Aminophylline 250 mg IV slowly if salbutamol insufficient
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook Table 39.1: 'aminophylline 250 mg slowly IV (adult)' — methylxanthine bronchodilator for cases not responding to beta-2 agonist; caution: side effects include tachyarrhythmia and seizures.

options.text: Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV and magnesium 2 g IV for severe/refractory bronchospasm
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook Table 39.1: 'hydrocortisone 200 mg IV; magnesium 2 g IV slowly' — corticosteroids reduce airway inflammation; magnesium is a smooth muscle relaxant. Ketamine 2 mg/kg is an additional option for refractory cases.

management_logic: Bronchospasm management proceeds: exclude mechanical causes, deepen volatile anaesthesia, then pharmacological bronchodilation in sequence. The non-obvious step is the ventilator adjustment — I:E 1:3 to 1:4 with permissive hypercapnia. Oxford Handbook specifically warns that failing to prolong expiratory time causes dynamic hyperinflation with obstructed venous return, reduced CO, and cardiovascular collapse.
learning_point: In mechanically ventilated bronchospasm, changing the ventilator I:E ratio to 1:3 or 1:4 is as important as pharmacological treatment. Failure to allow adequate expiratory time causes gas trapping, dynamic hyperinflation, and obstructed venous return — a rapidly fatal complication that mimics cardiac tamponade on the arterial trace.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39, Table 39.1 — Drug Doses in Bronchospasm; BTS/SIGN British Guideline on Asthma

title: Intraoperative Pulmonary Oedema
topic: Intraoperative Pulmonary Oedema
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Pulmonary Oedema
demographics: 74M, 88 kg · OT for TURP under spinal anaesthesia, 90 minutes in, spinal wearing off
presentation: Progressive fall in SpO2 from 99% to 88%. High airway pressures developing. Pink frothy sputum visible in circuit. CVP rising. HR 118/min. BP 168/108 mmHg. Bilateral crackles on auscultation. Pre-existing ischaemic cardiomyopathy (EF 32%).

options.text: Sit the patient up immediately — 15° head-up or reverse Trendelenburg to offload pulmonary vasculature and improve FRC
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'if anaesthetised and intubated: 15° head-up position to reduce atelectasis and improve FRC' — positioning reduces hydrostatic pressure in the pulmonary circulation and is immediate, equipment-free.

options.text: 100% O2 with PEEP 5–10 cmH2O — secure airway with ETT if not already intubated
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'commence IPPV with PEEP 5–10 cmH2O' — positive pressure ventilation with PEEP is the cornerstone of respiratory support in pulmonary oedema.

options.text: Furosemide 50 mg IV — reduce preload and promote diuresis
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'furosemide 50mg IV' — loop diuretic reduces pulmonary hydrostatic pressure by decreasing venous return; appropriate in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.

options.text: GTN 10 mg transdermal patch or IV GTN infusion (only if arterial line in situ) — reduce afterload given elevated BP
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'vasodilator if hypertensive (e.g. GTN 0.5–1.5 mg sublingually or 10 mg transcutaneous patch; IV GTN only if arterial line in situ)' — BP 168/108 mmHg makes afterload reduction appropriate.

options.text: 12-lead ECG and urgent echocardiography — exclude acute coronary event as precipitant
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'ECG: evidence of right heart strain; evidence of MI' — a new MI must be excluded as the precipitant in a patient with known IHD; immediate PCI may be required.

options.text: Arrange cardiac ICU admission — low EF patient with acute pulmonary oedema requires invasive haemodynamic monitoring and possible inotropic support
options.rationale: EF 32% indicates severely impaired contractile reserve; acute oedema on this background requires ICU-level management with inotropes if CO is inadequate after diuresis and afterload reduction.

management_logic: Intraoperative pulmonary oedema in a low-EF patient requires simultaneous positioning, ventilatory support, and pharmacological management. Head-up position and PEEP are immediate; furosemide and GTN follow. Oxford Handbook specifically cautions that IV GTN requires an arterial line — sublingual or transdermal GTN is the safe alternative without invasive monitoring.
learning_point: IV GTN for pulmonary oedema requires an arterial line already in situ — without beat-to-beat BP monitoring, rapid IV GTN titration is unsafe. Use transdermal or sublingual GTN if no arterial line is present.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Pulmonary Oedema

title: Severe Anaphylaxis — NMBA Trigger with Correct Tryptase Sampling
topic: Severe Anaphylaxis - NMBA Trigger with Correct Tryptase Sampling
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Anaphylaxis and Anaphylaxis Follow-Up
demographics: 44F, 66 kg · OT for laparoscopic appendicectomy under GA
presentation: 4 minutes after rocuronium 50 mg at induction. Sudden cardiovascular collapse: BP 52/28 mmHg. HR 152/min. Urticaria across chest and neck. Bilateral wheeze. SpO2 87%. No prior NMBA exposure documented. Propofol, fentanyl, and ondansetron also given at induction.
constraints: Multiple drugs given at induction — causative agent uncertain

options.text: Declare anaphylaxis and call for help — stop all IV infusions, identify and remove all potential causative agents including IV lines with chlorhexidine coating
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'stop any potential triggers, particularly IV agents; be aware of chlorhexidine-coated central lines' — chlorhexidine is a top-10 trigger and line coating is an overlooked source.

options.text: Adrenaline IV 20–50 mcg boluses every 1–2 minutes — titrate to response
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook Table 39.2: 'adrenaline IV adult >12y: 20–50 micrograms' — IV route preferred in anaesthetised patient; repeated small boluses rather than a large dose reduces risk of arrhythmia.

options.text: Lay flat with legs elevated — 100% O2, IV crystalloid 20 mL/kg rapidly
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'lay the patient flat with legs elevated/Trendelenburg; give IV crystalloid fluids 20 mL/kg' — distributive shock requires simultaneous volume expansion and vasopressor.

options.text: Take first mast cell tryptase sample NOW — immediately after resuscitation begins
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'take three venous blood samples — immediately after resuscitation, at 1–2h (not later than 6h), and baseline at 24h' — the immediate sample is time-critical and is often the step trainees forget under pressure.

options.text: Chlorphenamine 10–20 mg IV and hydrocortisone 200 mg IV after haemodynamic rescue
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'chlorphenamine 10–20mg; hydrocortisone 100–300mg' — antihistamines and corticosteroids are secondary treatments that prevent biphasic reaction but do not reverse anaphylactic shock.

options.text: Arrange allergy clinic referral with anaesthetic chart, drug chart, timings, and tryptase results — take second tryptase at 1–2h and third at 24h
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'referral to allergy clinic is the responsibility of the anaesthetist and should include: anaesthetic chart, drug chart, timings, tryptase results and timings' — systematic follow-up identifies the causative agent.

management_logic: The tryptase sampling protocol is the non-obvious time-critical step — three samples are required: immediately post-resuscitation, at 1–2h, and at 24h baseline. Oxford Handbook specifies the first sample must be taken 'immediately after resuscitation' — trainees take it only after the patient is stable, missing the peak. Chlorhexidine-coated lines are an overlooked trigger. NMBAs account for 33% of anaesthetic anaphylaxis triggers.
learning_point: Three tryptase samples are required for anaphylaxis investigation: immediately after resuscitation (not after stability), at 1–2 hours (not later than 6 hours), and at 24 hours for baseline. Missing the immediate sample eliminates the ability to biochemically confirm the diagnosis.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39, Table 39.2 and Anaphylaxis Follow-up; RCoA NAP6 — Perioperative Anaphylaxis 2018

title: Malignant Hyperthermia — Masseter Spasm After Suxamethonium
topic: Malignant Hyperthermia - Masseter Spasm After Suxamethonium
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Malignant Hyperthermia
demographics: 16M, 62 kg · OT for appendicectomy under GA with RSI (propofol + suxamethonium)
presentation: After suxamethonium 100 mg: sustained jaw rigidity (masseter spasm) preventing intubation. Patient ventilated by facemask. ETCO2 rising from 35 to 46 mmHg (2 minutes). Temperature 37.2°C. HR 88/min. SpO2 98% on 100% O2. No family history of MH elicited preoperatively.
constraints: Isolated masseter spasm — surgery not yet started, decision point for proceeding

options.text: Treat isolated masseter spasm after suxamethonium as MH until proven otherwise — declare MH alert and get dantrolene to the room
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'sustained jaw rigidity after suxamethonium (masseter spasm)' is listed as the first diagnostic criterion for MH — isolated masseter spasm may be an early and only sign; dantrolene must be immediately available.

options.text: Stop all volatile agents immediately — switch to TIVA with propofol; remove vaporisers from circuit
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'turn off volatile agent, stop giving suxamethonium' — if MH is suspected, the trigger must be removed immediately even before a formal diagnosis is made.

options.text: Monitor closely: continuous ETCO2, core temperature, ECG — watch for rising ETCO2, temperature, dysrhythmias
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'unexplained ETCO2 increase plus unexplained HR increase accompanied or followed by increasing core temperature' confirms MH — intensive monitoring determines whether the full crisis develops.

options.text: Do NOT proceed with further volatile anaesthesia — convert to MH-safe technique (propofol TIVA) if surgery must continue
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'MH patients should not be denied surgery solely because of MH; an MH-safe technique (avoid suxamethonium and volatile agents) such as propofol TIVA or regional anaesthesia is appropriate' — emergency appendicectomy can proceed safely.

options.text: If ETCO2 or temperature continue to rise: give dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV (based on actual body weight — max 300 mg per dose)
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'give dantrolene 2.5mg/kg; select dose based on actual body weight to maximum 300mg per dose; repeat every 10min until PaCO2 <6 kPa and decreasing body temperature; no cumulative ceiling dose' — a 62 kg patient requires 155 mg = approximately 8 ampoules of Dantrium 20mg.

options.text: Refer patient to MH investigation unit post-operatively for DNA testing and muscle biopsy (caffeine-halothane contracture test)
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'follow-up involves DNA testing for ryanodine receptor RYR1 mutations; if DNA negative, a muscle biopsy will be offered' — family members must also be referred for testing.

management_logic: Isolated masseter spasm after suxamethonium must be treated as early MH. Oxford Handbook lists it as the first diagnostic criterion. The non-obvious step is calculating the correct dantrolene dose from actual body weight with no ceiling dose — trainees underestimate the ampoule count. A 62 kg patient requires 155 mg = 7.75 ampoules of Dantrium 20mg; pragmatically 8 ampoules for the initial dose.
learning_point: Isolated masseter spasm after suxamethonium is MH until proven otherwise. Dantrolene dose is 2.5 mg/kg based on actual body weight with no cumulative ceiling — mortality increases with inadequate dosing. Calculate the ampoule count before the crisis: 1 ampoule Dantrium 20mg per 8 kg body weight per dose.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Malignant Hyperthermia; Hopkins PM et al. Malignant Hyperthermia 2020 Guideline, Association of Anaesthetists (PMID 33320947)

title: Planning Anaesthesia for Known MH-Susceptible Patient
topic: Planning Anaesthesia for Known MH-Susceptible Patient
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — MH-Susceptible Patient Anaesthesia
demographics: 34F, 58 kg · Pre-op for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy, known MH-susceptible (positive caffeine-halothane contracture test)
presentation: Pre-operative planning appointment. DNA positive for RYR1 mutation. Previous uneventful GA at age 12 (no volatile agents used). Surgical team requesting standard volatile GA. Machine is standard Draeger Fabius with sevoflurane vaporiser in situ. No activated charcoal filters available. Dantrolene is in the pharmacy 10 minutes away.
constraints: No activated charcoal filters available — standard machine preparation required
Dantrolene in pharmacy — not on OT trolley

options.text: Plan TIVA with propofol and remifentanil — avoid all volatile agents and suxamethonium entirely
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'an MH-safe technique (avoid suxamethonium and volatile agents), such as propofol TIVA or regional anaesthesia, is appropriate; all other drugs are MH-safe' — TIVA is the safest technique in a confirmed susceptible patient.

options.text: Machine preparation: remove vaporisers, flush circuit for 70+ minutes at high FGF OR use activated charcoal filters (2 minutes preparation if available)
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'anaesthetic machine must be purged and flushed to remove volatile traces; in modern machines this can take at least 70 minutes; activated charcoal filters reduce preparation time to 2 minutes' — machine preparation is mandatory before any MH-susceptible patient.

options.text: Ensure dantrolene is physically on the OT trolley before starting — not in pharmacy
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'dantrolene is not required prophylactically but should be available' — physically present in the room, not a 10-minute journey away. Dantrolene is only effective if administered rapidly.

options.text: Do NOT give dantrolene prophylactically — causes prolonged duration of NMBAs and is unpleasant for the patient
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook explicitly states: 'dantrolene is not required prophylactically; it is unpleasant for the patient and prolongs the action of NDMRs' — this is a clear contra-indication to prophylactic use despite MH susceptibility.

options.text: Standard monitoring with core temperature from induction — establish baseline before any potential trigger exposure
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'a baseline core temperature should be established before the procedure and monitored for 4h postoperatively' — temperature monitoring allows early MH detection.

options.text: Postoperative monitoring for 4 hours minimum in PACU — MH can occur up to 4h after exposure in susceptible patients
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'standard monitoring; baseline core temperature before procedure and monitored for 4h postoperatively' — delayed MH in recovery is a known complication of inadequate machine preparation.

management_logic: MH-susceptible patient management has two non-obvious steps: machine preparation takes ≥70 minutes without activated charcoal filters (critical pre-operative planning), and dantrolene must NOT be given prophylactically. Trainees sometimes pre-treat with dantrolene which Oxford Handbook explicitly states is wrong.
learning_point: Machine preparation for MH-susceptible patients takes at least 70 minutes with high-flow flushing in modern machines — this must be planned into the list scheduling. Dantrolene prophylaxis is explicitly contraindicated: it is unpleasant for the patient and prolongs neuromuscular blockade.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Anaesthesia for Known/Suspected MH-Susceptible Patients

title: LAST — Seizure After Brachial Plexus Block, No Cardiac Arrest
topic: LAST - Seizure After Brachial Plexus Block, No Cardiac Arrest
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 40 — Local Anaesthetics and Adjuncts; AAGBI LAST Guidelines
demographics: 48M, 80 kg · Pre-op area, axillary brachial plexus block for wrist surgery
presentation: 30 mL ropivacaine 0.5% injected under ultrasound guidance. 2 minutes after injection: perioral tingling, metallic taste reported by patient. 90 seconds later: generalised tonic-clonic seizure. Patient seizing but pulse present — HR 108/min, BP 148/92 mmHg. No cardiac arrhythmia on monitor.
constraints: Seizure without cardiac arrest — CNS toxicity only

options.text: Call for help and manage airway — jaw thrust, 100% O2 via bag-mask, suction
options.rationale: Airway compromise during seizure is the primary immediate threat — hypoxia worsens CNS toxicity and may precipitate secondary cardiac arrest from LAST.

options.text: Terminate seizure — midazolam 5 mg IV or propofol 1–1.5 mg/kg IV; do NOT use thiopentone in LAST
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines or propofol terminate LAST-induced seizure; thiopentone reduces the threshold for cardiovascular LAST toxicity and is avoided; propofol contains lipid but should not be counted as Intralipid therapy.

options.text: Prepare Intralipid 20% 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus — administer if seizure does not terminate rapidly or if cardiovascular signs develop
options.rationale: Intralipid lipid sink sequestrates free ropivacaine; while cardiac arrest has not occurred, Intralipid should be prepared immediately and administered if seizure persists or haemodynamics deteriorate.

options.text: Continuous ECG monitoring — LAST CNS seizure can evolve to cardiac toxicity within seconds
options.rationale: LAST progression from CNS to cardiovascular toxicity can be rapid; continuous ECG monitoring detects PR prolongation, QRS widening, or VT/VF early enough to intervene.

options.text: Intubate and ventilate if seizure continues beyond 60–90 seconds or if airway cannot be maintained
options.rationale: Prolonged seizure causes progressive hypoxia and acidosis that accelerate LAST cardiovascular toxicity; intubation secures the airway and enables controlled oxygenation.

options.text: Review maximum dose and technique — ropivacaine 30 mL of 0.5% = 150 mg; maximum dose ropivacaine 3 mg/kg in 80 kg = 240 mg; dose was within limit but review for intravascular injection
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook Table 40.2: 'ropivacaine maximum dose 3 mg/kg' — 150 mg in 80 kg patient is within limit; probable cause is intravascular injection despite aspiration, not dose exceedance.

management_logic: LAST CNS toxicity without cardiac arrest is managed with airway support and seizure termination. Intralipid must be prepared immediately and given if seizure continues or cardiovascular toxicity develops — the threshold for Intralipid use is lower than for cardiac arrest. Ropivacaine dose calculation from Oxford Handbook confirms the dose was within limits, pointing to intravascular injection as the mechanism.
learning_point: LAST CNS toxicity (seizure with intact circulation) can progress to cardiovascular toxicity within seconds — Intralipid must be prepared immediately and administered before cardiac arrest occurs if the seizure does not terminate rapidly. Do not wait for cardiac arrest to give Intralipid.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 40, Table 40.2; AAGBI LAST Guidelines 2010; Neal JM et al. ASRA LAST Checklist

title: Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome — Hip Replacement
topic: Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome - Hip Replacement
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension; Ch 23 Orthopaedic
demographics: 78F, 64 kg · OT under spinal anaesthesia for cemented total hip replacement
presentation: At the moment of prosthesis cementing: sudden BP fall from 126/74 to 58/32 mmHg. Simultaneous fall in ETCO2 from 30 to 14 mmHg. SpO2 92%. HR 128/min. Rhythm: sinus tachycardia. Known COPD. Pre-existing AF, on warfarin (INR 2.4, bridged pre-op). Cardiovascular collapse occurs at the exact moment the surgeon presses the femoral prosthesis into the cemented canal.

options.text: Declare BCIS emergency — alert surgeon to stop impacting, call for help
options.rationale: Bone cement implantation syndrome is caused by intravasation of cement monomer and fat/marrow emboli during femoral prosthesis insertion — alerting the surgeon immediately is the first step.

options.text: 100% O2 and intubate immediately — respiratory support is critical in COPD patient with BCIS
options.rationale: BCIS causes V/Q mismatch from pulmonary microembolism; COPD limits respiratory reserve; securing the airway and controlling ventilation prevents respiratory failure compounding haemodynamic collapse.

options.text: Turn off N2O if in use — N2O expands intravascular gas and fat emboli
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'N2O will expand any intravascular gas volume' — gas embolism complicates BCIS and N2O discontinuation is mandatory; same principle as VAE management.

options.text: Vasopressors and fluid resuscitation — phenylephrine 0.25–0.5 mg IV bolus or metaraminol 1–2 mg IV; rapid crystalloid
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'metaraminol 1–2 mg IV; phenylephrine 0.25–0.5 mg IV' for severe hypotension — haemodynamic support maintains coronary and cerebral perfusion while embolic material clears.

options.text: Adrenaline 10–50 mcg IV boluses if vasopressors insufficient — BCIS can cause profound RV failure requiring inotropic support
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'adrenaline 10 mcg IV' for refractory intraoperative hypotension — BCIS causing RV failure requires inotropic support beyond pure vasopressors.

options.text: Arrange ICU admission post-operatively — BCIS carries significant mortality in elderly patients with cardiorespiratory comorbidity
options.rationale: Severe BCIS in a 78-year-old with COPD and AF has significant 24-hour mortality; ICU-level monitoring is required for respiratory and cardiovascular support.

management_logic: BCIS presents with the classic triad of hypoxia, hypotension, and LOC at the moment of cementation. The fall in ETCO2 alongside BP indicates embolism of fat/marrow/monomer. N2O discontinuation is the non-obvious but important step — it prevents expansion of intravascular gas. The anticoagulation constraint prevents fibrinolysis but does not change the management hierarchy.
learning_point: Bone cement implantation syndrome presents at the exact moment of femoral prosthesis insertion — not before and not after. The ETCO2 fall is the diagnostic key. N2O must be discontinued immediately as it expands intravascular gas and fat emboli.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension; Donaldson AJ et al. BCIS — a review, BJA 2009 (PMID 19395342)

title: Negative Pressure Pulmonary Oedema — Post-Extubation
topic: Negative Pressure Pulmonary Oedema - Post-Extubation
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Pulmonary Oedema; Ch 16
demographics: 32M, 86 kg · PACU, 15 minutes after tracheal extubation following tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy
presentation: Patient bit the ETT on emergence, causing brief complete upper airway obstruction before ETT was removed. Now extubated. Progressive dyspnoea, SpO2 falling from 98% to 86% over 5 minutes. Pink frothy sputum. Bilateral crackles on auscultation. No cardiac history. HR 118/min, BP 148/88 mmHg.
constraints: Post-tonsillectomy — bleeding risk limits NGT placement and repositioning

options.text: High-flow O2 via non-rebreather mask and sit the patient upright
options.rationale: Immediate O2 supplementation and upright position offload the pulmonary circulation — the same first steps as cardiogenic pulmonary oedema regardless of the mechanism.

options.text: CPAP via tight-fitting mask 5–10 cmH2O — reduces alveolar oedema and improves FRC
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: '100% O2 via mask/HFNO/CPAP 5–10mmHg' for spontaneously breathing patient with pulmonary oedema — CPAP is the most effective non-invasive intervention for negative pressure pulmonary oedema.

options.text: IV furosemide 20–40 mg — reduce pulmonary venous pressure and promote diuresis
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'furosemide 50mg IV' for pulmonary oedema — diuresis reduces pulmonary hydrostatic pressure; dose can be reduced given a young patient without prior fluid overload.

options.text: Call for senior anaesthetic support and prepare for reintubation if not improving within 5–10 minutes
options.rationale: Negative pressure pulmonary oedema can deteriorate rapidly to frank respiratory failure requiring reintubation; preparation must occur in parallel with non-invasive management.

options.text: 12-lead ECG to exclude primary cardiac cause — negative pressure POED is diagnosis of exclusion
options.rationale: Young patient with no cardiac history makes primary cardiac cause unlikely, but a 12-lead ECG excludes MI and arrhythmia as alternative causes of acute pulmonary oedema.

options.text: Document cause and notify surgical team — negative pressure POED from biting ETT on emergence; preventable with bite block
options.rationale: Documentation of mechanism and preventable cause — a bite block should be placed before any patient is allowed to emerge from GA to prevent this complication.

management_logic: Negative pressure pulmonary oedema occurs when forceful inspiratory effort against a closed glottis (biting the ETT) generates extreme negative intrathoracic pressure, causing fluid transudation into alveoli from the pulmonary capillaries. Management is identical to cardiogenic POED: oxygen, upright positioning, CPAP, and diuresis. The non-obvious step is the mechanism recognition — without it, the ECG chase delays treatment.
learning_point: Negative pressure pulmonary oedema occurs after any acute upper airway obstruction and responds to CPAP + diuretics. Prevention: always place a bite block before emergence. If pink frothy sputum occurs within 20 minutes of extubation after any airway struggle, this is the diagnosis.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Pulmonary Oedema; Udeshi A et al. NPPOE — a review, Can J Anaesth 2010 (PMID 20428980)

title: High-Risk Obstetric Anaesthesia — Peripartum Cardiomyopathy
topic: High-Risk Obstetric Anaesthesia - Peripartum Cardiomyopathy
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 35 — Obstetric Anaesthesia; Ch 5 — Cardiovascular Disease
demographics: 29F, 72 kg, 38 weeks gestation · Labour ward — elective caesarean section for breech presentation. Newly diagnosed peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM), EF 28% on echo done this week.
presentation: Planning caesarean section. Recent echo: dilated cardiomyopathy, EF 28%, moderate MR. On furosemide and enalapril commenced 5 days ago. SpO2 94% at rest. Orthopnoea — sleeping with 4 pillows. Mildly elevated JVP. HR 96/min. BP 88/62 mmHg. ACE inhibitor was withheld today (day of surgery). Neonatology team has been notified.
constraints: BP 88/62 mmHg — already hypotensive; spinal anaesthesia risks catastrophic further drop
Left-to-right shunt through mitral regurgitation — afterload reduction critical

options.text: Multidisciplinary team briefing before anaesthesia: obstetric, anaesthetic, cardiac, neonatal teams — agree on plan for haemodynamic deterioration
options.rationale: PPCM with EF 28% for caesarean section is one of the highest-risk obstetric anaesthesia scenarios; a pre-brief with all teams including a clear escalation plan to ECMO/cardiac ICU is mandatory before any drug is given.

options.text: Avoid spinal anaesthesia — consider slow incremental epidural or general anaesthesia with invasive monitoring
options.rationale: Single-shot spinal in a patient with BP 88/62 mmHg and EF 28% will cause catastrophic cardiovascular collapse from sympathectomy; epidural with slow titration preserves haemodynamic control; GA gives full control with vasopressors.

options.text: Insert arterial line before induction — beat-to-beat BP monitoring is mandatory in EF 28%
options.rationale: Any anaesthesia with EF 28% and baseline hypotension requires arterial line before induction; NIBP will not detect the speed of haemodynamic change in a vulnerable ventricle.

options.text: Vasopressor of choice: noradrenaline infusion titrated to MAP ≥65 mmHg — phenylephrine raises afterload and is relatively contraindicated in severe MR
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook Ch 5: in severe MR, elevated afterload worsens regurgitant fraction; noradrenaline provides vasopressor support without excessive afterload increase compared to phenylephrine — the non-obvious choice in obstetric anaesthesia where phenylephrine is usually first-line.

options.text: Intraoperative TOE or focused echo to guide fluid management and vasopressor titration — avoid fluid overload
options.rationale: EF 28% with dilated ventricle means even modest fluid overload causes decompensation; real-time echo guides preload management.

options.text: Cardiac ICU admission postpartum — PPCM risk of further deterioration in first 24–48 hours after delivery; EF may worsen post-partum
options.rationale: PPCM may paradoxically worsen in the postpartum period despite delivery — haemodynamic monitoring and inotropic support must be available; some patients require LVAD or heart transplant listing.

management_logic: PPCM with EF 28% for caesarean is a scenario where standard obstetric anaesthesia protocols (spinal, phenylephrine) are both dangerous. Single-shot spinal is contraindicated given baseline hypotension. Phenylephrine — the standard obstetric vasopressor — is relatively contraindicated in severe MR as increased afterload worsens regurgitant fraction. Noradrenaline is the preferred vasopressor. Multi-team briefing before any intervention is non-negotiable.
learning_point: In severe MR, phenylephrine increases afterload and worsens the regurgitant fraction — noradrenaline is the preferred vasopressor. Single-shot spinal is contraindicated in severe systolic dysfunction with baseline hypotension. These two departures from standard obstetric anaesthesia practice are the key learning points.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 35 Obstetric; Ch 5 Cardiovascular Disease; Dennis AT, Cardiac disease in pregnancy, Anaesthesia 2018 (PMID 29119590)

title: Paediatric Laryngospasm — Complete Obstruction, No IV Access
topic: Paediatric Laryngospasm - Complete Obstruction, No IV Access
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 36 — Paediatric; Ch 39 — Laryngospasm
demographics: 4M, 16 kg · Day surgery PACU, 10 minutes post-adenotonsillectomy, just woken
presentation: Complete laryngospasm on emergence. No air movement despite respiratory effort. Paradoxical chest movement. SpO2 100% → 88% → 72% and falling rapidly over 90 seconds. IV cannula tissued on emergence — no IV access. PACU nurse present, paediatric anaesthetist arriving in 60 seconds.
constraints: Child — suxamethonium dose route must be adjusted

options.text: CPAP via tight facemask with 100% O2 and jaw thrust immediately — positive pressure at the glottis is first-line
options.rationale: CPAP is first-line for laryngospasm regardless of age — positive pressure at the glottis helps break adducted cords and maintains oxygenation; start while preparing drug rescue.

options.text: Larson manoeuvre — firm bilateral pressure in the notch between mastoid and posterior ramus of mandible; scaled down pressure for a 4-year-old
options.rationale: Larson manoeuvre is effective in paediatric laryngospasm and should be applied simultaneously with CPAP; the pressure required is less than in adults — the landmark is the same.

options.text: Suxamethonium 4 mg/kg IM (64 mg) into the lateral thigh — IV access not available; IM route is effective in paediatric laryngospasm
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'salbutamol IM 4 mg/kg' — specifically, for suxamethonium without IV access: standard IM dose is 4 mg/kg in paediatrics; onset is 3–4 minutes but is the only route available; draw up immediately.

options.text: Establish IO access if SpO2 <60% and IM suxamethonium not yet taking effect
options.rationale: Intraosseous access is faster to establish than IV in an emergency paediatric situation and provides immediate reliable drug delivery — used as IO bridge while the IM suxamethonium takes effect or if rapid deterioration occurs.

options.text: Reintubate if SpO2 remains <60% after suxamethonium — have appropriately sized paediatric ETT ready (size 4.5 uncuffed or 4.0 cuffed for a 4-year-old)
options.rationale: If suxamethonium breaks the spasm, intubation secures the airway for controlled ventilation and recovery; have the correct sized tube prepared before giving the drug.

options.text: Post-event review and parent communication — note the laryngospasm episode and ensure follow-up if recurrent
options.rationale: Documenting the event, communicating with parents, and noting in medical record is important for future anaesthetic management — laryngospasm risk is increased in children with recent URTI.

management_logic: Paediatric laryngospasm without IV access is a high-pressure scenario. IM suxamethonium 4 mg/kg is the definitive treatment when IV access is not available — it takes 3–4 minutes for full effect, making early CPAP and Larson manoeuvre critical bridges. IO access is faster to establish than IV in paediatric emergencies and can be used if IM onset is too slow for the rate of desaturation.
learning_point: IM suxamethonium 4 mg/kg is the paediatric laryngospasm rescue when IV access is not available. It takes 3–4 minutes to work — CPAP and the Larson manoeuvre are the bridges. IO access is faster to establish than IV in a paediatric emergency.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 36 Paediatric; Ch 39 Laryngospasm; Hampson-Evans D et al. Paediatric laryngospasm, Paed Anaesth 2008 (PMID 18494620)

title: Perioperative Major Haemorrhage — Massive Transfusion Protocol
topic: Perioperative Major Haemorrhage - Massive Transfusion Protocol
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 17 — Blood Products and Fluid Therapy; Ch 37 Major Trauma
demographics: 38F, 62 kg · OT for emergency laparotomy — ruptured ectopic pregnancy, haemorrhage estimation 2.5 L in 20 minutes
presentation: Emergency laparotomy for ruptured ectopic pregnancy. Ongoing haemorrhage visible in abdomen — 2.5 L estimated blood loss. BP 54/28 mmHg despite 1.5 L crystalloid already in. HR 148/min. SpO2 91%. Pale, cold peripheries. Estimated gestation: 10 weeks.
constraints: Active haemorrhage — source not yet controlled
Jehovah's Witness — documented refusal of blood products

options.text: Activate major haemorrhage protocol — call haematology, blood bank, and request O-negative packed red cells immediately while grouping sample is processed
options.rationale: Major haemorrhage protocol activation provides simultaneous blood bank preparation, haematology support, and pre-emptive product release — this must happen before haemostatic resuscitation begins.

options.text: Respect the Jehovah's Witness decision — do NOT transfuse blood products; focus on crystalloid, cell salvage, tranexamic acid, and surgical haemostasis
options.rationale: A competent adult's advance refusal of blood transfusion is legally binding even in life-threatening haemorrhage; blood must not be given without consent. Cell salvage and tranexamic acid are alternatives.

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 minutes immediately — within the first 3 hours of haemorrhage onset
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid reduces mortality in haemorrhage when given within 3 hours — this is the most important pharmacological intervention available that respects the patient's religious belief.

options.text: Cell salvage — request intraoperative cell salvage device to collect and reinfuse shed autologous blood (acceptable to most Jehovah's Witnesses if circuit remains continuous)
options.rationale: Cell salvage with a continuous circuit is acceptable to most (not all) Jehovah's Witnesses — discussion with the patient pre-operatively about this specific technique determines acceptability.

options.text: Active warming — fluid warmer, forced air warming blanket, warm OT environment — hypothermia worsens coagulopathy
options.rationale: Temperature 35.4°C promotes the lethal triad of trauma: hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy; active warming is a mandatory component of damage control resuscitation.

options.text: Damage control surgery — surgeon to achieve surgical haemostasis and pack abdomen; definitive repair deferred; ICU for resuscitation and rewarming
options.rationale: Ongoing surgical haemorrhage cannot be managed by transfusion alone — surgical source control is the definitive treatment; damage control laparotomy with ICU transfer is appropriate.

management_logic: The Jehovah's Witness constraint is the most important driver in this case — it overrides the standard massive haemorrhage protocol. A documented, competent adult refusal of blood products is legally binding in the UK. Tranexamic acid, cell salvage (if acceptable to the patient), and surgical haemostasis are the cornerstones. Cell salvage acceptability must be clarified pre-operatively as it varies between individuals.
learning_point: A Jehovah's Witness's advance refusal of blood transfusion is legally binding even in life-threatening haemorrhage — blood must never be given without consent. Tranexamic acid and cell salvage are the pharmacological and technical alternatives. Cell salvage acceptability varies between individual Jehovah's Witnesses and must be confirmed pre-operatively.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 17 — Blood Products; CRASH-2 Trial (tranexamic acid, PMID 20554319); UK Law: Re T (Adult: Refusal of Treatment) 1992

title: Intraoperative Air Embolism — Gas Embolism During Laparoscopy
topic: Intraoperative Air Embolism - Gas Embolism During Laparoscopy
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Air/Gas Embolism
demographics: 48F, 68 kg · OT under GA for laparoscopic cholecystectomy with N2O in use
presentation: 45 minutes into laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Sudden fall in ETCO2 from 32 to 9 mmHg. BP 62/34 mmHg. HR 132/min, then irregular. SpO2 89%. CVP 22 mmHg (rising). Millwheel murmur audible on precordial Doppler. Surgeon reports unusual resistance to insufflation.
constraints: N2O in use — will expand intravascular gas
CVP line not in situ — no central access for aspiration

options.text: Ask surgeon to stop insufflation immediately and deflate the pneumoperitoneum
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'stop insufflating gas; ensure open wound lower than heart; flood wound and compress drainage veins' — removing the source of gas entrainment is the first intervention.

options.text: Discontinue N2O immediately — switch to 100% O2
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'turn off N2O; N2O will expand any intravascular gas volume' — N2O is the most important intervention after source control; it directly worsens embolism and must be stopped immediately.

options.text: Place patient in left lateral head-down (Durant) position — keep gas bubble in right atrium away from right ventricular outflow
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'tip patient head-down in left lateral position to keep the bubble of gas in the right atrium or apex of RV; it can then be aspirated via central line or will eventually dissolve' — this is classic VAE positioning.

options.text: Insert central venous catheter urgently for gas aspiration from right atrium
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: aspiration via central line advanced into right atrium 'is likely to be difficult in practice' but should be attempted; central access is required even though aspiration success is limited.

options.text: CPAP 5–10 cmH2O to increase intrathoracic pressure and CVP — limits further gas entrainment
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'moderate CPAP advocated as means of rapidly increasing intrathoracic pressure and CVP' — however, be aware that 10% of patients have a patent foramen ovale; sustained right atrial pressure rise can cause paradoxical cerebral embolism.

options.text: Consider hyperbaric oxygen therapy if available — hyperbaric pressure reduces gas emboli volume
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'consider hyperbaric therapy if available; increased ambient pressure (3–6 bar) will decrease volume of gas emboli' — rarely immediately available but should be arranged if embolism is severe.

management_logic: Gas embolism management requires simultaneous actions: stop gas source, discontinue N2O (expands gas), position left lateral head-down (traps gas away from outflow). N2O discontinuation is the most important and most commonly delayed step — trainees focus on positioning while N2O continues. Oxford Handbook specifies 10% of patients have PFO — CPAP risks paradoxical cerebral embolism in these patients.
learning_point: N2O must be discontinued immediately in any gas embolism — it expands intravascular gas volume. This step takes precedence over positioning. Oxford Handbook cautions that CPAP risks paradoxical cerebral embolism through a PFO (present in 10% of the population) and must be used cautiously.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Air/Gas Embolism; Mirski MA et al. Diagnosis and treatment of vascular air embolism, Anesthesiology 2007 (PMID 17122583)

title: The Catastrophic Cannot-Ventilate Post-Induction — Bilateral Tension Pneumothorax
topic: The Catastrophic Cannot-Ventilate Post-Induction - Bilateral Tension Pneumothorax
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension; Ch 15 — Airway
demographics: 22M, 78 kg · OT under GA for emergency appendicectomy. Intubated, ventilating well initially. Pre-existing known severe asthma.
presentation: 10 minutes after intubation and commencing IPPV: sudden inability to ventilate — peak pressures >60 cmH2O, no chest rise bilaterally. SpO2 98% → 68% over 90 seconds. BP 128/82 → 52/18 mmHg. HR 148/min. No breath sounds bilaterally. Trachea displaced to the left. ETCO2 trace flat.
constraints: Bilateral tension pneumothorax — both sides affected simultaneously
IPPV context — positive pressure dramatically accelerates tension accumulation

options.text: Immediate bilateral needle decompression — 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line on BOTH sides without waiting for CXR
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: 'treat immediately by decompressing the pleural cavity with a large-bore cannula placed in the 2nd ICS MCL' — bilateral tension pneumothorax in IPPV context is immediately fatal; do not wait for CXR or imaging; bilateral decompression without delay.

options.text: Disconnect from ventilator immediately — allow passive exhalation; manual bag ventilation at lower pressure
options.rationale: Continued IPPV against bilateral tension pneumothorax dramatically worsens the situation — disconnecting allows decompression of alveolar pressure and reduces rate of tension accumulation during needle decompression.

options.text: Call for help and thoracic surgery/emergency thoracostomy — formal chest drain insertion required bilaterally after needle decompression
options.rationale: Needle decompression is a temporising measure — bilateral chest drains are required for definitive treatment. Senior surgeon or emergency thoracic involvement must be activated in parallel.

options.text: 100% O2 and manual ventilation at reduced pressures — aim tidal volume 6 mL/kg IBW with prolonged expiratory time
options.rationale: Once tension is decompressed, ventilate with lung-protective settings; this patient's asthma means bronchospasm contributes to the air-trapping; low tidal volume and long expiratory time prevent re-tension.

options.text: Vasopressor support — phenylephrine or noradrenaline to treat haemodynamic collapse from obstructed venous return
options.rationale: Bilateral tension pneumothorax causes complete obstruction of venous return; vasopressors support MAP while decompression restores haemodynamics — do not delay decompression for vasopressors.

options.text: Insert bilateral intercostal chest drains (28–32 Fr) — definitive treatment after needle decompression
options.rationale: Needle decompression is temporary; formal chest drain insertion is definitive. 4th or 5th ICS anterior axillary line (safe triangle) is the correct site; underwater seal drainage prevents re-accumulation.

management_logic: Bilateral tension pneumothorax in IPPV is one of the few true anaesthetic emergencies where the treatment must be immediate and physical — no drug will help before decompression. The non-obvious step is bilateral needle decompression without waiting for CXR confirmation, and disconnecting from the ventilator simultaneously. Continued IPPV accelerates bilateral tension in an asthmatic patient.
learning_point: Bilateral tension pneumothorax in IPPV requires immediate bilateral needle decompression without waiting for CXR — bilateral because unilateral decompression in a bilateral tension will not restore haemodynamics. Disconnect from ventilator simultaneously to stop positive pressure from continuing to expand the pneumothorax.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension; Ch 37 Major Trauma; Aho JM et al. Needle Thoracostomy, J Trauma 2016 (PMID 26196168)

title: Pre-operative Medication — ACE Inhibitor on Day of Surgery
topic: Pre-operative Medication - ACE Inhibitor on Day of Surgery
source: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 2 — Preoperative Considerations; Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension
demographics: 64M, 82 kg · Pre-op assessment, day of elective laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy. Antihypertensives: ramipril 10 mg OD, amlodipine 10 mg OD, atenolol 50 mg OD.
presentation: Pre-operative medication review. Patient has taken all his usual morning medications including ramipril 10 mg at 07:00. Surgery list starts at 09:00. Previous anaesthetic note documents 'severe refractory hypotension post-induction, difficult to manage, needed large doses noradrenaline' — ramipril was also taken on that occasion. BP today 146/88 mmHg.
constraints: ACE inhibitor taken on morning of surgery — vasoplegia risk
Beta-blocker also taken — blunted sympathetic response to hypotension
Previous documented refractory hypotension with same medication history

options.text: Document the pre-operative medication history carefully — confirm ACE inhibitor was taken and exact timing; note the previous anaesthetic complication
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook Ch 39: 'ACE inhibitors and ARBs' are listed as a risk factor for severe intraoperative hypotension; previous documented refractory hypotension with the same drug is a strong predictor — systematic documentation is the first step.

options.text: Discuss with the operating surgeon whether to proceed or defer 24 hours — ACE inhibitor already taken, cannot be 'untaken'
options.rationale: Unlike NSAIDs or anticoagulants, an already-taken ACE inhibitor cannot be reversed; proceeding requires a strategy; deferral eliminates the risk. The decision to proceed vs defer must be made before induction, not managed reactively.

options.text: If proceeding: pre-warm vasopressor — prepare noradrenaline infusion and have vasopressin 4 units available before induction
options.rationale: ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker vasoplegia post-induction may be refractory to ephedrine and phenylephrine; vasopressin (bypasses the renin-angiotensin axis) and noradrenaline infusion should be prepared before induction, not drawn up after collapse.

options.text: Modify induction technique — use ketamine or reduced-dose propofol titrated; avoid etomidate alone (provides no sympatholysis protection but obscures monitoring)
options.rationale: Ketamine preserves sympathetic tone; slow titration of propofol reduces the peak vasodilatation; induction technique modification reduces the severity of the predictable hypotension.

options.text: Insert arterial line before induction — beat-to-beat monitoring essential in predicted vasoplegia
options.rationale: Oxford Handbook: in high-risk intraoperative hypotension, arterial line monitoring is standard; in a patient with documented previous refractory hypotension on the same drugs, arterial line before induction is mandatory.

options.text: Advise patient and document: ACE inhibitor should be withheld on the morning of future elective procedures — add to anaesthetic alert
options.rationale: The evidence base (and clinical experience documented in this patient's own notes) supports withholding ACE inhibitors on the morning of elective surgery; documenting this as an anaesthetic alert prevents the same complication at future procedures.

management_logic: ACE inhibitor taken on the morning of surgery combined with a previous documented episode of refractory hypotension makes this a predictable complication — not an unpredictable emergency. The management must therefore be pre-emptive, not reactive. Vasopressin bypasses the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system and is the specific vasopressor for ACE-inhibitor-mediated vasoplegia. The non-obvious step is preparing vasopressin before induction rather than discovering its necessity after collapse.
learning_point: ACE inhibitor-mediated vasoplegia after induction may be refractory to standard vasopressors (phenylephrine, ephedrine) because they act on adrenergic pathways. Vasopressin (bypasses the RAAS) and noradrenaline should be prepared before induction in a patient with ACE inhibitor on board and a documented previous vasoplegia episode.
evidence_basis: Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5th Ed, Ch 39 — Severe Hypotension Risk Factors; Kheterpal S et al., ACE inhibitors and intraoperative hypotension, Anesthesiology 2012 (PMID 22343475)

title: Rapid desaturation during induction in morbid obesity
topic: Rapid desaturation during induction in morbid obesity
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: airway management, respiratory physiology, obese patient considerations; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: emergency airway management
presentation: A 52-year-old man with BMI 43 kg/m2 and severe obstructive sleep apnea is induced for emergency laparotomy. Mask ventilation is possible but difficult. Within 45 seconds after loss of consciousness, SpO2 falls from 99 percent to 84 percent.
constraints: High oxygen consumption and low FRC
Do not persist with repeated laryngoscopy during falling saturation

options.text: Stop laryngoscopy attempts, call for help, and restore oxygenation with two-person mask ventilation using 100 percent oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop laryngoscopy attempts, call for help, and restore oxygenation with two-person mask ventilation using 100 percent oxygen. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Optimize airway position with ramping, jaw thrust, oral airway, tight mask seal, and adequate positive pressure ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Optimize airway position with ramping, jaw thrust, oral airway, tight mask seal, and adequate positive pressure ventilation. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: If mask ventilation remains difficult, insert a second-generation supraglottic airway and ventilate.
options.rationale: Priority 3. If mask ventilation remains difficult, insert a second-generation supraglottic airway and ventilate. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Once SpO2 recovers, proceed with the most likely successful intubation plan using videolaryngoscope and bougie.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Once SpO2 recovers, proceed with the most likely successful intubation plan using videolaryngoscope and bougie. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Confirm tracheal placement with waveform capnography and secure the tube.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Confirm tracheal placement with waveform capnography and secure the tube. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Document difficult airway and plan extubation and postoperative monitoring before the end of surgery.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document difficult airway and plan extubation and postoperative monitoring before the end of surgery. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: The immediate danger is hypoxic arrest, not failure to intubate. In the obese patient, safe apnea time is short; oxygenation must be restored before further airway instrumentation.
learning_point: In peri-induction hypoxemia, stop the failing maneuver and oxygenate first. Intubation is secondary to oxygenation.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: airway management, respiratory physiology, obese patient considerations; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: emergency airway management

title: Failed direct laryngoscopy but mask ventilation is adequate
topic: Failed direct laryngoscopy but mask ventilation is adequate
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: adult airway management; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: difficult airway
presentation: A 46-year-old woman undergoing thyroidectomy has an unanticipated grade 3 laryngoscopic view. Two direct laryngoscopy attempts fail. SpO2 remains 98 percent with mask ventilation.
constraints: Repeated direct attempts will worsen edema

options.text: Stop further direct laryngoscopy and maintain oxygenation with face-mask ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop further direct laryngoscopy and maintain oxygenation with face-mask ventilation. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Call for senior airway help and bring the difficult-airway cart.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Call for senior airway help and bring the difficult-airway cart. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Reassess airway plan: videolaryngoscope, bougie, flexible bronchoscope, or wake the patient depending on urgency and conditions.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Reassess airway plan: videolaryngoscope, bougie, flexible bronchoscope, or wake the patient depending on urgency and conditions. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Limit the next attempt to the best available technique with optimized positioning and suction.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Limit the next attempt to the best available technique with optimized positioning and suction. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: If intubation succeeds, confirm with waveform capnography and protect against airway swelling.
options.rationale: Priority 5. If intubation succeeds, confirm with waveform capnography and protect against airway swelling. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: If safe tracheal intubation remains unlikely, wake the patient and reschedule with an awake airway plan.
options.rationale: Priority 6. If safe tracheal intubation remains unlikely, wake the patient and reschedule with an awake airway plan. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: This is failed intubation, not CICO. Oxygenation is preserved, so the safest priority is to stop repeated trauma, call help, and select a better technique or wake the patient.
learning_point: When mask ventilation is adequate, failed intubation is a controlled problem. Avoid turning it into a bleeding, swollen airway.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: adult airway management; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: difficult airway

title: Cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate after induction
topic: Cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate after induction
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: adult difficult airway and emergency invasive airway access; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: emergency airway
presentation: A trauma patient with facial swelling is induced for wound exploration. Mask ventilation fails despite two-person technique. Supraglottic airway placement fails twice. SpO2 is 62 percent and falling. No waveform capnography is seen.
constraints: Further attempts above the neck are unsafe

options.text: Declare CICO loudly and call for front-of-neck airway immediately.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Declare CICO loudly and call for front-of-neck airway immediately. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Continue best-attempt oxygenation while the neck is exposed and cricothyroid membrane identified.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Continue best-attempt oxygenation while the neck is exposed and cricothyroid membrane identified. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Perform emergency front-of-neck airway using the locally trained scalpel-bougie-tube or approved technique.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Perform emergency front-of-neck airway using the locally trained scalpel-bougie-tube or approved technique. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Confirm ventilation with chest rise, improving SpO2, and waveform capnography.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Confirm ventilation with chest rise, improving SpO2, and waveform capnography. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.
