options.text: Treat peri-arrest physiology with chest compressions if pulseless, vasopressor support, and correction of hypoxia.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Treat peri-arrest physiology with chest compressions if pulseless, vasopressor support, and correction of hypoxia. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: After stabilization, secure definitive airway care, inform the team, and document the event.
options.rationale: Priority 6. After stabilization, secure definitive airway care, inform the team, and document the event. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Once both mask ventilation and supraglottic rescue have failed with falling saturation, definitive oxygenation must come through the neck. Additional laryngoscopy attempts waste the remaining oxygen reserve.
learning_point: CICO is an oxygenation emergency. Declare it early and move to front-of-neck access without delay.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: adult difficult airway and emergency invasive airway access; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: emergency airway

title: Aspiration during induction for intestinal obstruction
topic: Aspiration during induction for intestinal obstruction
source: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: aspiration and postoperative respiratory failure; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: emergency airway and perioperative pulmonary complications
presentation: A 64-year-old man with bowel obstruction regurgitates feculent fluid during RSI. The laryngoscopic view is temporarily obscured. SpO2 drops to 88 percent.
constraints: Avoid blind positive pressure before airway control if possible

options.text: Turn head-down or lateral if feasible and suction the oropharynx aggressively.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Turn head-down or lateral if feasible and suction the oropharynx aggressively. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Secure the trachea rapidly with cuffed endotracheal tube and inflate cuff.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Secure the trachea rapidly with cuffed endotracheal tube and inflate cuff. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography and ventilate with 100 percent oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography and ventilate with 100 percent oxygen. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Suction the trachea through the tube and consider bronchoscopy if particulate matter or lobar obstruction is suspected.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Suction the trachea through the tube and consider bronchoscopy if particulate matter or lobar obstruction is suspected. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Assess gas exchange and lung mechanics; obtain ABG and chest imaging after immediate stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Assess gas exchange and lung mechanics; obtain ABG and chest imaging after immediate stabilization. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Plan postoperative ventilation or ICU observation according to oxygenation, contamination, and surgical urgency.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan postoperative ventilation or ICU observation according to oxygenation, contamination, and surgical urgency. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: The sequence prioritizes airway protection and oxygenation. Antibiotics and steroids are not the first response to aspiration; they depend on contamination, infection risk, and subsequent clinical course.
learning_point: In aspiration during induction, suction and cuffed tracheal isolation come before diagnostic workup.
evidence_basis: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: aspiration and postoperative respiratory failure; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: emergency airway and perioperative pulmonary complications

title: Sudden severe bronchospasm after intubation
topic: Sudden severe bronchospasm after intubation
source: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: asthma/COPD and intraoperative bronchospasm; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: pulmonary pharmacology and respiratory monitoring
presentation: A 35-year-old known asthmatic develops wheeze, high peak airway pressure, prolonged expiration, and falling EtCO2 after intubation. SpO2 falls to 90 percent.

options.text: Call for help, increase FiO2 to 100 percent, and manually ventilate to assess compliance.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help, increase FiO2 to 100 percent, and manually ventilate to assess compliance. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Exclude mechanical causes: kinked tube, endobronchial intubation, blocked circuit, mucus plug, pneumothorax.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Exclude mechanical causes: kinked tube, endobronchial intubation, blocked circuit, mucus plug, pneumothorax. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Deepen anesthesia with bronchodilating anesthetic strategy and avoid light anesthesia stimulation.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Deepen anesthesia with bronchodilating anesthetic strategy and avoid light anesthesia stimulation. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Administer inhaled beta-2 agonist through the circuit and repeat as clinically indicated.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Administer inhaled beta-2 agonist through the circuit and repeat as clinically indicated. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Adjust ventilation for obstructive physiology: low rate, longer expiratory time, permissive hypercapnia if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Adjust ventilation for obstructive physiology: low rate, longer expiratory time, permissive hypercapnia if needed. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: If refractory or unstable, escalate to epinephrine, magnesium, steroids, ABG-guided care, and ICU planning.
options.rationale: Priority 6. If refractory or unstable, escalate to epinephrine, magnesium, steroids, ABG-guided care, and ICU planning. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Severe bronchospasm can be mimicked by tube or circuit problems. Immediate management combines oxygenation, exclusion of mechanical causes, deepening anesthesia, bronchodilation, and prevention of air trapping.
learning_point: Treat bronchospasm, but first make sure it is bronchospasm.
evidence_basis: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: asthma/COPD and intraoperative bronchospasm; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: pulmonary pharmacology and respiratory monitoring

title: Anaphylaxis after antibiotic and neuromuscular blocker
topic: Anaphylaxis after antibiotic and neuromuscular blocker
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: allergy/anaphylaxis and perioperative crisis management; Primary FRCA SOE Guide 2: critical incident structure
presentation: A 29-year-old woman receives cefazolin and rocuronium after induction. Two minutes later she develops severe hypotension, bronchospasm, generalized flushing, and falling capnography.

options.text: Declare suspected anaphylaxis, stop suspected agents, call for help, and give 100 percent oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Declare suspected anaphylaxis, stop suspected agents, call for help, and give 100 percent oxygen. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Administer epinephrine promptly according to severity and institutional protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Administer epinephrine promptly according to severity and institutional protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Give rapid IV crystalloid resuscitation and start vasopressor infusion if shock persists.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give rapid IV crystalloid resuscitation and start vasopressor infusion if shock persists. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat bronchospasm with inhaled bronchodilator and deepen anesthesia as required.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat bronchospasm with inhaled bronchodilator and deepen anesthesia as required. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Give adjuncts after epinephrine: antihistamine and corticosteroid per protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Give adjuncts after epinephrine: antihistamine and corticosteroid per protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Send timed serum tryptase, document all exposures, defer elective surgery, and arrange allergy referral.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Send timed serum tryptase, document all exposures, defer elective surgery, and arrange allergy referral. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Perioperative anaphylaxis is a circulation-first emergency. Epinephrine and fluids are definitive early actions; antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts, not substitutes.
learning_point: In anesthesia anaphylaxis, delayed epinephrine is the common dangerous error.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: allergy/anaphylaxis and perioperative crisis management; Primary FRCA SOE Guide 2: critical incident structure

title: Profound hypotension after spinal anesthesia for hip fracture
topic: Profound hypotension after spinal anesthesia for hip fracture
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: spinal anesthesia physiology and complications; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: neuraxial anesthesia emergencies
presentation: An 82-year-old woman receives spinal anesthesia for hip fracture fixation. Five minutes later she becomes drowsy and nauseated. BP is 62/34 mmHg.
constraints: High block possible but breathing intact

options.text: Recognize severe spinal-induced hypotension, call for help, give oxygen, and check pulse and consciousness.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Recognize severe spinal-induced hypotension, call for help, give oxygen, and check pulse and consciousness. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat immediately with vasopressor appropriate to heart rate and clinical context.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Treat immediately with vasopressor appropriate to heart rate and clinical context. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Give cautious IV fluid bolus while avoiding overload in frail cardiac patients.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give cautious IV fluid bolus while avoiding overload in frail cardiac patients. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Assess block height, ventilation, and need for airway support if block ascends.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Assess block height, ventilation, and need for airway support if block ascends. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Search for coexisting causes: occult bleeding, myocardial ischemia, sepsis, medication effect.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Search for coexisting causes: occult bleeding, myocardial ischemia, sepsis, medication effect. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Continue invasive or frequent BP monitoring and plan postoperative surveillance.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue invasive or frequent BP monitoring and plan postoperative surveillance. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Severe hypotension after spinal anesthesia can rapidly cause cerebral and myocardial hypoperfusion. Vasopressor treatment should not wait for large fluid loading.
learning_point: After spinal anesthesia, treat dangerous hypotension first; analyze causes in parallel.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: spinal anesthesia physiology and complications; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: neuraxial anesthesia emergencies

title: High spinal after cesarean spinal anesthesia
topic: High spinal after cesarean spinal anesthesia
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia, 6e: neuraxial complications and cesarean anesthesia; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: obstetric anesthesia
presentation: A 30-year-old term parturient develops arm weakness, difficulty breathing, whispering voice, severe hypotension, and bradycardia after spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery.

options.text: Call for obstetric, anesthesia, neonatal, and nursing help; give 100 percent oxygen and left uterine displacement.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for obstetric, anesthesia, neonatal, and nursing help; give 100 percent oxygen and left uterine displacement. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Support airway and ventilation early; prepare for rapid tracheal intubation if speech, breathing, or consciousness worsens.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Support airway and ventilation early; prepare for rapid tracheal intubation if speech, breathing, or consciousness worsens. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat bradycardia and hypotension aggressively with vasopressor and anticholinergic as indicated.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Treat bradycardia and hypotension aggressively with vasopressor and anticholinergic as indicated. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Give rapid IV fluid co-load while avoiding delay in vasopressor therapy.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Give rapid IV fluid co-load while avoiding delay in vasopressor therapy. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Proceed with delivery once maternal oxygenation and circulation are being actively supported.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Proceed with delivery once maternal oxygenation and circulation are being actively supported. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Continue ventilatory and hemodynamic support until block regresses; document and debrief.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue ventilatory and hemodynamic support until block regresses; document and debrief. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: High spinal in obstetrics is a maternal resuscitation problem. Airway, ventilation, vasopressor support, uterine displacement, and delivery must be coordinated.
learning_point: In cesarean high spinal, the mother is the resuscitation priority; fetal benefit follows maternal oxygenation and perfusion.
evidence_basis: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia, 6e: neuraxial complications and cesarean anesthesia; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: obstetric anesthesia

title: Seizure after supraclavicular brachial plexus block
topic: Seizure after supraclavicular brachial plexus block
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: local anesthetic toxicity; Advanced Anesthesia Review, 2023: LAST and lipid emulsion topics
presentation: A 41-year-old man receives an ultrasound-guided supraclavicular block. Shortly after incremental injection, he reports metallic taste and tinnitus, then develops generalized tonic-clonic seizure.
constraints: Avoid large propofol doses in unstable patient

options.text: Stop local anesthetic injection, call for LAST help, and secure oxygenation with 100 percent oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop local anesthetic injection, call for LAST help, and secure oxygenation with 100 percent oxygen. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Control seizure with benzodiazepine or small titrated anesthetic dose while avoiding cardiovascular depression.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Control seizure with benzodiazepine or small titrated anesthetic dose while avoiding cardiovascular depression. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Start lipid emulsion therapy immediately according to institutional LAST protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Start lipid emulsion therapy immediately according to institutional LAST protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat arrhythmia or cardiac arrest with modified resuscitation appropriate for LAST.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat arrhythmia or cardiac arrest with modified resuscitation appropriate for LAST. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Avoid vasopressin, calcium-channel blockers, beta-blockers, and excessive epinephrine unless local protocol specifies otherwise.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Avoid vasopressin, calcium-channel blockers, beta-blockers, and excessive epinephrine unless local protocol specifies otherwise. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Arrange monitored observation because recurrence can occur after initial stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Arrange monitored observation because recurrence can occur after initial stabilization. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: LAST is a time-critical diagnosis when neurologic symptoms follow local anesthetic injection. Airway support, seizure control, lipid therapy, and modified resuscitation are the core sequence.
learning_point: After a regional block, seizure plus arrhythmia is LAST until proved otherwise.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: local anesthetic toxicity; Advanced Anesthesia Review, 2023: LAST and lipid emulsion topics

title: Hypercarbia and rigidity during volatile anesthesia
topic: Hypercarbia and rigidity during volatile anesthesia
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: malignant hyperthermia and neuromuscular disorders; Coté and Lerman, 6e: malignant hyperthermia in children
presentation: A 17-year-old male undergoing scoliosis correction under sevoflurane develops rapidly rising EtCO2, masseter rigidity, tachycardia, hyperthermia, and mixed acidosis.
constraints: Surgery ongoing but emergency overrides surgery

options.text: Declare malignant hyperthermia, stop volatile anesthetic and succinylcholine, and call for MH cart and help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Declare malignant hyperthermia, stop volatile anesthetic and succinylcholine, and call for MH cart and help. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Hyperventilate with 100 percent oxygen using a clean circuit or charcoal filters according to local setup.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Hyperventilate with 100 percent oxygen using a clean circuit or charcoal filters according to local setup. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Give dantrolene immediately per MH protocol and repeat until signs improve.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give dantrolene immediately per MH protocol and repeat until signs improve. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Actively cool, treat hyperkalemia and acidosis, and monitor ABG, electrolytes, CK, myoglobin, urine output, and coagulation.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Actively cool, treat hyperkalemia and acidosis, and monitor ABG, electrolytes, CK, myoglobin, urine output, and coagulation. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Control arrhythmias while avoiding calcium-channel blockers with dantrolene.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Control arrhythmias while avoiding calcium-channel blockers with dantrolene. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Transfer to ICU for ongoing dantrolene protocol, recurrence monitoring, counseling, and family referral.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU for ongoing dantrolene protocol, recurrence monitoring, counseling, and family referral. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Rising EtCO2 despite ventilation is often the earliest practical warning. Treatment is immediate trigger removal, oxygen, dantrolene, cooling, and management of hyperkalemic acidosis.
learning_point: In suspected MH, do not wait for fever. Rising CO2 plus rigidity and tachycardia is enough to act.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: malignant hyperthermia and neuromuscular disorders; Coté and Lerman, 6e: malignant hyperthermia in children

title: Sudden high airway pressure after pneumoperitoneum
topic: Sudden high airway pressure after pneumoperitoneum
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: respiratory monitoring and robotic/laparoscopic anesthesia; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: intraoperative ventilation problems
presentation: A 58-year-old woman undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy develops sudden high peak pressure after insufflation. SpO2 falls from 99 percent to 92 percent. EtCO2 rises.
constraints: Need rapid differentiation of tube/circuit/patient/surgical causes

options.text: Switch to 100 percent oxygen and manually ventilate to assess compliance and circuit function.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Switch to 100 percent oxygen and manually ventilate to assess compliance and circuit function. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Check tube and circuit immediately: kink, obstruction, filter, secretions, endobronchial migration.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Check tube and circuit immediately: kink, obstruction, filter, secretions, endobronchial migration. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Ask surgeon to stop insufflation and release pneumoperitoneum if ventilation remains difficult.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ask surgeon to stop insufflation and release pneumoperitoneum if ventilation remains difficult. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Examine chest and capnography for bronchospasm, pneumothorax, or reduced compliance.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Examine chest and capnography for bronchospasm, pneumothorax, or reduced compliance. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat the identified cause: suction, withdraw tube, bronchodilator, decompression, or ventilator adjustment.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Treat the identified cause: suction, withdraw tube, bronchodilator, decompression, or ventilator adjustment. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Resume surgery only after stable ventilation, acceptable gas exchange, and clear shared plan.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Resume surgery only after stable ventilation, acceptable gas exchange, and clear shared plan. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: High airway pressure is not a diagnosis. The priority is oxygen, manual assessment, mechanical checks, and reversal of the provoking surgical factor while excluding pneumothorax and bronchospasm.
learning_point: For high airway pressure, think circuit, tube, lung, and surgery—in that order during the first minute.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: respiratory monitoring and robotic/laparoscopic anesthesia; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: intraoperative ventilation problems

title: PACU laryngospasm after pediatric tonsillectomy
topic: PACU laryngospasm after pediatric tonsillectomy
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children, 10e: pediatric airway complications; Coté and Lerman, 6e: pediatric airway and PACU
presentation: A 5-year-old child after tonsillectomy develops inspiratory effort with no air movement, suprasternal recession, desaturation, and bradycardia in PACU.

options.text: Call for help, apply 100 percent oxygen, and remove stimulus by suctioning blood and secretions.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help, apply 100 percent oxygen, and remove stimulus by suctioning blood and secretions. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Perform airway maneuvers: jaw thrust, continuous positive airway pressure, and tight mask seal.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Perform airway maneuvers: jaw thrust, continuous positive airway pressure, and tight mask seal. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Deepen anesthesia or give small propofol dose if IV access and circulation permit.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Deepen anesthesia or give small propofol dose if IV access and circulation permit. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: If complete obstruction or bradycardia persists, give succinylcholine or alternative paralytic per pediatric protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 4. If complete obstruction or bradycardia persists, give succinylcholine or alternative paralytic per pediatric protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Ventilate with bag-mask after relaxation; intubate if oxygenation remains unsafe or bleeding is suspected.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Ventilate with bag-mask after relaxation; intubate if oxygenation remains unsafe or bleeding is suspected. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Observe for negative-pressure pulmonary edema and need for admission.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Observe for negative-pressure pulmonary edema and need for admission. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Complete laryngospasm in children can progress to bradycardic arrest. Suction, CPAP, jaw thrust, deepening anesthesia, and rapid paralysis are prioritized by severity.
learning_point: In pediatric laryngospasm with bradycardia, paralysis is safer than prolonged obstruction.
evidence_basis: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children, 10e: pediatric airway complications; Coté and Lerman, 6e: pediatric airway and PACU

title: ST depression during emergence in ischemic heart disease
topic: ST depression during emergence in ischemic heart disease
source: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: ischemic heart disease and noncardiac surgery; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: cardiac risk and PACU ischemia
presentation: A 68-year-old man with prior MI develops hypertension, tachycardia, and 2 mm ST depression during emergence. He is coughing on the tube and shivering.

options.text: Increase oxygen delivery and confirm hemodynamic stability; deepen or smooth emergence if still intubated.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Increase oxygen delivery and confirm hemodynamic stability; deepen or smooth emergence if still intubated. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat tachycardia and hypertension promptly while avoiding hypotension.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Treat tachycardia and hypertension promptly while avoiding hypotension. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat pain, coughing, agitation, and shivering as reversible demand triggers.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Treat pain, coughing, agitation, and shivering as reversible demand triggers. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Check ECG leads, obtain 12-lead ECG when feasible, and send troponin according to protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Check ECG leads, obtain 12-lead ECG when feasible, and send troponin according to protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Consult cardiology or perioperative physician if ischemia persists or biomarkers rise.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Consult cardiology or perioperative physician if ischemia persists or biomarkers rise. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Plan monitored postoperative location, analgesia, warming, oxygen, and continuation of indicated cardiac medications.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan monitored postoperative location, analgesia, warming, oxygen, and continuation of indicated cardiac medications. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Myocardial ischemia during emergence is usually supply-demand mismatch. The immediate sequence is oxygenation, control of heart rate and blood pressure, relief of stimulation, and diagnostic confirmation.
learning_point: In ischemic heart disease, emergence is a stress test. Control demand before chasing labels.
evidence_basis: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: ischemic heart disease and noncardiac surgery; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: cardiac risk and PACU ischemia

title: Massive hemorrhage during pelvic cancer surgery
topic: Massive hemorrhage during pelvic cancer surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: patient blood management and transfusion therapy; Morgan & Mikhail's Clinical Anesthesiology, 7e: intraoperative fluid and blood management
presentation: During pelvic tumor resection, sudden venous bleeding fills the field. Suction canisters rapidly collect 1500 mL blood. BP falls and EtCO2 decreases.
constraints: Need communication and massive transfusion protocol

options.text: Announce major hemorrhage, call for help, and ask surgeon for immediate packing or vascular control.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Announce major hemorrhage, call for help, and ask surgeon for immediate packing or vascular control. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Increase FiO2, adjust anesthesia for shock, and support circulation with vasopressors as a bridge to volume replacement.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Increase FiO2, adjust anesthesia for shock, and support circulation with vasopressors as a bridge to volume replacement. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Activate massive transfusion protocol and obtain rapid blood products.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Activate massive transfusion protocol and obtain rapid blood products. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Secure adequate access: large-bore IV/rapid infuser/arterial line and central access if needed without delaying transfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Secure adequate access: large-bore IV/rapid infuser/arterial line and central access if needed without delaying transfusion. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Use goal-directed coagulation correction with labs or viscoelastic testing; treat calcium, temperature, acid-base, and fibrinogen deficits.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use goal-directed coagulation correction with labs or viscoelastic testing; treat calcium, temperature, acid-base, and fibrinogen deficits. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Plan ICU transfer, postoperative ventilation if needed, and handover of blood loss, products, labs, and ongoing risks.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan ICU transfer, postoperative ventilation if needed, and handover of blood loss, products, labs, and ongoing risks. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Bleeding control and balanced resuscitation must proceed together. Vasopressors can temporize but cannot replace blood volume and hemostasis.
learning_point: In massive hemorrhage, the first drug is communication; the definitive treatment is surgical control plus blood component resuscitation.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: patient blood management and transfusion therapy; Morgan & Mikhail's Clinical Anesthesiology, 7e: intraoperative fluid and blood management

title: Confusion and bradycardia during TURP
topic: Confusion and bradycardia during TURP
source: Morgan & Mikhail's Clinical Anesthesiology, 7e: urologic anesthesia; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: TURP syndrome
presentation: A 72-year-old man under spinal anesthesia for TURP becomes restless, confused, nauseated, bradycardic, and hypertensive after prolonged resection.
constraints: Spinal anesthesia permits early neurologic symptoms

options.text: Suspect TURP syndrome, inform surgeon, and stop or rapidly conclude surgery.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Suspect TURP syndrome, inform surgeon, and stop or rapidly conclude surgery. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Assess airway, oxygenation, circulation, and neurologic status; give oxygen and prepare for airway support if consciousness worsens.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Assess airway, oxygenation, circulation, and neurologic status; give oxygen and prepare for airway support if consciousness worsens. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Send urgent electrolytes, osmolality if available, ABG, glucose, and hemoglobin.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Send urgent electrolytes, osmolality if available, ABG, glucose, and hemoglobin. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat seizures or severe symptomatic hyponatremia according to institutional hypertonic saline protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat seizures or severe symptomatic hyponatremia according to institutional hypertonic saline protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Treat fluid overload or pulmonary edema if present; avoid rapid overcorrection of sodium.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Treat fluid overload or pulmonary edema if present; avoid rapid overcorrection of sodium. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Transfer to monitored recovery or ICU with serial sodium and neurologic observation.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to monitored recovery or ICU with serial sodium and neurologic observation. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: TURP syndrome is suspected from neurologic changes, bradycardia, hypertension, and prolonged irrigation exposure. Surgery must stop while airway, circulation, and sodium-related neurologic risk are managed.
learning_point: During TURP under spinal anesthesia, new confusion is a warning sign, not anxiety.
evidence_basis: Morgan & Mikhail's Clinical Anesthesiology, 7e: urologic anesthesia; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: TURP syndrome

title: Abrupt EtCO2 fall during laparoscopic liver surgery
topic: Abrupt EtCO2 fall during laparoscopic liver surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: laparoscopy/robotic surgery and embolic complications; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: intraoperative embolism
presentation: During laparoscopic liver resection, the surgeon reports venous bleeding. EtCO2 abruptly falls from 36 to 18 mmHg, SpO2 falls, and hypotension develops.
constraints: EtCO2 fall suggests reduced pulmonary blood flow

options.text: Declare suspected gas embolism, stop insufflation, and release pneumoperitoneum.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Declare suspected gas embolism, stop insufflation, and release pneumoperitoneum. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Give 100 percent oxygen and support ventilation and circulation immediately.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100 percent oxygen and support ventilation and circulation immediately. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Ask surgeon to flood or pack the field and prevent further gas entrainment.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ask surgeon to flood or pack the field and prevent further gas entrainment. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Place patient in Durant or left lateral head-down position if feasible and appropriate for surgery.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Place patient in Durant or left lateral head-down position if feasible and appropriate for surgery. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Aspirate via central venous catheter if already present and correctly positioned; do not delay resuscitation to insert one.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Aspirate via central venous catheter if already present and correctly positioned; do not delay resuscitation to insert one. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Use invasive monitoring, ABG, echocardiography if available, and decide whether to abandon or resume surgery after stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Use invasive monitoring, ABG, echocardiography if available, and decide whether to abandon or resume surgery after stabilization. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: The abrupt fall in EtCO2 with hypotension during laparoscopy indicates sudden reduction in pulmonary perfusion. Insufflation must stop while oxygenation and circulation are restored.
learning_point: In suspected gas embolism, stop gas entry first, then resuscitate physiology.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: laparoscopy/robotic surgery and embolic complications; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: intraoperative embolism

title: Airway fire during laser microlaryngeal surgery
topic: Airway fire during laser microlaryngeal surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: ENT/laser airway surgery and operating room fire safety; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: airway fire
presentation: During laser excision of a vocal cord lesion, a flash and smoke are seen near the endotracheal tube. The patient is being ventilated with high FiO2.

options.text: Announce airway fire; stop ventilation and disconnect oxygen and anesthetic gases.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Announce airway fire; stop ventilation and disconnect oxygen and anesthetic gases. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Remove the burning endotracheal tube immediately.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Remove the burning endotracheal tube immediately. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Flood airway with saline or water as available and extinguish residual fire.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Flood airway with saline or water as available and extinguish residual fire. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Re-establish ventilation with room air first, then titrated oxygen after fire is extinguished.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Re-establish ventilation with room air first, then titrated oxygen after fire is extinguished. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Perform bronchoscopy to assess airway injury and remove debris.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Perform bronchoscopy to assess airway injury and remove debris. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Plan ICU observation, steroid or bronchodilator strategy as indicated, and full incident documentation.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan ICU observation, steroid or bronchodilator strategy as indicated, and full incident documentation. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Airway fire management requires immediate removal of the oxidizer and fuel source. Ventilation resumes only after the fire is extinguished and the airway is reassessed.
learning_point: In airway fire, oxygen off and tube out are the first lifesaving actions.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: ENT/laser airway surgery and operating room fire safety; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia, 5e: airway fire

title: Pacemaker inhibition during monopolar cautery
topic: Pacemaker inhibition during monopolar cautery
source: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: pacemakers and ICDs; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: implantable cardiac pulse generators
presentation: A pacemaker-dependent patient undergoing abdominal surgery develops profound bradycardia and hypotension whenever monopolar cautery is used. ECG shows pacing spikes disappearing during cautery.

options.text: Tell surgeon to stop cautery immediately and assess pulse, BP, and perfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Tell surgeon to stop cautery immediately and assess pulse, BP, and perfusion. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Support circulation with vasopressor or chronotropic therapy while restoring pacing.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Support circulation with vasopressor or chronotropic therapy while restoring pacing. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Apply magnet or reprogram to asynchronous pacing according to device type and preoperative plan.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Apply magnet or reprogram to asynchronous pacing according to device type and preoperative plan. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Ensure external pacing and defibrillation are ready and pads correctly positioned.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Ensure external pacing and defibrillation are ready and pads correctly positioned. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Minimize EMI: use bipolar cautery, short bursts, lowest effective energy, and current path away from device.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Minimize EMI: use bipolar cautery, short bursts, lowest effective energy, and current path away from device. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Arrange postoperative device interrogation and document intraoperative EMI event.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Arrange postoperative device interrogation and document intraoperative EMI event. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Pacemaker inhibition in a dependent patient is a circulation emergency. Stop the EMI source first, restore reliable pacing, and alter surgical energy strategy.
learning_point: In pacemaker-dependent patients, cautery-induced loss of pacing is loss of cardiac output until proved otherwise.
evidence_basis: Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: pacemakers and ICDs; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: implantable cardiac pulse generators

title: Severe postpartum hemorrhage under general anesthesia
topic: Severe postpartum hemorrhage under general anesthesia
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia, 6e: postpartum hemorrhage; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: obstetric anesthesia and transfusion
presentation: After cesarean delivery for placenta previa, the uterus remains atonic with brisk bleeding. Estimated blood loss crosses 2000 mL. BP falls and fibrinogen is suspected to be low.

options.text: Declare major obstetric hemorrhage, call for obstetric, anesthesia, blood bank, and neonatal support.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Declare major obstetric hemorrhage, call for obstetric, anesthesia, blood bank, and neonatal support. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Ask obstetrician to perform immediate uterine massage and escalate uterotonic therapy per protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Ask obstetrician to perform immediate uterine massage and escalate uterotonic therapy per protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Activate massive transfusion protocol and secure rapid blood component delivery.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Activate massive transfusion protocol and secure rapid blood component delivery. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Optimize access and monitoring; maintain oxygenation, normothermia, calcium, acid-base status, and perfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Optimize access and monitoring; maintain oxygenation, normothermia, calcium, acid-base status, and perfusion. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Check coagulation including fibrinogen or viscoelastic testing and replace fibrinogen early if low or bleeding persists.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Check coagulation including fibrinogen or viscoelastic testing and replace fibrinogen early if low or bleeding persists. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Prepare for surgical escalation: balloon tamponade, compression sutures, arterial ligation, embolization, or hysterectomy.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Prepare for surgical escalation: balloon tamponade, compression sutures, arterial ligation, embolization, or hysterectomy. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Obstetric hemorrhage requires simultaneous uterine treatment, surgical control, transfusion, and coagulation correction. Fibrinogen depletion is especially important in severe PPH.
learning_point: In PPH, uterotonics, blood products, and surgical escalation must run in parallel, not sequentially after collapse.
evidence_basis: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia, 6e: postpartum hemorrhage; Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: obstetric anesthesia and transfusion

title: Brain swelling during craniotomy for tumor
topic: Brain swelling during craniotomy for tumor
source: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: neuroanesthesia and cerebral physiology; Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: neurosurgical anesthesia decision-making
presentation: During craniotomy for frontal tumor, the neurosurgeon reports tight brain before dural opening. The patient is anesthetized, intubated, and mechanically ventilated.
constraints: Avoid hypoxia, hypercapnia, hypotension, venous obstruction

options.text: Confirm airway, oxygenation, ventilation, anesthetic depth, head position, and absence of jugular venous obstruction.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Confirm airway, oxygenation, ventilation, anesthetic depth, head position, and absence of jugular venous obstruction. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Correct hypercapnia by increasing minute ventilation toward neurosurgical target EtCO2.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Correct hypercapnia by increasing minute ventilation toward neurosurgical target EtCO2. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Ensure head elevation, neutral neck, unobstructed venous drainage, and remove excessive PEEP if compromising venous return.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ensure head elevation, neutral neck, unobstructed venous drainage, and remove excessive PEEP if compromising venous return. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Administer osmotherapy as agreed with neurosurgeon and institutional protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Administer osmotherapy as agreed with neurosurgeon and institutional protocol. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Optimize anesthetic technique to reduce cerebral blood volume while maintaining perfusion pressure.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Optimize anesthetic technique to reduce cerebral blood volume while maintaining perfusion pressure. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

options.text: Discuss surgical decompression steps and postoperative ventilation or ICU plan if swelling persists.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Discuss surgical decompression steps and postoperative ventilation or ICU plan if swelling persists. This step is sequenced here because of the case physiology and the management logic described below.

management_logic: Tight brain management begins with reversible physiologic causes: hypoxia, hypercapnia, venous obstruction, excessive airway pressure, light anesthesia, and hypertension. Osmotherapy follows in a coordinated neurosurgical plan.
learning_point: In neuroanesthesia, the first treatment for tight brain is correction of physiology that increases cerebral blood volume.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia, 10e: neuroanesthesia and cerebral physiology; Yao & Artusio's Anesthesiology, 9e: neurosurgical anesthesia decision-making

title: Rapid desaturation during RSI for perforation
topic: Rapid desaturation during RSI for perforation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A full-stomach patient desaturates during RSI before the tube is placed. Mask ventilation is possible but aspiration risk is high.

options.text: Stop the intubation attempt and restore oxygenation with two-person mask ventilation using 100% oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop the intubation attempt and restore oxygenation with two-person mask ventilation using 100% oxygen. This is placed here because oxygenation must be restored before repeated airway attempts; the full-stomach risk modifies but does not abolish rescue ventilation.

options.text: Suction the mouth and optimize head position, jaw thrust, oral airway, and mask seal.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Suction the mouth and optimize head position, jaw thrust, oral airway, and mask seal. This is placed here because oxygenation must be restored before repeated airway attempts; the full-stomach risk modifies but does not abolish rescue ventilation.

options.text: Proceed to the best first-pass intubation plan with videolaryngoscope, bougie, and suction ready.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Proceed to the best first-pass intubation plan with videolaryngoscope, bougie, and suction ready. This is placed here because oxygenation must be restored before repeated airway attempts; the full-stomach risk modifies but does not abolish rescue ventilation.

options.text: Confirm tracheal intubation with sustained waveform capnography and cuff inflation.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Confirm tracheal intubation with sustained waveform capnography and cuff inflation. This is placed here because oxygenation must be restored before repeated airway attempts; the full-stomach risk modifies but does not abolish rescue ventilation.

options.text: Ventilate with lung-protective settings and reassess for aspiration, bronchospasm, and hypoxemia.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Ventilate with lung-protective settings and reassess for aspiration, bronchospasm, and hypoxemia. This is placed here because oxygenation must be restored before repeated airway attempts; the full-stomach risk modifies but does not abolish rescue ventilation.

options.text: Plan postoperative monitoring or ICU if aspiration or difficult airway physiology persists.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan postoperative monitoring or ICU if aspiration or difficult airway physiology persists. This is placed here because oxygenation must be restored before repeated airway attempts; the full-stomach risk modifies but does not abolish rescue ventilation.

management_logic: Oxygenation must be restored before repeated airway attempts; the full-stomach risk modifies but does not abolish rescue ventilation.
learning_point: Falling saturation changes RSI from aspiration-prevention mode to oxygen-rescue mode.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Expanding neck hematoma after thyroidectomy
topic: Expanding neck hematoma after thyroidectomy
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: Two hours after thyroidectomy, a patient develops stridor, neck swelling, agitation, and falling oxygen saturation.

options.text: Call ENT surgeon, anesthesia help, and prepare for immediate airway rescue.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call ENT surgeon, anesthesia help, and prepare for immediate airway rescue. This is placed here because the expanding hematoma is a time-critical extrinsic airway obstruction; decompression may be required before formal intubation.

options.text: Give high-flow oxygen and keep the patient sitting upright while equipment arrives.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give high-flow oxygen and keep the patient sitting upright while equipment arrives. This is placed here because the expanding hematoma is a time-critical extrinsic airway obstruction; decompression may be required before formal intubation.

options.text: Open the neck wound at bedside if airway obstruction is severe or rapidly worsening.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Open the neck wound at bedside if airway obstruction is severe or rapidly worsening. This is placed here because the expanding hematoma is a time-critical extrinsic airway obstruction; decompression may be required before formal intubation.

options.text: Secure the airway with the most experienced operator using awake or controlled technique as feasible.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Secure the airway with the most experienced operator using awake or controlled technique as feasible. This is placed here because the expanding hematoma is a time-critical extrinsic airway obstruction; decompression may be required before formal intubation.

options.text: Proceed to operating room for definitive hemostasis after airway and decompression are addressed.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Proceed to operating room for definitive hemostasis after airway and decompression are addressed. This is placed here because the expanding hematoma is a time-critical extrinsic airway obstruction; decompression may be required before formal intubation.

options.text: Monitor for tracheomalacia, recurrent laryngeal nerve dysfunction, and rebleeding.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor for tracheomalacia, recurrent laryngeal nerve dysfunction, and rebleeding. This is placed here because the expanding hematoma is a time-critical extrinsic airway obstruction; decompression may be required before formal intubation.

management_logic: The expanding hematoma is a time-critical extrinsic airway obstruction; decompression may be required before formal intubation.
learning_point: A post-thyroidectomy tense neck with stridor is a surgical airway emergency, not routine postoperative anxiety.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Extubation failure after major neck dissection
topic: Extubation failure after major neck dissection
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After long head and neck surgery, cuff leak is absent and the airway looks swollen. The surgeon requests extubation.

options.text: Do not extubate until a shared airway plan is agreed.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Do not extubate until a shared airway plan is agreed. This is placed here because the risk is failed reintubation after premature extubation; extubation is an elective airway procedure.

options.text: Assess edema, surgical field, bleeding risk, cuff leak, and reintubation difficulty.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Assess edema, surgical field, bleeding risk, cuff leak, and reintubation difficulty. This is placed here because the risk is failed reintubation after premature extubation; extubation is an elective airway procedure.

options.text: Prepare staged extubation with airway exchange catheter or planned postoperative ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Prepare staged extubation with airway exchange catheter or planned postoperative ventilation. This is placed here because the risk is failed reintubation after premature extubation; extubation is an elective airway procedure.

options.text: Ensure expert help, difficult airway cart, and front-of-neck access plan are immediately available.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Ensure expert help, difficult airway cart, and front-of-neck access plan are immediately available. This is placed here because the risk is failed reintubation after premature extubation; extubation is an elective airway procedure.

options.text: Extubate only when criteria are met, with oxygenation and reintubation plan active.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Extubate only when criteria are met, with oxygenation and reintubation plan active. This is placed here because the risk is failed reintubation after premature extubation; extubation is an elective airway procedure.

options.text: Document high-risk extubation plan and provide monitored postoperative care.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document high-risk extubation plan and provide monitored postoperative care. This is placed here because the risk is failed reintubation after premature extubation; extubation is an elective airway procedure.

management_logic: The risk is failed reintubation after premature extubation; extubation is an elective airway procedure.
learning_point: High-risk extubation deserves the same planning discipline as difficult intubation.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Fresh tracheostomy tube dislodgement
topic: Fresh tracheostomy tube dislodgement
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A fresh surgical tracheostomy dislodges during transfer. The patient is cyanosed and bagging through the tracheostomy is ineffective.
constraints: Oral airway may still be usable

options.text: Call for help and give 100% oxygen to face and stoma.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and give 100% oxygen to face and stoma. This is placed here because blind reinsertion into a fresh tract can create a false passage; oxygenation should use the safest patent route.

options.text: Remove the displaced tracheostomy tube if ventilation through it is ineffective.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Remove the displaced tracheostomy tube if ventilation through it is ineffective. This is placed here because blind reinsertion into a fresh tract can create a false passage; oxygenation should use the safest patent route.

options.text: Attempt oral bag-mask ventilation and oral intubation if upper airway is patent.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Attempt oral bag-mask ventilation and oral intubation if upper airway is patent. This is placed here because blind reinsertion into a fresh tract can create a false passage; oxygenation should use the safest patent route.

options.text: Ask surgeon to dilate or reopen the tracheostomy tract under direct vision if oral route fails.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Ask surgeon to dilate or reopen the tracheostomy tract under direct vision if oral route fails. This is placed here because blind reinsertion into a fresh tract can create a false passage; oxygenation should use the safest patent route.

options.text: Confirm ventilation with waveform capnography and secure the definitive airway.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Confirm ventilation with waveform capnography and secure the definitive airway. This is placed here because blind reinsertion into a fresh tract can create a false passage; oxygenation should use the safest patent route.

options.text: Review cause of displacement and secure tube before any further transfer.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Review cause of displacement and secure tube before any further transfer. This is placed here because blind reinsertion into a fresh tract can create a false passage; oxygenation should use the safest patent route.

management_logic: Blind reinsertion into a fresh tract can create a false passage; oxygenation should use the safest patent route.
learning_point: In fresh tracheostomy displacement, do not blindly push the tube back.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Hypoxemia after double-lumen tube placement
topic: Hypoxemia after double-lumen tube placement
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After positioning for thoracotomy, one-lung ventilation begins and SpO2 falls to 84%. Airway pressures are acceptable.

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and resume two-lung ventilation if saturation continues to fall.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and resume two-lung ventilation if saturation continues to fall. This is placed here because most early OLV desaturation is correctable by confirming tube position and restoring recruitable lung volume.

options.text: Check double-lumen tube position with bronchoscopy after lateral positioning.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Check double-lumen tube position with bronchoscopy after lateral positioning. This is placed here because most early OLV desaturation is correctable by confirming tube position and restoring recruitable lung volume.

options.text: Optimize ventilation of dependent lung with recruitment and appropriate PEEP.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Optimize ventilation of dependent lung with recruitment and appropriate PEEP. This is placed here because most early OLV desaturation is correctable by confirming tube position and restoring recruitable lung volume.

options.text: Ask surgeon to pause compression and apply CPAP or oxygen insufflation to operative lung if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Ask surgeon to pause compression and apply CPAP or oxygen insufflation to operative lung if needed. This is placed here because most early OLV desaturation is correctable by confirming tube position and restoring recruitable lung volume.

options.text: Assess hemodynamics, anemia, secretions, pneumothorax, and bronchospasm.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Assess hemodynamics, anemia, secretions, pneumothorax, and bronchospasm. This is placed here because most early OLV desaturation is correctable by confirming tube position and restoring recruitable lung volume.

options.text: Proceed only after oxygenation strategy and DLT position are stable.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Proceed only after oxygenation strategy and DLT position are stable. This is placed here because most early OLV desaturation is correctable by confirming tube position and restoring recruitable lung volume.

management_logic: Most early OLV desaturation is correctable by confirming tube position and restoring recruitable lung volume.
learning_point: In OLV hypoxemia, bronchoscopy is a treatment step, not a luxury.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Severe hypoxemia during one-lung ventilation
topic: Severe hypoxemia during one-lung ventilation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: During lobectomy on OLV, SpO2 falls despite FiO2 1.0. The surgeon is compressing the dependent lung intermittently.
constraints: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction may be impaired

options.text: Confirm oxygen delivery, FiO2 1.0, circuit function, and hemodynamics.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Confirm oxygen delivery, FiO2 1.0, circuit function, and hemodynamics. This is placed here because oLV hypoxemia is managed by restoring dependent-lung ventilation and reducing shunt while coordinating with surgery.

options.text: Use bronchoscopy to confirm lung-isolation device position and clear secretions.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Use bronchoscopy to confirm lung-isolation device position and clear secretions. This is placed here because oLV hypoxemia is managed by restoring dependent-lung ventilation and reducing shunt while coordinating with surgery.

options.text: Recruit dependent lung and apply individualized PEEP while avoiding overdistension.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Recruit dependent lung and apply individualized PEEP while avoiding overdistension. This is placed here because oLV hypoxemia is managed by restoring dependent-lung ventilation and reducing shunt while coordinating with surgery.

options.text: Ask surgeon to stop dependent lung compression and consider intermittent two-lung ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Ask surgeon to stop dependent lung compression and consider intermittent two-lung ventilation. This is placed here because oLV hypoxemia is managed by restoring dependent-lung ventilation and reducing shunt while coordinating with surgery.

options.text: Apply CPAP or oxygen insufflation to nondependent lung if surgical field allows.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Apply CPAP or oxygen insufflation to nondependent lung if surgical field allows. This is placed here because oLV hypoxemia is managed by restoring dependent-lung ventilation and reducing shunt while coordinating with surgery.

options.text: Consider abandoning OLV or postoperative ventilation if hypoxemia remains unsafe.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Consider abandoning OLV or postoperative ventilation if hypoxemia remains unsafe. This is placed here because oLV hypoxemia is managed by restoring dependent-lung ventilation and reducing shunt while coordinating with surgery.

management_logic: OLV hypoxemia is managed by restoring dependent-lung ventilation and reducing shunt while coordinating with surgery.
learning_point: During OLV, the dependent lung is the life-support lung.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Airway edema after prolonged prone spine surgery
topic: Airway edema after prolonged prone spine surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After 8 hours prone spine surgery with large fluids, the patient has facial edema and absent cuff leak.

options.text: Recognize high-risk extubation and postpone routine extubation.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Recognize high-risk extubation and postpone routine extubation. This is placed here because the safe decision may be delayed extubation when edema and reintubation risk are high.

options.text: Assess airway edema, cuff leak, fluid balance, blood loss, and neurologic need for examination.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Assess airway edema, cuff leak, fluid balance, blood loss, and neurologic need for examination. This is placed here because the safe decision may be delayed extubation when edema and reintubation risk are high.

options.text: Discuss planned postoperative ventilation versus staged extubation with the surgical team.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Discuss planned postoperative ventilation versus staged extubation with the surgical team. This is placed here because the safe decision may be delayed extubation when edema and reintubation risk are high.

options.text: If extubating later, prepare airway exchange catheter, expert help, and reintubation plan.
options.rationale: Priority 4. If extubating later, prepare airway exchange catheter, expert help, and reintubation plan. This is placed here because the safe decision may be delayed extubation when edema and reintubation risk are high.

options.text: Maintain head elevation, diuresis or fluid optimization as clinically appropriate.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Maintain head elevation, diuresis or fluid optimization as clinically appropriate. This is placed here because the safe decision may be delayed extubation when edema and reintubation risk are high.

options.text: Handover airway risk clearly to PACU or ICU.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Handover airway risk clearly to PACU or ICU. This is placed here because the safe decision may be delayed extubation when edema and reintubation risk are high.

management_logic: The safe decision may be delayed extubation when edema and reintubation risk are high.
learning_point: Extubation after prolonged prone surgery is a planned event, not the automatic end of anesthesia.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Cardiovascular collapse in anterior mediastinal mass
topic: Cardiovascular collapse in anterior mediastinal mass
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A young adult with anterior mediastinal mass collapses after induction. Ventilation is difficult and BP is unrecordable.

options.text: Call for help and stop further anesthetic deepening.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and stop further anesthetic deepening. This is placed here because loss of tone and supine positioning can convert mediastinal compression into airway and vascular collapse.

options.text: Return to the position of best preoperative breathing, often lateral or sitting.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Return to the position of best preoperative breathing, often lateral or sitting. This is placed here because loss of tone and supine positioning can convert mediastinal compression into airway and vascular collapse.

options.text: Ventilate with 100% oxygen while restoring spontaneous ventilation if possible.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ventilate with 100% oxygen while restoring spontaneous ventilation if possible. This is placed here because loss of tone and supine positioning can convert mediastinal compression into airway and vascular collapse.

options.text: Treat hypotension with vasopressors and fluid while avoiding delay in relieving compression.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat hypotension with vasopressors and fluid while avoiding delay in relieving compression. This is placed here because loss of tone and supine positioning can convert mediastinal compression into airway and vascular collapse.

options.text: Ask surgeon to prepare emergent decompression or rigid bronchoscopy; activate ECMO/CPB backup if available.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Ask surgeon to prepare emergent decompression or rigid bronchoscopy; activate ECMO/CPB backup if available. This is placed here because loss of tone and supine positioning can convert mediastinal compression into airway and vascular collapse.

options.text: After stabilization, abandon elective procedure or proceed only with a revised high-risk plan.
options.rationale: Priority 6. After stabilization, abandon elective procedure or proceed only with a revised high-risk plan. This is placed here because loss of tone and supine positioning can convert mediastinal compression into airway and vascular collapse.

management_logic: Loss of tone and supine positioning can convert mediastinal compression into airway and vascular collapse.
learning_point: In mediastinal mass collapse, position is a resuscitation drug.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Child with inhaled foreign body for bronchoscopy
topic: Child with inhaled foreign body for bronchoscopy
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A 3-year-old with suspected peanut aspiration has wheeze and air trapping. Rigid bronchoscopy is planned.

options.text: Call experienced pediatric anesthesia and ENT team with rigid bronchoscopy ready.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call experienced pediatric anesthesia and ENT team with rigid bronchoscopy ready. This is placed here because the foreign body and shared airway make controlled spontaneous ventilation the safer initial strategy.

options.text: Maintain spontaneous ventilation during induction until airway control strategy is secured.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Maintain spontaneous ventilation during induction until airway control strategy is secured. This is placed here because the foreign body and shared airway make controlled spontaneous ventilation the safer initial strategy.

options.text: Avoid positive-pressure ventilation that may push the foreign body distally unless oxygenation fails.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Avoid positive-pressure ventilation that may push the foreign body distally unless oxygenation fails. This is placed here because the foreign body and shared airway make controlled spontaneous ventilation the safer initial strategy.

options.text: Provide topicalization and carefully titrated anesthetic depth for bronchoscopy.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Provide topicalization and carefully titrated anesthetic depth for bronchoscopy. This is placed here because the foreign body and shared airway make controlled spontaneous ventilation the safer initial strategy.

options.text: Treat complete obstruction with immediate rigid bronchoscopy, oxygenation, and surgical extraction.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Treat complete obstruction with immediate rigid bronchoscopy, oxygenation, and surgical extraction. This is placed here because the foreign body and shared airway make controlled spontaneous ventilation the safer initial strategy.

options.text: Observe postoperatively for edema, pneumothorax, atelectasis, and infection.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Observe postoperatively for edema, pneumothorax, atelectasis, and infection. This is placed here because the foreign body and shared airway make controlled spontaneous ventilation the safer initial strategy.

management_logic: The foreign body and shared airway make controlled spontaneous ventilation the safer initial strategy.
learning_point: In pediatric foreign body aspiration, the airway is both the problem and the surgical field.
evidence_basis: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Severe epistaxis during nasal intubation
topic: Severe epistaxis during nasal intubation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: During nasal intubation for mandibular surgery, brisk epistaxis obscures the airway and blood enters the pharynx.
constraints: Surgical access needs nasal tube but oxygenation comes first

options.text: Stop nasal attempts and suction the oropharynx aggressively.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop nasal attempts and suction the oropharynx aggressively. This is placed here because bleeding converts a planned nasal intubation into an oxygenation and aspiration problem.

options.text: Restore oxygenation with mask ventilation or oral airway device if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Restore oxygenation with mask ventilation or oral airway device if needed. This is placed here because bleeding converts a planned nasal intubation into an oxygenation and aspiration problem.

options.text: Secure the airway orally with cuffed tube if nasal route is unsafe.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Secure the airway orally with cuffed tube if nasal route is unsafe. This is placed here because bleeding converts a planned nasal intubation into an oxygenation and aspiration problem.

options.text: Pack or vasoconstrict the bleeding nostril and reassess hemodynamics.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Pack or vasoconstrict the bleeding nostril and reassess hemodynamics. This is placed here because bleeding converts a planned nasal intubation into an oxygenation and aspiration problem.

options.text: Decide with surgeon whether to convert to oral tube, submental route, or tracheostomy.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Decide with surgeon whether to convert to oral tube, submental route, or tracheostomy. This is placed here because bleeding converts a planned nasal intubation into an oxygenation and aspiration problem.

options.text: Document difficult nasal intubation and monitor for aspiration.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document difficult nasal intubation and monitor for aspiration. This is placed here because bleeding converts a planned nasal intubation into an oxygenation and aspiration problem.

management_logic: Bleeding converts a planned nasal intubation into an oxygenation and aspiration problem.
learning_point: When nasal intubation causes major bleeding, abandon the nose before the airway is lost.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Severe aortic stenosis hypotension after induction
topic: Severe aortic stenosis hypotension after induction
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A patient with severe aortic stenosis becomes hypotensive immediately after induction. The pulse is weak and ECG shows sinus rhythm.

options.text: Call for help and confirm rhythm, pulse, and arterial pressure accuracy.
