options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and confirm rhythm, pulse, and arterial pressure accuracy. This is placed here because severe AS patients need preload, sinus rhythm, and afterload; hypotension threatens coronary perfusion.

options.text: Restore afterload immediately with alpha-agonist vasopressor.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Restore afterload immediately with alpha-agonist vasopressor. This is placed here because severe AS patients need preload, sinus rhythm, and afterload; hypotension threatens coronary perfusion.

options.text: Optimize preload with cautious fluid bolus guided by response.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Optimize preload with cautious fluid bolus guided by response. This is placed here because severe AS patients need preload, sinus rhythm, and afterload; hypotension threatens coronary perfusion.

options.text: Maintain sinus rhythm and avoid tachycardia or bradycardia.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Maintain sinus rhythm and avoid tachycardia or bradycardia. This is placed here because severe AS patients need preload, sinus rhythm, and afterload; hypotension threatens coronary perfusion.

options.text: Reduce anesthetic depth if excessive and treat myocardial ischemia if present.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Reduce anesthetic depth if excessive and treat myocardial ischemia if present. This is placed here because severe AS patients need preload, sinus rhythm, and afterload; hypotension threatens coronary perfusion.

options.text: Use invasive monitoring and plan postoperative high-dependency or ICU care.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Use invasive monitoring and plan postoperative high-dependency or ICU care. This is placed here because severe AS patients need preload, sinus rhythm, and afterload; hypotension threatens coronary perfusion.

management_logic: Severe AS patients need preload, sinus rhythm, and afterload; hypotension threatens coronary perfusion.
learning_point: In severe AS, hypotension is ischemia until proved otherwise.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Acute pulmonary edema in severe mitral regurgitation
topic: Acute pulmonary edema in severe mitral regurgitation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A patient with severe MR develops acute pulmonary edema and hypoxemia during skin closure after fluid loading.

options.text: Increase FiO2, deepen anesthesia, and apply PEEP for oxygenation.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Increase FiO2, deepen anesthesia, and apply PEEP for oxygenation. This is placed here because mR worsens with high afterload; therapy prioritizes oxygenation and forward flow.

options.text: Reduce afterload and hypertension with titrated vasodilator therapy.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Reduce afterload and hypertension with titrated vasodilator therapy. This is placed here because mR worsens with high afterload; therapy prioritizes oxygenation and forward flow.

options.text: Support forward flow and avoid bradycardia.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Support forward flow and avoid bradycardia. This is placed here because mR worsens with high afterload; therapy prioritizes oxygenation and forward flow.

options.text: Stop unnecessary fluids and give diuretic if perfusion allows.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Stop unnecessary fluids and give diuretic if perfusion allows. This is placed here because mR worsens with high afterload; therapy prioritizes oxygenation and forward flow.

options.text: Use echocardiography or focused assessment if available to assess LV, MR, and volume status.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use echocardiography or focused assessment if available to assess LV, MR, and volume status. This is placed here because mR worsens with high afterload; therapy prioritizes oxygenation and forward flow.

options.text: Plan postoperative ventilation and ICU if oxygenation or hemodynamics remain unstable.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan postoperative ventilation and ICU if oxygenation or hemodynamics remain unstable. This is placed here because mR worsens with high afterload; therapy prioritizes oxygenation and forward flow.

management_logic: MR worsens with high afterload; therapy prioritizes oxygenation and forward flow.
learning_point: In severe MR pulmonary edema, afterload reduction can be more important than more diuretic.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Hypotension in hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy
topic: Hypotension in hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A patient with known HOCM becomes hypotensive and tachycardic after induction and vasodilation.
constraints: Tachycardia and low SVR worsen obstruction

options.text: Call for help and reduce anesthetic vasodilation.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and reduce anesthetic vasodilation. This is placed here because hOCM collapse is often dynamic obstruction worsened by tachycardia, low preload, and low afterload.

options.text: Increase preload with cautious fluid bolus.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Increase preload with cautious fluid bolus. This is placed here because hOCM collapse is often dynamic obstruction worsened by tachycardia, low preload, and low afterload.

options.text: Increase afterload with pure alpha-agonist vasopressor.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Increase afterload with pure alpha-agonist vasopressor. This is placed here because hOCM collapse is often dynamic obstruction worsened by tachycardia, low preload, and low afterload.

options.text: Slow heart rate with appropriate beta-blockade once pressure is supported.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Slow heart rate with appropriate beta-blockade once pressure is supported. This is placed here because hOCM collapse is often dynamic obstruction worsened by tachycardia, low preload, and low afterload.

options.text: Avoid inotropes and vasodilators unless another diagnosis is proven.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Avoid inotropes and vasodilators unless another diagnosis is proven. This is placed here because hOCM collapse is often dynamic obstruction worsened by tachycardia, low preload, and low afterload.

options.text: Use echocardiography if instability persists and plan monitored recovery.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Use echocardiography if instability persists and plan monitored recovery. This is placed here because hOCM collapse is often dynamic obstruction worsened by tachycardia, low preload, and low afterload.

management_logic: HOCM collapse is often dynamic obstruction worsened by tachycardia, low preload, and low afterload.
learning_point: In HOCM, treat hypotension by making the ventricle fuller, slower, and tighter.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Pulmonary hypertensive crisis after intubation
topic: Pulmonary hypertensive crisis after intubation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A patient with severe pulmonary hypertension develops sudden hypoxemia, systemic hypotension, and high CVP after intubation.

options.text: Call for help and treat as pulmonary hypertensive crisis with RV failure.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and treat as pulmonary hypertensive crisis with RV failure. This is placed here because the RV fails when PVR rises and systemic pressure falls; oxygenation, PVR reduction, and systemic perfusion must be restored together.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and correct hypoventilation, hypercarbia, acidosis, pain, and hypothermia.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and correct hypoventilation, hypercarbia, acidosis, pain, and hypothermia. This is placed here because the RV fails when PVR rises and systemic pressure falls; oxygenation, PVR reduction, and systemic perfusion must be restored together.

options.text: Support systemic pressure with vasopressor to maintain RV coronary perfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Support systemic pressure with vasopressor to maintain RV coronary perfusion. This is placed here because the RV fails when PVR rises and systemic pressure falls; oxygenation, PVR reduction, and systemic perfusion must be restored together.

options.text: Start selective pulmonary vasodilator if available, such as inhaled nitric oxide or prostacyclin.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Start selective pulmonary vasodilator if available, such as inhaled nitric oxide or prostacyclin. This is placed here because the RV fails when PVR rises and systemic pressure falls; oxygenation, PVR reduction, and systemic perfusion must be restored together.

options.text: Optimize preload carefully and use inotrope for RV contractility if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Optimize preload carefully and use inotrope for RV contractility if needed. This is placed here because the RV fails when PVR rises and systemic pressure falls; oxygenation, PVR reduction, and systemic perfusion must be restored together.

options.text: Transfer to ICU with PH rescue plan and avoid abrupt withdrawal of pulmonary vasodilators.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU with PH rescue plan and avoid abrupt withdrawal of pulmonary vasodilators. This is placed here because the RV fails when PVR rises and systemic pressure falls; oxygenation, PVR reduction, and systemic perfusion must be restored together.

management_logic: The RV fails when PVR rises and systemic pressure falls; oxygenation, PVR reduction, and systemic perfusion must be restored together.
learning_point: In pulmonary hypertension, hypoxia and hypotension are a lethal combination.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Acute right ventricular failure during liver resection
topic: Acute right ventricular failure during liver resection
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: During major liver resection, EtCO2 falls, CVP rises, and hypotension develops. TEE suggests dilated RV.

options.text: Call for help and increase FiO2 while confirming rhythm and perfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and increase FiO2 while confirming rhythm and perfusion. This is placed here because rV failure management focuses on PVR reduction, systemic pressure, contractility, and cause identification.

options.text: Reduce PVR by correcting hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, high airway pressures, and hypothermia.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Reduce PVR by correcting hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, high airway pressures, and hypothermia. This is placed here because rV failure management focuses on PVR reduction, systemic pressure, contractility, and cause identification.

options.text: Support systemic pressure with vasopressor to maintain RV perfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Support systemic pressure with vasopressor to maintain RV perfusion. This is placed here because rV failure management focuses on PVR reduction, systemic pressure, contractility, and cause identification.

options.text: Use inotrope or pulmonary vasodilator if RV contractility or PVR remains problematic.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Use inotrope or pulmonary vasodilator if RV contractility or PVR remains problematic. This is placed here because rV failure management focuses on PVR reduction, systemic pressure, contractility, and cause identification.

options.text: Assess for cause with TEE or POCUS: embolism, ischemia, tamponade, tension pneumothorax.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Assess for cause with TEE or POCUS: embolism, ischemia, tamponade, tension pneumothorax. This is placed here because rV failure management focuses on PVR reduction, systemic pressure, contractility, and cause identification.

options.text: Plan ICU transfer and continued hemodynamic monitoring.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan ICU transfer and continued hemodynamic monitoring. This is placed here because rV failure management focuses on PVR reduction, systemic pressure, contractility, and cause identification.

management_logic: RV failure management focuses on PVR reduction, systemic pressure, contractility, and cause identification.
learning_point: For RV failure, more fluid is not always more preload; it may be more distension.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Acute aortic dissection arrives for emergency surgery
topic: Acute aortic dissection arrives for emergency surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A patient with type A aortic dissection arrives hypertensive and agitated with tearing chest pain.

options.text: Call cardiac surgery, perfusion, blood bank, and invasive monitoring team.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call cardiac surgery, perfusion, blood bank, and invasive monitoring team. This is placed here because anti-impulse therapy prioritizes heart rate and shear reduction before isolated vasodilation.

options.text: Treat pain and agitation to reduce sympathetic drive.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Treat pain and agitation to reduce sympathetic drive. This is placed here because anti-impulse therapy prioritizes heart rate and shear reduction before isolated vasodilation.

options.text: Control heart rate first with titrated beta-blockade if no contraindication.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Control heart rate first with titrated beta-blockade if no contraindication. This is placed here because anti-impulse therapy prioritizes heart rate and shear reduction before isolated vasodilation.

options.text: Reduce systolic pressure after rate control using titrated vasodilator.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Reduce systolic pressure after rate control using titrated vasodilator. This is placed here because anti-impulse therapy prioritizes heart rate and shear reduction before isolated vasodilation.

options.text: Prepare for controlled induction avoiding hypertension, tachycardia, and hypotension.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Prepare for controlled induction avoiding hypertension, tachycardia, and hypotension. This is placed here because anti-impulse therapy prioritizes heart rate and shear reduction before isolated vasodilation.

options.text: Continue arterial pressure monitoring and communicate neurologic or malperfusion signs.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue arterial pressure monitoring and communicate neurologic or malperfusion signs. This is placed here because anti-impulse therapy prioritizes heart rate and shear reduction before isolated vasodilation.

management_logic: Anti-impulse therapy prioritizes heart rate and shear reduction before isolated vasodilation.
learning_point: In aortic dissection, pressure control without rate control can still tear the aorta.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Pheochromocytoma hypertensive crisis at tumor handling
topic: Pheochromocytoma hypertensive crisis at tumor handling
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: During adrenal tumor manipulation, BP rises to 260/140 mmHg with ventricular ectopy.

options.text: Tell surgeon to stop manipulation and call for help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Tell surgeon to stop manipulation and call for help. This is placed here because tumor manipulation causes catecholamine surge; alpha/vasodilator control precedes beta blockade.

options.text: Deepen anesthesia and ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Deepen anesthesia and ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation. This is placed here because tumor manipulation causes catecholamine surge; alpha/vasodilator control precedes beta blockade.

options.text: Treat hypertension with titrated short-acting vasodilator or alpha-blocking strategy.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Treat hypertension with titrated short-acting vasodilator or alpha-blocking strategy. This is placed here because tumor manipulation causes catecholamine surge; alpha/vasodilator control precedes beta blockade.

options.text: Treat tachyarrhythmia after alpha control with short-acting beta-blocker if appropriate.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat tachyarrhythmia after alpha control with short-acting beta-blocker if appropriate. This is placed here because tumor manipulation causes catecholamine surge; alpha/vasodilator control precedes beta blockade.

options.text: Prepare for post-ligation hypotension with fluids and vasopressor support.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Prepare for post-ligation hypotension with fluids and vasopressor support. This is placed here because tumor manipulation causes catecholamine surge; alpha/vasodilator control precedes beta blockade.

options.text: Continue invasive monitoring and ICU handover.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue invasive monitoring and ICU handover. This is placed here because tumor manipulation causes catecholamine surge; alpha/vasodilator control precedes beta blockade.

management_logic: Tumor manipulation causes catecholamine surge; alpha/vasodilator control precedes beta blockade.
learning_point: In pheochromocytoma, uncontrolled beta blockade before alpha control can worsen crisis.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Carcinoid crisis during tumor manipulation
topic: Carcinoid crisis during tumor manipulation
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: During manipulation of bowel tumor, the patient develops flushing, bronchospasm, and severe hypotension.

options.text: Call for help and tell surgeon to stop tumor manipulation.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and tell surgeon to stop tumor manipulation. This is placed here because carcinoid crisis requires interruption of stimulus, octreotide, and hemodynamic support.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and support ventilation for bronchospasm.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and support ventilation for bronchospasm. This is placed here because carcinoid crisis requires interruption of stimulus, octreotide, and hemodynamic support.

options.text: Administer octreotide bolus or infusion according to local protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Administer octreotide bolus or infusion according to local protocol. This is placed here because carcinoid crisis requires interruption of stimulus, octreotide, and hemodynamic support.

options.text: Treat hypotension with fluids and vasopressors while avoiding histamine-releasing drugs.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat hypotension with fluids and vasopressors while avoiding histamine-releasing drugs. This is placed here because carcinoid crisis requires interruption of stimulus, octreotide, and hemodynamic support.

options.text: Correct acidosis, hypothermia, and electrolyte abnormalities.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Correct acidosis, hypothermia, and electrolyte abnormalities. This is placed here because carcinoid crisis requires interruption of stimulus, octreotide, and hemodynamic support.

options.text: Continue postoperative monitoring for recurrent crisis.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue postoperative monitoring for recurrent crisis. This is placed here because carcinoid crisis requires interruption of stimulus, octreotide, and hemodynamic support.

management_logic: Carcinoid crisis requires interruption of stimulus, octreotide, and hemodynamic support.
learning_point: In carcinoid crisis, octreotide is source control for mediator physiology.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Refractory vasoplegia after ACE inhibitor
topic: Refractory vasoplegia after ACE inhibitor
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After induction, a patient who took ACE inhibitor this morning has severe hypotension poorly responsive to phenylephrine.

options.text: Confirm depth, rhythm, pulse, and exclude anaphylaxis or hemorrhage.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Confirm depth, rhythm, pulse, and exclude anaphylaxis or hemorrhage. This is placed here because aCE inhibitor-associated vasoplegia may respond poorly to pure alpha agonists and require vasopressin pathway support.

options.text: Reduce anesthetic dose and give fluid bolus guided by response.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Reduce anesthetic dose and give fluid bolus guided by response. This is placed here because aCE inhibitor-associated vasoplegia may respond poorly to pure alpha agonists and require vasopressin pathway support.

options.text: Use norepinephrine or vasopressor infusion for persistent vasodilation.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Use norepinephrine or vasopressor infusion for persistent vasodilation. This is placed here because aCE inhibitor-associated vasoplegia may respond poorly to pure alpha agonists and require vasopressin pathway support.

options.text: Consider vasopressin for catecholamine-resistant vasoplegia according to local protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Consider vasopressin for catecholamine-resistant vasoplegia according to local protocol. This is placed here because aCE inhibitor-associated vasoplegia may respond poorly to pure alpha agonists and require vasopressin pathway support.

options.text: Check acid-base, calcium, and temperature if shock persists.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Check acid-base, calcium, and temperature if shock persists. This is placed here because aCE inhibitor-associated vasoplegia may respond poorly to pure alpha agonists and require vasopressin pathway support.

options.text: Plan postoperative monitoring if high vasopressor need continues.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan postoperative monitoring if high vasopressor need continues. This is placed here because aCE inhibitor-associated vasoplegia may respond poorly to pure alpha agonists and require vasopressin pathway support.

management_logic: ACE inhibitor-associated vasoplegia may respond poorly to pure alpha agonists and require vasopressin pathway support.
learning_point: Ask about ACE inhibitor because it changes the rescue pressor ladder.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Adrenaline infiltration causes unstable tachyarrhythmia
topic: Adrenaline infiltration causes unstable tachyarrhythmia
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After local infiltration containing adrenaline, the ECG shows narrow-complex tachycardia at 190/min and BP falls.

options.text: Stop surgical stimulation and call for help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop surgical stimulation and call for help. This is placed here because unstable tachyarrhythmia is treated electrically; adrenaline trigger should be stopped but not allowed to delay cardioversion.

options.text: Assess pulse, blood pressure, consciousness surrogate, and signs of instability.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Assess pulse, blood pressure, consciousness surrogate, and signs of instability. This is placed here because unstable tachyarrhythmia is treated electrically; adrenaline trigger should be stopped but not allowed to delay cardioversion.

options.text: If unstable, perform synchronized cardioversion according to ACLS principles.
options.rationale: Priority 3. If unstable, perform synchronized cardioversion according to ACLS principles. This is placed here because unstable tachyarrhythmia is treated electrically; adrenaline trigger should be stopped but not allowed to delay cardioversion.

options.text: If stable enough, treat with vagal or pharmacologic strategy appropriate to rhythm.
options.rationale: Priority 4. If stable enough, treat with vagal or pharmacologic strategy appropriate to rhythm. This is placed here because unstable tachyarrhythmia is treated electrically; adrenaline trigger should be stopped but not allowed to delay cardioversion.

options.text: Correct hypoxia, hypercarbia, electrolyte disturbance, and anesthetic lightness.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Correct hypoxia, hypercarbia, electrolyte disturbance, and anesthetic lightness. This is placed here because unstable tachyarrhythmia is treated electrically; adrenaline trigger should be stopped but not allowed to delay cardioversion.

options.text: Document adrenaline dose and avoid further sympathomimetic infiltration.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document adrenaline dose and avoid further sympathomimetic infiltration. This is placed here because unstable tachyarrhythmia is treated electrically; adrenaline trigger should be stopped but not allowed to delay cardioversion.

management_logic: Unstable tachyarrhythmia is treated electrically; adrenaline trigger should be stopped but not allowed to delay cardioversion.
learning_point: In arrhythmia, instability decides the first treatment.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Auto-PEEP causing hypotension in COPD
topic: Auto-PEEP causing hypotension in COPD
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A COPD patient ventilated after induction becomes hypotensive. Capnogram shows prolonged expiration and breath stacking.

options.text: Disconnect briefly from ventilator if severe breath stacking and hypotension occur.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Disconnect briefly from ventilator if severe breath stacking and hypotension occur. This is placed here because dynamic hyperinflation can obstruct venous return and mimic shock; ventilator adjustment is the treatment.

options.text: Manually ventilate slowly with long expiratory time and 100% oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Manually ventilate slowly with long expiratory time and 100% oxygen. This is placed here because dynamic hyperinflation can obstruct venous return and mimic shock; ventilator adjustment is the treatment.

options.text: Reduce respiratory rate and tidal volume; increase expiratory time.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Reduce respiratory rate and tidal volume; increase expiratory time. This is placed here because dynamic hyperinflation can obstruct venous return and mimic shock; ventilator adjustment is the treatment.

options.text: Treat bronchospasm and secretions with bronchodilator and suction.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat bronchospasm and secretions with bronchodilator and suction. This is placed here because dynamic hyperinflation can obstruct venous return and mimic shock; ventilator adjustment is the treatment.

options.text: Use cautious fluids and vasopressor only after correcting dynamic hyperinflation.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use cautious fluids and vasopressor only after correcting dynamic hyperinflation. This is placed here because dynamic hyperinflation can obstruct venous return and mimic shock; ventilator adjustment is the treatment.

options.text: Accept permissive hypercapnia if pH and hemodynamics are tolerable.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Accept permissive hypercapnia if pH and hemodynamics are tolerable. This is placed here because dynamic hyperinflation can obstruct venous return and mimic shock; ventilator adjustment is the treatment.

management_logic: Dynamic hyperinflation can obstruct venous return and mimic shock; ventilator adjustment is the treatment.
learning_point: In obstructive lungs, hypotension may be solved by letting the patient exhale.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: ARDS patient needs emergency laparotomy
topic: ARDS patient needs emergency laparotomy
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A ventilated ARDS patient is brought for emergency laparotomy with high oxygen requirement and low compliance.

options.text: Confirm goals, code status, and senior ICU-anesthesia-surgery plan before transfer if time allows.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Confirm goals, code status, and senior ICU-anesthesia-surgery plan before transfer if time allows. This is placed here because aRDS anesthesia is continuation of ICU lung protection through a surgical stress.

options.text: Maintain lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volume and plateau pressure awareness.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Maintain lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volume and plateau pressure awareness. This is placed here because aRDS anesthesia is continuation of ICU lung protection through a surgical stress.

options.text: Avoid derecruitment during transfer, circuit changes, and induction transitions.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Avoid derecruitment during transfer, circuit changes, and induction transitions. This is placed here because aRDS anesthesia is continuation of ICU lung protection through a surgical stress.

options.text: Use PEEP and recruitment cautiously while monitoring blood pressure and RV strain.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Use PEEP and recruitment cautiously while monitoring blood pressure and RV strain. This is placed here because aRDS anesthesia is continuation of ICU lung protection through a surgical stress.

options.text: Treat shock, acidosis, hypothermia, and bleeding promptly during surgery.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Treat shock, acidosis, hypothermia, and bleeding promptly during surgery. This is placed here because aRDS anesthesia is continuation of ICU lung protection through a surgical stress.

options.text: Return to ICU with explicit ventilator settings, vasopressors, and rescue plan.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Return to ICU with explicit ventilator settings, vasopressors, and rescue plan. This is placed here because aRDS anesthesia is continuation of ICU lung protection through a surgical stress.

management_logic: ARDS anesthesia is continuation of ICU lung protection through a surgical stress.
learning_point: The dangerous moment in ARDS is every circuit disconnection.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Tension pneumothorax after subclavian line
topic: Tension pneumothorax after subclavian line
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After subclavian central line insertion under anesthesia, airway pressure rises and BP collapses. Breath sounds are absent on one side.

options.text: Call for help and increase FiO2 to 100%.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and increase FiO2 to 100%. This is placed here because tension pneumothorax under positive pressure is a clinical diagnosis; treatment should precede imaging.

options.text: Suspect tension pneumothorax from unilateral absent breath sounds with shock.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Suspect tension pneumothorax from unilateral absent breath sounds with shock. This is placed here because tension pneumothorax under positive pressure is a clinical diagnosis; treatment should precede imaging.

options.text: Perform immediate needle decompression or finger thoracostomy according to available expertise.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Perform immediate needle decompression or finger thoracostomy according to available expertise. This is placed here because tension pneumothorax under positive pressure is a clinical diagnosis; treatment should precede imaging.

options.text: Insert definitive chest drain after emergency decompression.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Insert definitive chest drain after emergency decompression. This is placed here because tension pneumothorax under positive pressure is a clinical diagnosis; treatment should precede imaging.

options.text: Confirm improvement in oxygenation, airway pressure, and blood pressure.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Confirm improvement in oxygenation, airway pressure, and blood pressure. This is placed here because tension pneumothorax under positive pressure is a clinical diagnosis; treatment should precede imaging.

options.text: Obtain imaging after stabilization and document line complication.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Obtain imaging after stabilization and document line complication. This is placed here because tension pneumothorax under positive pressure is a clinical diagnosis; treatment should precede imaging.

management_logic: Tension pneumothorax under positive pressure is a clinical diagnosis; treatment should precede imaging.
learning_point: Do not X-ray a dying tension pneumothorax.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Mucus plug after intubation
topic: Mucus plug after intubation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: Soon after intubation, SpO2 falls and one hemithorax has poor air entry. Suction catheter cannot pass easily.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and manually ventilate to assess compliance.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Give 100% oxygen and manually ventilate to assess compliance. This is placed here because tube obstruction is among the first checks in sudden high pressure and hypoxemia.

options.text: Check tube depth, kinking, biting, and circuit obstruction.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Check tube depth, kinking, biting, and circuit obstruction. This is placed here because tube obstruction is among the first checks in sudden high pressure and hypoxemia.

options.text: Pass suction catheter; if blocked, suction and consider tube exchange.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Pass suction catheter; if blocked, suction and consider tube exchange. This is placed here because tube obstruction is among the first checks in sudden high pressure and hypoxemia.

options.text: Use bronchoscopy if available to identify mucus plug or malposition.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Use bronchoscopy if available to identify mucus plug or malposition. This is placed here because tube obstruction is among the first checks in sudden high pressure and hypoxemia.

options.text: Recruit lung after obstruction relief and reassess gas exchange.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Recruit lung after obstruction relief and reassess gas exchange. This is placed here because tube obstruction is among the first checks in sudden high pressure and hypoxemia.

options.text: Plan humidification and secretion management postoperatively.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan humidification and secretion management postoperatively. This is placed here because tube obstruction is among the first checks in sudden high pressure and hypoxemia.

management_logic: Tube obstruction is among the first checks in sudden high pressure and hypoxemia.
learning_point: If the suction catheter will not pass, the airway may be blocked.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Endobronchial intubation after Trendelenburg positioning
topic: Endobronchial intubation after Trendelenburg positioning
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After steep Trendelenburg, airway pressure rises and SpO2 drops. Right-sided breath sounds dominate.
constraints: Robotic access limits airway access later

options.text: Increase FiO2 and manually ventilate while assessing chest movement.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Increase FiO2 and manually ventilate while assessing chest movement. This is placed here because position changes can push the tube into a main bronchus; simple depth correction may fix the crisis.

options.text: Check tube depth and auscultate both lungs.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Check tube depth and auscultate both lungs. This is placed here because position changes can push the tube into a main bronchus; simple depth correction may fix the crisis.

options.text: Withdraw endotracheal tube incrementally until bilateral ventilation returns.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Withdraw endotracheal tube incrementally until bilateral ventilation returns. This is placed here because position changes can push the tube into a main bronchus; simple depth correction may fix the crisis.

options.text: Confirm position with capnography and bronchoscopy if available.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Confirm position with capnography and bronchoscopy if available. This is placed here because position changes can push the tube into a main bronchus; simple depth correction may fix the crisis.

options.text: Secure tube firmly before docking or continuing robotic surgery.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Secure tube firmly before docking or continuing robotic surgery. This is placed here because position changes can push the tube into a main bronchus; simple depth correction may fix the crisis.

options.text: Reassess after any major position change.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Reassess after any major position change. This is placed here because position changes can push the tube into a main bronchus; simple depth correction may fix the crisis.

management_logic: Position changes can push the tube into a main bronchus; simple depth correction may fix the crisis.
learning_point: After positioning, re-check the tube before blaming the lung.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Aspiration detected in PACU
topic: Aspiration detected in PACU
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A postoperative patient vomits in PACU, coughs, desaturates, and develops coarse crepitations.

options.text: Turn lateral, suction mouth and pharynx, and call anesthesia help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Turn lateral, suction mouth and pharynx, and call anesthesia help. This is placed here because pACU aspiration management starts with positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway protection.

options.text: Give high-flow oxygen and assess airway protection and work of breathing.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give high-flow oxygen and assess airway protection and work of breathing. This is placed here because pACU aspiration management starts with positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway protection.

options.text: Intubate if hypoxemia, poor airway reflexes, or respiratory fatigue persists.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Intubate if hypoxemia, poor airway reflexes, or respiratory fatigue persists. This is placed here because pACU aspiration management starts with positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway protection.

options.text: Suction trachea after intubation if particulate aspiration is suspected.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Suction trachea after intubation if particulate aspiration is suspected. This is placed here because pACU aspiration management starts with positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway protection.

options.text: Obtain ABG and chest imaging after stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Obtain ABG and chest imaging after stabilization. This is placed here because pACU aspiration management starts with positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway protection.

options.text: Observe or admit depending on oxygen requirement and clinical course.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Observe or admit depending on oxygen requirement and clinical course. This is placed here because pACU aspiration management starts with positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway protection.

management_logic: PACU aspiration management starts with positioning, suction, oxygen, and airway protection.
learning_point: The first treatment for aspiration is not antibiotics; it is airway protection.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Negative pressure pulmonary edema after biting tube
topic: Negative pressure pulmonary edema after biting tube
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A young muscular patient bites the tube during emergence, obstructs, then develops pink frothy sputum and hypoxemia.

options.text: Relieve obstruction immediately with jaw thrust, bite block, suction, and positive pressure.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Relieve obstruction immediately with jaw thrust, bite block, suction, and positive pressure. This is placed here because nPPE follows forceful inspiration against obstruction; airway relief and positive pressure are central.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and apply CPAP or PEEP.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and apply CPAP or PEEP. This is placed here because nPPE follows forceful inspiration against obstruction; airway relief and positive pressure are central.

options.text: Reintubate if oxygenation or work of breathing is unsafe.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Reintubate if oxygenation or work of breathing is unsafe. This is placed here because nPPE follows forceful inspiration against obstruction; airway relief and positive pressure are central.

options.text: Exclude aspiration, bronchospasm, and cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Exclude aspiration, bronchospasm, and cardiogenic pulmonary edema. This is placed here because nPPE follows forceful inspiration against obstruction; airway relief and positive pressure are central.

options.text: Use diuretic only if volume overload is likely; prioritize PEEP and oxygenation.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use diuretic only if volume overload is likely; prioritize PEEP and oxygenation. This is placed here because nPPE follows forceful inspiration against obstruction; airway relief and positive pressure are central.

options.text: Monitor until oxygen requirement and edema resolve.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor until oxygen requirement and edema resolve. This is placed here because nPPE follows forceful inspiration against obstruction; airway relief and positive pressure are central.

management_logic: NPPE follows forceful inspiration against obstruction; airway relief and positive pressure are central.
learning_point: Negative pressure pulmonary edema is an airway complication before it is a fluid problem.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: OSA patient with opioid-induced hypoventilation
topic: OSA patient with opioid-induced hypoventilation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A patient with severe OSA receives repeated morphine and is found drowsy with snoring, shallow breathing, and desaturation.

options.text: Call for help, stimulate patient, open airway, and give oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help, stimulate patient, open airway, and give oxygen. This is placed here because oSA plus opioids causes both obstruction and central hypoventilation; airway support and titrated reversal are priorities.

options.text: Support ventilation with bag-mask or noninvasive ventilation if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Support ventilation with bag-mask or noninvasive ventilation if needed. This is placed here because oSA plus opioids causes both obstruction and central hypoventilation; airway support and titrated reversal are priorities.

options.text: Give titrated naloxone if opioid-induced respiratory depression is significant.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give titrated naloxone if opioid-induced respiratory depression is significant. This is placed here because oSA plus opioids causes both obstruction and central hypoventilation; airway support and titrated reversal are priorities.

options.text: Stop further opioids and switch to monitored multimodal analgesia.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Stop further opioids and switch to monitored multimodal analgesia. This is placed here because oSA plus opioids causes both obstruction and central hypoventilation; airway support and titrated reversal are priorities.

options.text: Check ABG or capnography and monitor for renarcotization.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Check ABG or capnography and monitor for renarcotization. This is placed here because oSA plus opioids causes both obstruction and central hypoventilation; airway support and titrated reversal are priorities.

options.text: Escalate to HDU/ICU if repeated naloxone or ventilatory support is required.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Escalate to HDU/ICU if repeated naloxone or ventilatory support is required. This is placed here because oSA plus opioids causes both obstruction and central hypoventilation; airway support and titrated reversal are priorities.

management_logic: OSA plus opioids causes both obstruction and central hypoventilation; airway support and titrated reversal are priorities.
learning_point: Treat the breathing problem, then redesign the analgesia.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Suspected spinal epidural hematoma after neuraxial block
topic: Suspected spinal epidural hematoma after neuraxial block
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: A patient on anticoagulation develops new leg weakness and back pain after epidural analgesia.

options.text: Recognize epidural hematoma as a neurosurgical emergency and stop epidural infusion.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Recognize epidural hematoma as a neurosurgical emergency and stop epidural infusion. This is placed here because neurologic deficit after neuraxial block requires rapid diagnosis and decompression planning.

options.text: Perform focused neurologic examination and document motor, sensory, and bladder findings.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Perform focused neurologic examination and document motor, sensory, and bladder findings. This is placed here because neurologic deficit after neuraxial block requires rapid diagnosis and decompression planning.

options.text: Call anesthesia consultant, neurosurgery, and radiology immediately.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Call anesthesia consultant, neurosurgery, and radiology immediately. This is placed here because neurologic deficit after neuraxial block requires rapid diagnosis and decompression planning.

options.text: Arrange urgent MRI without delaying escalation.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Arrange urgent MRI without delaying escalation. This is placed here because neurologic deficit after neuraxial block requires rapid diagnosis and decompression planning.

options.text: Reverse anticoagulation or correct coagulopathy according to protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Reverse anticoagulation or correct coagulopathy according to protocol. This is placed here because neurologic deficit after neuraxial block requires rapid diagnosis and decompression planning.

options.text: Proceed to decompression if confirmed or strongly suspected within the therapeutic window.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Proceed to decompression if confirmed or strongly suspected within the therapeutic window. This is placed here because neurologic deficit after neuraxial block requires rapid diagnosis and decompression planning.

management_logic: Neurologic deficit after neuraxial block requires rapid diagnosis and decompression planning.
learning_point: Back pain plus motor weakness after epidural is hematoma until proved otherwise.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: Unexpected dense motor block from labor epidural
topic: Unexpected dense motor block from labor epidural
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: A laboring woman with epidural analgesia develops dense bilateral motor block and rising sensory level after top-up.

options.text: Stop epidural dosing and call obstetric anesthesia help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop epidural dosing and call obstetric anesthesia help. This is placed here because unexpected dense block after epidural dosing suggests intrathecal or subdural spread.

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, blood pressure, block height, and fetal status.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Assess airway, breathing, blood pressure, block height, and fetal status. This is placed here because unexpected dense block after epidural dosing suggests intrathecal or subdural spread.

options.text: Treat hypotension with left uterine displacement, vasopressor, and fluid as needed.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Treat hypotension with left uterine displacement, vasopressor, and fluid as needed. This is placed here because unexpected dense block after epidural dosing suggests intrathecal or subdural spread.

options.text: Prepare for high spinal airway support if block ascends.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Prepare for high spinal airway support if block ascends. This is placed here because unexpected dense block after epidural dosing suggests intrathecal or subdural spread.

options.text: Label catheter unsafe until position is clarified; do not give further epidural boluses.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Label catheter unsafe until position is clarified; do not give further epidural boluses. This is placed here because unexpected dense block after epidural dosing suggests intrathecal or subdural spread.

options.text: Plan delivery and postoperative monitoring based on maternal and fetal condition.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan delivery and postoperative monitoring based on maternal and fetal condition. This is placed here because unexpected dense block after epidural dosing suggests intrathecal or subdural spread.

management_logic: Unexpected dense block after epidural dosing suggests intrathecal or subdural spread.
learning_point: A labor epidural that suddenly behaves like a spinal should be treated as one.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: Accidental dural puncture during labor epidural
topic: Accidental dural puncture during labor epidural
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: Clear CSF flows through the Tuohy needle during attempted labor epidural.

options.text: Stop advancing and acknowledge accidental dural puncture.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop advancing and acknowledge accidental dural puncture. This is placed here because recognized dural puncture needs senior plan, clear communication, and dosing safety.

options.text: Call senior help and explain the event calmly to the patient.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Call senior help and explain the event calmly to the patient. This is placed here because recognized dural puncture needs senior plan, clear communication, and dosing safety.

options.text: Decide on intrathecal catheter placement versus resiting epidural according to local policy.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Decide on intrathecal catheter placement versus resiting epidural according to local policy. This is placed here because recognized dural puncture needs senior plan, clear communication, and dosing safety.

options.text: Label any intrathecal catheter clearly and use strict dosing safeguards.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Label any intrathecal catheter clearly and use strict dosing safeguards. This is placed here because recognized dural puncture needs senior plan, clear communication, and dosing safety.

options.text: Arrange follow-up for headache, neurologic symptoms, and postpartum review.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Arrange follow-up for headache, neurologic symptoms, and postpartum review. This is placed here because recognized dural puncture needs senior plan, clear communication, and dosing safety.

options.text: Document the event and counseling fully.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document the event and counseling fully. This is placed here because recognized dural puncture needs senior plan, clear communication, and dosing safety.

management_logic: Recognized dural puncture needs senior plan, clear communication, and dosing safety.
learning_point: After dural puncture, the next danger is wrong-route dosing.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: LAST after TAP block in frail patient
topic: LAST after TAP block in frail patient
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: A frail elderly patient becomes confused and then has ventricular ectopy after bilateral TAP block with large-volume local anesthetic.

options.text: Stop local anesthetic administration and call for LAST protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop local anesthetic administration and call for LAST protocol. This is placed here because fascial plane blocks can cause systemic toxicity, especially with high total dose or frailty.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and secure airway support to prevent acidosis and hypoxia.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and secure airway support to prevent acidosis and hypoxia. This is placed here because fascial plane blocks can cause systemic toxicity, especially with high total dose or frailty.

options.text: Start lipid emulsion therapy according to institutional protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Start lipid emulsion therapy according to institutional protocol. This is placed here because fascial plane blocks can cause systemic toxicity, especially with high total dose or frailty.

options.text: Treat arrhythmias with LAST-modified resuscitation and avoid contraindicated drugs.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat arrhythmias with LAST-modified resuscitation and avoid contraindicated drugs. This is placed here because fascial plane blocks can cause systemic toxicity, especially with high total dose or frailty.

options.text: Check dose, timing, weight, renal/hepatic status, and co-administered drugs.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Check dose, timing, weight, renal/hepatic status, and co-administered drugs. This is placed here because fascial plane blocks can cause systemic toxicity, especially with high total dose or frailty.

options.text: Monitor for recurrence for an extended period.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor for recurrence for an extended period. This is placed here because fascial plane blocks can cause systemic toxicity, especially with high total dose or frailty.

management_logic: Fascial plane blocks can cause systemic toxicity, especially with high total dose or frailty.
learning_point: LAST is not limited to brachial plexus blocks.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: Failed spinal after multiple attempts
topic: Failed spinal after multiple attempts
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: Spinal anesthesia fails after multiple traumatic attempts for hip surgery. The patient is anxious and in pain.

options.text: Stop repeated attempts and reassess the plan.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop repeated attempts and reassess the plan. This is placed here because repeated attempts increase trauma and patient distress; a failed technique should be abandoned early.

options.text: Call senior help and evaluate anatomy, anticoagulation, and patient comfort.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Call senior help and evaluate anatomy, anticoagulation, and patient comfort. This is placed here because repeated attempts increase trauma and patient distress; a failed technique should be abandoned early.

options.text: Offer alternative technique: ultrasound-assisted neuraxial, peripheral block, or general anesthesia.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Offer alternative technique: ultrasound-assisted neuraxial, peripheral block, or general anesthesia. This is placed here because repeated attempts increase trauma and patient distress; a failed technique should be abandoned early.

options.text: Treat pain and anxiety before repositioning.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat pain and anxiety before repositioning. This is placed here because repeated attempts increase trauma and patient distress; a failed technique should be abandoned early.

options.text: Document attempts, paresthesia, bleeding, and neurologic baseline.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Document attempts, paresthesia, bleeding, and neurologic baseline. This is placed here because repeated attempts increase trauma and patient distress; a failed technique should be abandoned early.

options.text: Postoperatively review for back pain or neurologic symptoms.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Postoperatively review for back pain or neurologic symptoms. This is placed here because repeated attempts increase trauma and patient distress; a failed technique should be abandoned early.

management_logic: Repeated attempts increase trauma and patient distress; a failed technique should be abandoned early.
learning_point: Persistence is not skill when the neuraxis is becoming injured.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: Neuraxial request with severe thrombocytopenia
topic: Neuraxial request with severe thrombocytopenia
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: A patient with platelets 28,000/µL requests spinal anesthesia for lower limb surgery.

options.text: Decline neuraxial block until coagulation risk is acceptably corrected.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Decline neuraxial block until coagulation risk is acceptably corrected. This is placed here because severe thrombocytopenia makes neuraxial bleeding risk unacceptable unless corrected in a justified plan.

options.text: Explain risk of spinal hematoma and discuss alternatives.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Explain risk of spinal hematoma and discuss alternatives. This is placed here because severe thrombocytopenia makes neuraxial bleeding risk unacceptable unless corrected in a justified plan.

options.text: Check trend, cause of thrombocytopenia, bleeding history, and other coagulation tests.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Check trend, cause of thrombocytopenia, bleeding history, and other coagulation tests. This is placed here because severe thrombocytopenia makes neuraxial bleeding risk unacceptable unless corrected in a justified plan.

options.text: Discuss platelet transfusion or surgical delay if neuraxial anesthesia is essential.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Discuss platelet transfusion or surgical delay if neuraxial anesthesia is essential. This is placed here because severe thrombocytopenia makes neuraxial bleeding risk unacceptable unless corrected in a justified plan.

options.text: Choose general or peripheral technique with risk mitigation if surgery proceeds.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Choose general or peripheral technique with risk mitigation if surgery proceeds. This is placed here because severe thrombocytopenia makes neuraxial bleeding risk unacceptable unless corrected in a justified plan.

options.text: Document shared decision and hematology or surgical input.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document shared decision and hematology or surgical input. This is placed here because severe thrombocytopenia makes neuraxial bleeding risk unacceptable unless corrected in a justified plan.

management_logic: Severe thrombocytopenia makes neuraxial bleeding risk unacceptable unless corrected in a justified plan.
learning_point: A normal INR does not make a platelet count of 28,000 safe for spinal.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: Post-block neurologic complaint
topic: Post-block neurologic complaint
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: After interscalene block, the patient reports persistent hand weakness and numbness beyond expected duration.
constraints: Need distinguish expected block from injury

options.text: Assess and document focused neurologic findings immediately.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Assess and document focused neurologic findings immediately. This is placed here because post-block deficits require structured evaluation rather than reassurance alone.

options.text: Review block type, local anesthetic dose, needle events, paresthesia, and surgical factors.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Review block type, local anesthetic dose, needle events, paresthesia, and surgical factors. This is placed here because post-block deficits require structured evaluation rather than reassurance alone.

options.text: Check for compressive causes: tight dressing, hematoma, positioning injury, compartment syndrome.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Check for compressive causes: tight dressing, hematoma, positioning injury, compartment syndrome. This is placed here because post-block deficits require structured evaluation rather than reassurance alone.

options.text: Inform anesthesia consultant and surgeon early.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Inform anesthesia consultant and surgeon early. This is placed here because post-block deficits require structured evaluation rather than reassurance alone.

options.text: Arrange follow-up, neurology imaging if progressive or severe deficit.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Arrange follow-up, neurology imaging if progressive or severe deficit. This is placed here because post-block deficits require structured evaluation rather than reassurance alone.

options.text: Provide clear patient explanation and safety-net instructions.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Provide clear patient explanation and safety-net instructions. This is placed here because post-block deficits require structured evaluation rather than reassurance alone.

management_logic: Post-block deficits require structured evaluation rather than reassurance alone.

title: Eclamptic seizure in operating room
topic: Eclamptic seizure in operating room
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A preeclamptic patient awaiting cesarean has a generalized seizure in the OR.

options.text: Call obstetric, anesthesia, neonatal, and nursing help; protect patient from injury.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call obstetric, anesthesia, neonatal, and nursing help; protect patient from injury. This is placed here because eclampsia management prioritizes maternal airway, magnesium, and BP control before delivery.

options.text: Turn lateral, suction, give high-flow oxygen, and support airway after seizure.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Turn lateral, suction, give high-flow oxygen, and support airway after seizure. This is placed here because eclampsia management prioritizes maternal airway, magnesium, and BP control before delivery.

options.text: Administer magnesium sulfate according to eclampsia protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Administer magnesium sulfate according to eclampsia protocol. This is placed here because eclampsia management prioritizes maternal airway, magnesium, and BP control before delivery.

options.text: Treat severe hypertension with pregnancy-appropriate antihypertensive therapy.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat severe hypertension with pregnancy-appropriate antihypertensive therapy. This is placed here because eclampsia management prioritizes maternal airway, magnesium, and BP control before delivery.

options.text: Assess maternal status, labs, fetal status, and aspiration risk after stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Assess maternal status, labs, fetal status, and aspiration risk after stabilization. This is placed here because eclampsia management prioritizes maternal airway, magnesium, and BP control before delivery.

options.text: Proceed to delivery once the mother is stabilized and team is ready.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Proceed to delivery once the mother is stabilized and team is ready. This is placed here because eclampsia management prioritizes maternal airway, magnesium, and BP control before delivery.

management_logic: Eclampsia management prioritizes maternal airway, magnesium, and BP control before delivery.
learning_point: In eclampsia, fetal resuscitation starts with maternal resuscitation.
evidence_basis: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Failed spinal for emergency cesarean delivery
topic: Failed spinal for emergency cesarean delivery
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: After spinal anesthesia for emergency cesarean, the block is inadequate and incision pain occurs.

options.text: Ask surgeon to stop stimulation and assess block level quickly.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Ask surgeon to stop stimulation and assess block level quickly. This is placed here because severe pain during cesarean is an anesthesia failure that requires decisive correction.

options.text: Communicate with patient and give supplemental analgesia only if safe and likely adequate.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Communicate with patient and give supplemental analgesia only if safe and likely adequate. This is placed here because severe pain during cesarean is an anesthesia failure that requires decisive correction.

options.text: Decide rapidly whether to convert to general anesthesia based on urgency and pain.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Decide rapidly whether to convert to general anesthesia based on urgency and pain. This is placed here because severe pain during cesarean is an anesthesia failure that requires decisive correction.

options.text: If converting, perform obstetric RSI with difficult airway and aspiration plan ready.
options.rationale: Priority 4. If converting, perform obstetric RSI with difficult airway and aspiration plan ready. This is placed here because severe pain during cesarean is an anesthesia failure that requires decisive correction.

options.text: Ensure neonatal team is prepared for delivery after maternal anesthesia is safe.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Ensure neonatal team is prepared for delivery after maternal anesthesia is safe. This is placed here because severe pain during cesarean is an anesthesia failure that requires decisive correction.

options.text: Document failed block, conversion reason, and patient counseling.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document failed block, conversion reason, and patient counseling. This is placed here because severe pain during cesarean is an anesthesia failure that requires decisive correction.

management_logic: Severe pain during cesarean is an anesthesia failure that requires decisive correction.
learning_point: Do not let a patient endure surgery under an inadequate spinal.
evidence_basis: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Amniotic fluid embolism during cesarean
topic: Amniotic fluid embolism during cesarean
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: During cesarean delivery, the patient develops sudden hypoxia, hypotension, coagulopathy, and cardiac arrest physiology.

options.text: Call massive obstetric emergency, start ACLS with left uterine displacement if undelivered.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call massive obstetric emergency, start ACLS with left uterine displacement if undelivered. This is placed here because aFE is supportive resuscitation: oxygen, CPR, delivery when indicated, and aggressive coagulopathy management.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and secure airway or confirm ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and secure airway or confirm ventilation. This is placed here because aFE is supportive resuscitation: oxygen, CPR, delivery when indicated, and aggressive coagulopathy management.

options.text: Perform high-quality CPR and consider perimortem delivery if maternal arrest before delivery.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Perform high-quality CPR and consider perimortem delivery if maternal arrest before delivery. This is placed here because aFE is supportive resuscitation: oxygen, CPR, delivery when indicated, and aggressive coagulopathy management.

options.text: Activate massive transfusion and correct coagulopathy including fibrinogen.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Activate massive transfusion and correct coagulopathy including fibrinogen. This is placed here because aFE is supportive resuscitation: oxygen, CPR, delivery when indicated, and aggressive coagulopathy management.

options.text: Support RV/LV failure with vasopressors, inotropes, and echo-guided care if available.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Support RV/LV failure with vasopressors, inotropes, and echo-guided care if available. This is placed here because aFE is supportive resuscitation: oxygen, CPR, delivery when indicated, and aggressive coagulopathy management.

options.text: Transfer to ICU after ROSC with ongoing hemorrhage and organ support plan.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU after ROSC with ongoing hemorrhage and organ support plan. This is placed here because aFE is supportive resuscitation: oxygen, CPR, delivery when indicated, and aggressive coagulopathy management.

management_logic: AFE is supportive resuscitation: oxygen, CPR, delivery when indicated, and aggressive coagulopathy management.
learning_point: AFE is diagnosed clinically; treatment cannot wait for confirmation.
evidence_basis: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Uterine inversion with shock
topic: Uterine inversion with shock
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: After delivery, the patient has severe pain, hemorrhage, and profound vagal shock from uterine inversion.

options.text: Call obstetric emergency and resuscitation help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call obstetric emergency and resuscitation help. This is placed here because uterine replacement precedes uterotonics because contraction before replacement traps the inversion.

options.text: Start oxygen, large-bore IV access, fluids, and blood preparation.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Start oxygen, large-bore IV access, fluids, and blood preparation. This is placed here because uterine replacement precedes uterotonics because contraction before replacement traps the inversion.

options.text: Ask obstetrician for immediate uterine replacement before uterotonics.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ask obstetrician for immediate uterine replacement before uterotonics. This is placed here because uterine replacement precedes uterotonics because contraction before replacement traps the inversion.

options.text: Provide uterine relaxation if required for replacement while supporting BP.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Provide uterine relaxation if required for replacement while supporting BP. This is placed here because uterine replacement precedes uterotonics because contraction before replacement traps the inversion.

options.text: After replacement, give uterotonics and manage hemorrhage.
options.rationale: Priority 5. After replacement, give uterotonics and manage hemorrhage. This is placed here because uterine replacement precedes uterotonics because contraction before replacement traps the inversion.

options.text: Monitor for recurrent inversion, anemia, and coagulopathy.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor for recurrent inversion, anemia, and coagulopathy. This is placed here because uterine replacement precedes uterotonics because contraction before replacement traps the inversion.

management_logic: Uterine replacement precedes uterotonics because contraction before replacement traps the inversion.
learning_point: In uterine inversion, put the uterus back before making it contract.
evidence_basis: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Magnesium toxicity in preeclampsia
topic: Magnesium toxicity in preeclampsia
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A preeclamptic patient on magnesium becomes drowsy with absent reflexes and shallow breathing.

options.text: Stop magnesium infusion and call for help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop magnesium infusion and call for help. This is placed here because magnesium toxicity causes loss of reflexes and respiratory depression; calcium is the antidote.

options.text: Support airway and ventilation with oxygen and bag-mask if required.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Support airway and ventilation with oxygen and bag-mask if required. This is placed here because magnesium toxicity causes loss of reflexes and respiratory depression; calcium is the antidote.

options.text: Give IV calcium gluconate according to protocol for significant toxicity.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give IV calcium gluconate according to protocol for significant toxicity. This is placed here because magnesium toxicity causes loss of reflexes and respiratory depression; calcium is the antidote.

options.text: Check magnesium level, renal function, urine output, ABG, and electrolytes.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Check magnesium level, renal function, urine output, ABG, and electrolytes. This is placed here because magnesium toxicity causes loss of reflexes and respiratory depression; calcium is the antidote.

options.text: Continue seizure prophylaxis plan with obstetric input after stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Continue seizure prophylaxis plan with obstetric input after stabilization. This is placed here because magnesium toxicity causes loss of reflexes and respiratory depression; calcium is the antidote.

options.text: Monitor respiratory function and reflexes until recovery.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor respiratory function and reflexes until recovery. This is placed here because magnesium toxicity causes loss of reflexes and respiratory depression; calcium is the antidote.

management_logic: Magnesium toxicity causes loss of reflexes and respiratory depression; calcium is the antidote.
learning_point: Absent reflexes during magnesium therapy are a warning sign, not a normal side effect.
evidence_basis: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Failed intubation in obstetric general anesthesia
topic: Failed intubation in obstetric general anesthesia
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: During category-1 cesarean under GA, intubation fails and mask ventilation is difficult but possible.

options.text: Call failed obstetric airway and maintain oxygenation with two-person mask ventilation.

options.text: Release cricoid if it impairs ventilation or rescue airway insertion.

options.text: Insert second-generation supraglottic airway if mask ventilation is inadequate.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Insert second-generation supraglottic airway if mask ventilation is inadequate. This is placed here because the mother’s oxygenation overrides fetal urgency; fetal outcome worsens if maternal hypoxia worsens.

options.text: Decide whether to wake, proceed with SGA, or intubate with best device based on maternal oxygenation and urgency.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Decide whether to wake, proceed with SGA, or intubate with best device based on maternal oxygenation and urgency. This is placed here because the mother’s oxygenation overrides fetal urgency; fetal outcome worsens if maternal hypoxia worsens.

options.text: If CICO develops, proceed to emergency front-of-neck airway.

options.text: Debrief and document airway event after maternal and neonatal stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Debrief and document airway event after maternal and neonatal stabilization. This is placed here because the mother’s oxygenation overrides fetal urgency; fetal outcome worsens if maternal hypoxia worsens.

management_logic: The mother’s oxygenation overrides fetal urgency; fetal outcome worsens if maternal hypoxia worsens.
learning_point: In obstetric failed intubation, do not sacrifice oxygenation to urgency.

title: Preeclampsia with pulmonary edema before cesarean

options.text: Treat severe hypertension with pregnancy-appropriate IV agent.

options.text: Restrict fluids and give diuretic if pulmonary edema is clinically significant.

options.text: Choose neuraxial versus general anesthesia based on oxygenation, coagulation, urgency, and airway risk.

options.text: Plan postoperative ICU or HDU monitoring.

title: Placenta accreta spectrum hemorrhage plan
topic: Placenta accreta spectrum hemorrhage plan
source: Chestnut's Obstetric Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A patient with placenta accreta spectrum is planned for cesarean hysterectomy with anticipated massive bleeding.

options.text: Confirm multidisciplinary plan, consent, blood availability, and escalation roles before incision.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Confirm multidisciplinary plan, consent, blood availability, and escalation roles before incision. This is placed here because accreta anesthesia is won before incision with planning, access, blood, and communication.

options.text: Secure two large-bore IV lines, arterial line, warming, rapid infuser, and cell salvage if available.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Secure two large-bore IV lines, arterial line, warming, rapid infuser, and cell salvage if available. This is placed here because accreta anesthesia is won before incision with planning, access, blood, and communication.

options.text: Use neuraxial, general, or combined technique according to plan and bleeding risk.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Use neuraxial, general, or combined technique according to plan and bleeding risk. This is placed here because accreta anesthesia is won before incision with planning, access, blood, and communication.

options.text: Avoid placental disruption before surgical control strategy is ready.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Avoid placental disruption before surgical control strategy is ready. This is placed here because accreta anesthesia is won before incision with planning, access, blood, and communication.

options.text: Activate massive transfusion early if bleeding exceeds plan or instability develops.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Activate massive transfusion early if bleeding exceeds plan or instability develops. This is placed here because accreta anesthesia is won before incision with planning, access, blood, and communication.

options.text: Transfer to ICU or HDU with detailed hemorrhage and product handover.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU or HDU with detailed hemorrhage and product handover. This is placed here because accreta anesthesia is won before incision with planning, access, blood, and communication.

management_logic: Accreta anesthesia is won before incision with planning, access, blood, and communication.
learning_point: For accreta, the checklist is part of the resuscitation.
