title: Infant pyloromyotomy with metabolic alkalosis
topic: Infant pyloromyotomy with metabolic alkalosis
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A 6-week-old with pyloric stenosis arrives for pyloromyotomy after vomiting. Chloride is low and bicarbonate high.

options.text: Do not proceed until dehydration, chloride, potassium, and alkalosis are corrected unless life-saving indication exists.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Do not proceed until dehydration, chloride, potassium, and alkalosis are corrected unless life-saving indication exists. This is placed here because pyloromyotomy is urgent but resuscitation precedes anesthesia; alkalosis increases apnea risk.

options.text: Confirm recent labs, urine output, weight, and resuscitation status.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Confirm recent labs, urine output, weight, and resuscitation status. This is placed here because pyloromyotomy is urgent but resuscitation precedes anesthesia; alkalosis increases apnea risk.

options.text: Plan aspiration precautions with gastric decompression before induction.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Plan aspiration precautions with gastric decompression before induction. This is placed here because pyloromyotomy is urgent but resuscitation precedes anesthesia; alkalosis increases apnea risk.

options.text: Use controlled RSI by experienced pediatric anesthetist when optimized.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Use controlled RSI by experienced pediatric anesthetist when optimized. This is placed here because pyloromyotomy is urgent but resuscitation precedes anesthesia; alkalosis increases apnea risk.

options.text: Extubate only when fully awake with adequate ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Extubate only when fully awake with adequate ventilation. This is placed here because pyloromyotomy is urgent but resuscitation precedes anesthesia; alkalosis increases apnea risk.

options.text: Monitor for postoperative apnea, especially if alkalosis was significant.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor for postoperative apnea, especially if alkalosis was significant. This is placed here because pyloromyotomy is urgent but resuscitation precedes anesthesia; alkalosis increases apnea risk.

management_logic: Pyloromyotomy is urgent but resuscitation precedes anesthesia; alkalosis increases apnea risk.
learning_point: Pyloric stenosis is a medical optimization problem before it is a surgical case.
evidence_basis: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Child with active URI develops bronchospasm
topic: Child with active URI develops bronchospasm
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A 4-year-old with recent wheeze develops bronchospasm after LMA insertion for minor surgery.

options.text: Stop stimulation, give 100% oxygen, and call for help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop stimulation, give 100% oxygen, and call for help. This is placed here because in elective pediatric URI bronchospasm, stabilization is followed by a low threshold to cancel.

options.text: Deepen anesthesia and ensure airway device position is correct.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Deepen anesthesia and ensure airway device position is correct. This is placed here because in elective pediatric URI bronchospasm, stabilization is followed by a low threshold to cancel.

options.text: Give inhaled bronchodilator through circuit or spacer setup.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give inhaled bronchodilator through circuit or spacer setup. This is placed here because in elective pediatric URI bronchospasm, stabilization is followed by a low threshold to cancel.

options.text: Exclude laryngospasm, aspiration, obstruction, and pneumothorax.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Exclude laryngospasm, aspiration, obstruction, and pneumothorax. This is placed here because in elective pediatric URI bronchospasm, stabilization is followed by a low threshold to cancel.

options.text: Decide whether to abandon elective surgery after stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Decide whether to abandon elective surgery after stabilization. This is placed here because in elective pediatric URI bronchospasm, stabilization is followed by a low threshold to cancel.

options.text: Provide PACU observation and parent counseling.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Provide PACU observation and parent counseling. This is placed here because in elective pediatric URI bronchospasm, stabilization is followed by a low threshold to cancel.

management_logic: In elective pediatric URI bronchospasm, stabilization is followed by a low threshold to cancel.
learning_point: A child wheezing after airway instrumentation is telling you the case may no longer be elective.

title: Pierre Robin sequence difficult airway
topic: Pierre Robin sequence difficult airway
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: An infant with micrognathia and glossoptosis is scheduled for cleft palate repair.

options.text: Prepare difficult pediatric airway plan with ENT backup before induction.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Prepare difficult pediatric airway plan with ENT backup before induction. This is placed here because pierre Robin airway risk persists at induction and extubation; spontaneous ventilation preserves safety margin.

options.text: Maintain spontaneous ventilation until ability to ventilate is proven.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Maintain spontaneous ventilation until ability to ventilate is proven. This is placed here because pierre Robin airway risk persists at induction and extubation; spontaneous ventilation preserves safety margin.

options.text: Position to relieve glossoptosis and use oral/nasal airway adjuncts.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Position to relieve glossoptosis and use oral/nasal airway adjuncts. This is placed here because pierre Robin airway risk persists at induction and extubation; spontaneous ventilation preserves safety margin.

options.text: Use fiberoptic or videolaryngoscopic technique through appropriate conduit if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Use fiberoptic or videolaryngoscopic technique through appropriate conduit if needed. This is placed here because pierre Robin airway risk persists at induction and extubation; spontaneous ventilation preserves safety margin.

options.text: Have supraglottic rescue and surgical airway plan ready, recognizing infant limitations.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Have supraglottic rescue and surgical airway plan ready, recognizing infant limitations. This is placed here because pierre Robin airway risk persists at induction and extubation; spontaneous ventilation preserves safety margin.

options.text: Plan extubation and postoperative airway monitoring carefully.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan extubation and postoperative airway monitoring carefully. This is placed here because pierre Robin airway risk persists at induction and extubation; spontaneous ventilation preserves safety margin.

management_logic: Pierre Robin airway risk persists at induction and extubation; spontaneous ventilation preserves safety margin.
learning_point: In micrognathia, the airway plan must exist before the child is asleep.

title: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage for emergency surgery
topic: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage for emergency surgery
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A 7-year-old returns bleeding after tonsillectomy, pale and swallowing blood.

options.text: Call experienced pediatric anesthesia, ENT, and blood bank.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call experienced pediatric anesthesia, ENT, and blood bank. This is placed here because the bleeding tonsil child has both aspiration and shock physiology.

options.text: Resuscitate with IV access, fluids, blood preparation, and suction ready.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Resuscitate with IV access, fluids, blood preparation, and suction ready. This is placed here because the bleeding tonsil child has both aspiration and shock physiology.

options.text: Plan RSI with two suctions and difficult-airway backup.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Plan RSI with two suctions and difficult-airway backup. This is placed here because the bleeding tonsil child has both aspiration and shock physiology.

options.text: Intubate with cuffed tube and confirm capnography; anticipate obscured view.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Intubate with cuffed tube and confirm capnography; anticipate obscured view. This is placed here because the bleeding tonsil child has both aspiration and shock physiology.

options.text: Allow surgeon to control bleeding and replace blood products as needed.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Allow surgeon to control bleeding and replace blood products as needed. This is placed here because the bleeding tonsil child has both aspiration and shock physiology.

options.text: Extubate only when awake, hemostatic, normothermic, and adequately resuscitated.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Extubate only when awake, hemostatic, normothermic, and adequately resuscitated. This is placed here because the bleeding tonsil child has both aspiration and shock physiology.

management_logic: The bleeding tonsil child has both aspiration and shock physiology.
learning_point: For post-tonsil bleed, two suctions are not optional.
evidence_basis: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Pediatric fasting hypoglycemia before MRI anesthesia
topic: Pediatric fasting hypoglycemia before MRI anesthesia
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A small child fasted overnight for MRI is drowsy and sweaty before induction.

options.text: Check glucose immediately in any drowsy fasting child.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Check glucose immediately in any drowsy fasting child. This is placed here because children have limited glycogen reserve and prolonged fasting can cause hypoglycemia.

options.text: Treat hypoglycemia with IV dextrose according to weight and protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Treat hypoglycemia with IV dextrose according to weight and protocol. This is placed here because children have limited glycogen reserve and prolonged fasting can cause hypoglycemia.

options.text: Delay anesthesia until mental status and glucose improve.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Delay anesthesia until mental status and glucose improve. This is placed here because children have limited glycogen reserve and prolonged fasting can cause hypoglycemia.

options.text: Start maintenance fluid containing glucose if prolonged fasting continues.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Start maintenance fluid containing glucose if prolonged fasting continues. This is placed here because children have limited glycogen reserve and prolonged fasting can cause hypoglycemia.

options.text: Identify cause and repeat glucose monitoring during and after anesthesia.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Identify cause and repeat glucose monitoring during and after anesthesia. This is placed here because children have limited glycogen reserve and prolonged fasting can cause hypoglycemia.

options.text: Document fasting breach and modify scheduling process.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document fasting breach and modify scheduling process. This is placed here because children have limited glycogen reserve and prolonged fasting can cause hypoglycemia.

management_logic: Children have limited glycogen reserve and prolonged fasting can cause hypoglycemia.
learning_point: In pediatric anesthesia, drowsiness before induction may be glucose, not behavior.
evidence_basis: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Neonatal hypothermia and bradycardia
topic: Neonatal hypothermia and bradycardia
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A neonate becomes cold and bradycardic during emergency laparotomy.

options.text: Increase FiO2 and confirm ventilation and oxygenation.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Increase FiO2 and confirm ventilation and oxygenation. This is placed here because neonatal bradycardia is commonly driven by hypoxia, hypothermia, or metabolic derangement.

options.text: Actively warm with forced-air warmer, warm fluids, increased room temperature, and covering exposed areas.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Actively warm with forced-air warmer, warm fluids, increased room temperature, and covering exposed areas. This is placed here because neonatal bradycardia is commonly driven by hypoxia, hypothermia, or metabolic derangement.

options.text: Check glucose, calcium, acid-base, and hemoglobin.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Check glucose, calcium, acid-base, and hemoglobin. This is placed here because neonatal bradycardia is commonly driven by hypoxia, hypothermia, or metabolic derangement.

options.text: Treat bradycardia according to neonatal physiology after correcting hypoxia and hypothermia.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat bradycardia according to neonatal physiology after correcting hypoxia and hypothermia. This is placed here because neonatal bradycardia is commonly driven by hypoxia, hypothermia, or metabolic derangement.

options.text: Minimize exposure and coordinate surgical pauses if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Minimize exposure and coordinate surgical pauses if needed. This is placed here because neonatal bradycardia is commonly driven by hypoxia, hypothermia, or metabolic derangement.

options.text: Transfer to NICU with temperature and metabolic handover.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to NICU with temperature and metabolic handover. This is placed here because neonatal bradycardia is commonly driven by hypoxia, hypothermia, or metabolic derangement.

management_logic: Neonatal bradycardia is commonly driven by hypoxia, hypothermia, or metabolic derangement.
learning_point: A cold neonate is an unstable neonate.
evidence_basis: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Cyanotic congenital heart disease during induction
topic: Cyanotic congenital heart disease during induction
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A child with Tetralogy physiology becomes deeply cyanosed and hypotensive during crying and induction.

options.text: Call for help and give 100% oxygen while calming/deepening anesthesia.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and give 100% oxygen while calming/deepening anesthesia. This is placed here because hypercyanotic spells improve with increased SVR, preload, oxygen, and reduced infundibular spasm.

options.text: Increase systemic vascular resistance with phenylephrine or appropriate vasopressor.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Increase systemic vascular resistance with phenylephrine or appropriate vasopressor. This is placed here because hypercyanotic spells improve with increased SVR, preload, oxygen, and reduced infundibular spasm.

options.text: Give fluid bolus to improve RV filling if hypovolemia suspected.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give fluid bolus to improve RV filling if hypovolemia suspected. This is placed here because hypercyanotic spells improve with increased SVR, preload, oxygen, and reduced infundibular spasm.

options.text: Treat infundibular spasm with beta-blocker if appropriate and supervised.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat infundibular spasm with beta-blocker if appropriate and supervised. This is placed here because hypercyanotic spells improve with increased SVR, preload, oxygen, and reduced infundibular spasm.

options.text: Correct acidosis and avoid increases in PVR such as hypoxia and hypercarbia.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Correct acidosis and avoid increases in PVR such as hypoxia and hypercarbia. This is placed here because hypercyanotic spells improve with increased SVR, preload, oxygen, and reduced infundibular spasm.

options.text: Proceed only after stable saturation and hemodynamics or postpone if unsafe.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Proceed only after stable saturation and hemodynamics or postpone if unsafe. This is placed here because hypercyanotic spells improve with increased SVR, preload, oxygen, and reduced infundibular spasm.

management_logic: Hypercyanotic spells improve with increased SVR, preload, oxygen, and reduced infundibular spasm.
learning_point: In Tet spell physiology, phenylephrine may improve oxygen saturation.

title: Child with OSA obstructs after opioid in PACU
topic: Child with OSA obstructs after opioid in PACU
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A child after adenotonsillectomy receives opioid and becomes obstructed, snoring, and desaturated.

options.text: Call for help, stimulate, reposition, jaw thrust, and apply oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help, stimulate, reposition, jaw thrust, and apply oxygen. This is placed here because oSA children are opioid-sensitive and prone to obstruction after airway surgery.

options.text: Suction blood or secretions and apply CPAP if obstruction persists.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Suction blood or secretions and apply CPAP if obstruction persists. This is placed here because oSA children are opioid-sensitive and prone to obstruction after airway surgery.

options.text: Support ventilation with bag-mask if hypoventilation is significant.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Support ventilation with bag-mask if hypoventilation is significant. This is placed here because oSA children are opioid-sensitive and prone to obstruction after airway surgery.

options.text: Give titrated naloxone for opioid-induced respiratory depression if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Give titrated naloxone for opioid-induced respiratory depression if needed. This is placed here because oSA children are opioid-sensitive and prone to obstruction after airway surgery.

options.text: Stop opioids and use multimodal non-opioid analgesia plan.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Stop opioids and use multimodal non-opioid analgesia plan. This is placed here because oSA children are opioid-sensitive and prone to obstruction after airway surgery.

options.text: Admit for monitored observation rather than routine discharge.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Admit for monitored observation rather than routine discharge. This is placed here because oSA children are opioid-sensitive and prone to obstruction after airway surgery.

management_logic: OSA children are opioid-sensitive and prone to obstruction after airway surgery.

title: Aneurysm rupture during clipping
topic: Aneurysm rupture during clipping

options.text: Announce rupture response and coordinate with neurosurgeon.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Announce rupture response and coordinate with neurosurgeon. This is placed here because aneurysm rupture requires coordinated surgical control and controlled hemodynamics.

options.text: Temporarily lower blood pressure only to agreed target while preserving cerebral perfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Temporarily lower blood pressure only to agreed target while preserving cerebral perfusion. This is placed here because aneurysm rupture requires coordinated surgical control and controlled hemodynamics.

options.text: Ensure adequate anesthesia, paralysis, oxygenation, and normocapnia.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ensure adequate anesthesia, paralysis, oxygenation, and normocapnia. This is placed here because aneurysm rupture requires coordinated surgical control and controlled hemodynamics.

options.text: Prepare blood, rapid access, and hemodynamic support.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Prepare blood, rapid access, and hemodynamic support. This is placed here because aneurysm rupture requires coordinated surgical control and controlled hemodynamics.

options.text: Give brain relaxation measures such as CSF drainage or osmotherapy as agreed.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Give brain relaxation measures such as CSF drainage or osmotherapy as agreed. This is placed here because aneurysm rupture requires coordinated surgical control and controlled hemodynamics.

options.text: After control, restore appropriate perfusion and plan postoperative neurocritical care.

title: Venous air embolism in sitting craniotomy
topic: Venous air embolism in sitting craniotomy
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Yao & Artusio 9e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: During sitting posterior fossa surgery, EtCO2 falls abruptly and mill-wheel murmur is suspected.

options.text: Notify surgeon and flood/pack the field with saline.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Notify surgeon and flood/pack the field with saline. This is placed here because vAE treatment stops entrainment, eliminates nitrous oxide, aspirates air when possible, and supports circulation.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and stop nitrous oxide if in use.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and stop nitrous oxide if in use. This is placed here because vAE treatment stops entrainment, eliminates nitrous oxide, aspirates air when possible, and supports circulation.

options.text: Compress jugular veins briefly if requested and safe to identify entrainment.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Compress jugular veins briefly if requested and safe to identify entrainment. This is placed here because vAE treatment stops entrainment, eliminates nitrous oxide, aspirates air when possible, and supports circulation.

options.text: Aspirate air from central venous catheter if positioned and available.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Aspirate air from central venous catheter if positioned and available. This is placed here because vAE treatment stops entrainment, eliminates nitrous oxide, aspirates air when possible, and supports circulation.

options.text: Support circulation with fluids and vasopressors; lower head if feasible.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Support circulation with fluids and vasopressors; lower head if feasible. This is placed here because vAE treatment stops entrainment, eliminates nitrous oxide, aspirates air when possible, and supports circulation.

options.text: Use TEE/precordial Doppler data if available and decide on continuation.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Use TEE/precordial Doppler data if available and decide on continuation. This is placed here because vAE treatment stops entrainment, eliminates nitrous oxide, aspirates air when possible, and supports circulation.

management_logic: VAE treatment stops entrainment, eliminates nitrous oxide, aspirates air when possible, and supports circulation.
learning_point: A sudden EtCO2 fall in sitting craniotomy is VAE until proved otherwise.

title: Traumatic brain injury becomes hypotensive after induction
topic: Traumatic brain injury becomes hypotensive after induction
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Yao & Artusio 9e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: A severe TBI patient becomes hypotensive after induction for decompressive craniectomy.

options.text: Restore blood pressure immediately with vasopressor while assessing volume loss.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Restore blood pressure immediately with vasopressor while assessing volume loss. This is placed here because secondary brain injury from hypotension and hypoxia is preventable and time critical.

options.text: Ensure oxygenation and ventilation; avoid hypoxia and severe hyperventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Ensure oxygenation and ventilation; avoid hypoxia and severe hyperventilation. This is placed here because secondary brain injury from hypotension and hypoxia is preventable and time critical.

options.text: Treat hemorrhage with blood products if suspected.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Treat hemorrhage with blood products if suspected. This is placed here because secondary brain injury from hypotension and hypoxia is preventable and time critical.

options.text: Maintain anesthetic depth without excessive vasodilation.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Maintain anesthetic depth without excessive vasodilation. This is placed here because secondary brain injury from hypotension and hypoxia is preventable and time critical.

options.text: Communicate CPP targets with neurosurgeon and monitor pupils if feasible.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Communicate CPP targets with neurosurgeon and monitor pupils if feasible. This is placed here because secondary brain injury from hypotension and hypoxia is preventable and time critical.

options.text: Transfer to ICU with BP, ventilation, osmotherapy, and sedation targets.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU with BP, ventilation, osmotherapy, and sedation targets. This is placed here because secondary brain injury from hypotension and hypoxia is preventable and time critical.

management_logic: Secondary brain injury from hypotension and hypoxia is preventable and time critical.
learning_point: In TBI, one episode of hypotension can matter.

title: Loss of motor evoked potentials during spine surgery
topic: Loss of motor evoked potentials during spine surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Yao & Artusio 9e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: During scoliosis correction, MEPs disappear bilaterally after rod placement.

options.text: Announce signal loss and ask surgeon to pause corrective maneuver.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Announce signal loss and ask surgeon to pause corrective maneuver. This is placed here because mEP loss needs immediate parallel correction of surgical and anesthetic causes.

options.text: Increase MAP to agreed spinal cord perfusion target.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Increase MAP to agreed spinal cord perfusion target. This is placed here because mEP loss needs immediate parallel correction of surgical and anesthetic causes.

options.text: Correct anesthetic confounders: reduce volatile, ensure TIVA consistency, avoid extra paralytic.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Correct anesthetic confounders: reduce volatile, ensure TIVA consistency, avoid extra paralytic. This is placed here because mEP loss needs immediate parallel correction of surgical and anesthetic causes.

options.text: Correct physiologic factors: anemia, hypothermia, hypoxia, hypocapnia, and hypotension.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Correct physiologic factors: anemia, hypothermia, hypoxia, hypocapnia, and hypotension. This is placed here because mEP loss needs immediate parallel correction of surgical and anesthetic causes.

options.text: Ask surgeon to reverse recent correction or inspect hardware if signals do not return.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Ask surgeon to reverse recent correction or inspect hardware if signals do not return. This is placed here because mEP loss needs immediate parallel correction of surgical and anesthetic causes.

options.text: Document timing, interventions, and signal recovery.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document timing, interventions, and signal recovery. This is placed here because mEP loss needs immediate parallel correction of surgical and anesthetic causes.

management_logic: MEP loss needs immediate parallel correction of surgical and anesthetic causes.

options.text: Tell surgeon to stop bladder distension and drain bladder.

options.text: Deepen anesthesia and ensure adequate analgesia.

options.text: Treat severe hypertension with short-acting vasodilator if it persists.

options.text: Treat bradycardia if symptomatic or severe.

options.text: Search for ongoing triggers: blocked catheter, bowel, skin pressure, surgical stimulation.

options.text: Monitor postoperatively for recurrence.

options.text: Adjust sedation carefully to preserve cooperation and ventilation.

options.text: Resume only after patient cooperation and hemodynamics are stable.
