evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Yao & Artusio 9e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: DKA patient for emergency surgery
topic: DKA patient for emergency surgery
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: A patient with abdominal sepsis and DKA needs emergency source control.

options.text: Call senior anesthesia, surgery, and ICU; clarify urgency versus optimization window.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call senior anesthesia, surgery, and ICU; clarify urgency versus optimization window. This is placed here because dKA emergency anesthesia requires simultaneous metabolic resuscitation and source control planning.

options.text: Start resuscitation with isotonic fluids and potassium-aware insulin protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Start resuscitation with isotonic fluids and potassium-aware insulin protocol. This is placed here because dKA emergency anesthesia requires simultaneous metabolic resuscitation and source control planning.

options.text: Correct hypovolemia and shock before induction as much as time permits.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Correct hypovolemia and shock before induction as much as time permits. This is placed here because dKA emergency anesthesia requires simultaneous metabolic resuscitation and source control planning.

options.text: Monitor glucose, potassium, acid-base, and urine output frequently.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Monitor glucose, potassium, acid-base, and urine output frequently. This is placed here because dKA emergency anesthesia requires simultaneous metabolic resuscitation and source control planning.

options.text: Use aspiration precautions and hemodynamically stable induction plan.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use aspiration precautions and hemodynamically stable induction plan. This is placed here because dKA emergency anesthesia requires simultaneous metabolic resuscitation and source control planning.

options.text: Transfer to ICU for ongoing DKA and sepsis management.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU for ongoing DKA and sepsis management. This is placed here because dKA emergency anesthesia requires simultaneous metabolic resuscitation and source control planning.

management_logic: DKA emergency anesthesia requires simultaneous metabolic resuscitation and source control planning.
learning_point: In DKA, potassium can fall fast after insulin; induction is not the only danger.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Adrenal crisis after induction
topic: Adrenal crisis after induction
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: A chronic steroid user becomes refractory hypotensive after induction despite fluids and vasopressors.

options.text: Consider adrenal crisis and call for help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Consider adrenal crisis and call for help. This is placed here because adrenal crisis causes vasopressor-resistant hypotension and hypoglycemia in at-risk patients.

options.text: Support airway, oxygenation, fluids, and vasopressors.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Support airway, oxygenation, fluids, and vasopressors. This is placed here because adrenal crisis causes vasopressor-resistant hypotension and hypoglycemia in at-risk patients.

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone stress dose according to protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give IV hydrocortisone stress dose according to protocol. This is placed here because adrenal crisis causes vasopressor-resistant hypotension and hypoglycemia in at-risk patients.

options.text: Treat hypoglycemia and check sodium, potassium, and acid-base status.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Treat hypoglycemia and check sodium, potassium, and acid-base status. This is placed here because adrenal crisis causes vasopressor-resistant hypotension and hypoglycemia in at-risk patients.

options.text: Look for sepsis, bleeding, anaphylaxis, and myocardial causes in parallel.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Look for sepsis, bleeding, anaphylaxis, and myocardial causes in parallel. This is placed here because adrenal crisis causes vasopressor-resistant hypotension and hypoglycemia in at-risk patients.

options.text: Continue steroid coverage and ICU monitoring if instability persists.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue steroid coverage and ICU monitoring if instability persists. This is placed here because adrenal crisis causes vasopressor-resistant hypotension and hypoglycemia in at-risk patients.

management_logic: Adrenal crisis causes vasopressor-resistant hypotension and hypoglycemia in at-risk patients.
learning_point: In a steroid-dependent patient, refractory hypotension needs steroid replacement early.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Thyroid storm during emergency surgery
topic: Thyroid storm during emergency surgery
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: A patient with untreated thyrotoxicosis develops hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, and hypertension during emergency surgery.

options.text: Call for help and treat hypermetabolic crisis while excluding MH and sepsis.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and treat hypermetabolic crisis while excluding MH and sepsis. This is placed here because thyroid storm is clinical and requires supportive care plus thyroid-specific therapy.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen, active cooling, and deepen anesthesia.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen, active cooling, and deepen anesthesia. This is placed here because thyroid storm is clinical and requires supportive care plus thyroid-specific therapy.

options.text: Control sympathetic surge with beta-blocker if no contraindication.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Control sympathetic surge with beta-blocker if no contraindication. This is placed here because thyroid storm is clinical and requires supportive care plus thyroid-specific therapy.

options.text: Give antithyroid therapy, iodine timing, and steroid with endocrine guidance.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Give antithyroid therapy, iodine timing, and steroid with endocrine guidance. This is placed here because thyroid storm is clinical and requires supportive care plus thyroid-specific therapy.

options.text: Treat arrhythmia, dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and heart failure.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Treat arrhythmia, dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and heart failure. This is placed here because thyroid storm is clinical and requires supportive care plus thyroid-specific therapy.

options.text: Transfer to ICU for ongoing storm management.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU for ongoing storm management. This is placed here because thyroid storm is clinical and requires supportive care plus thyroid-specific therapy.

management_logic: Thyroid storm is clinical and requires supportive care plus thyroid-specific therapy.
learning_point: Hyperthermia with tachyarrhythmia is not always MH; context matters.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Myxedema coma for urgent laparotomy
topic: Myxedema coma for urgent laparotomy
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: An elderly patient with severe hypothyroidism, hypothermia, bradycardia, and ileus needs urgent laparotomy.

options.text: Call senior anesthesia, endocrine, ICU, and surgery for risk-benefit decision.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call senior anesthesia, endocrine, ICU, and surgery for risk-benefit decision. This is placed here because myxedema coma carries extreme anesthetic sensitivity and requires endocrine resuscitation.

options.text: Support airway and ventilation; avoid sedative overdose.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Support airway and ventilation; avoid sedative overdose. This is placed here because myxedema coma carries extreme anesthetic sensitivity and requires endocrine resuscitation.

options.text: Give stress-dose steroid before thyroid hormone if adrenal status uncertain.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give stress-dose steroid before thyroid hormone if adrenal status uncertain. This is placed here because myxedema coma carries extreme anesthetic sensitivity and requires endocrine resuscitation.

options.text: Start thyroid hormone replacement as guided by endocrine/ICU.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Start thyroid hormone replacement as guided by endocrine/ICU. This is placed here because myxedema coma carries extreme anesthetic sensitivity and requires endocrine resuscitation.

options.text: Warm carefully and correct sodium, glucose, and hemodynamics slowly.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Warm carefully and correct sodium, glucose, and hemodynamics slowly. This is placed here because myxedema coma carries extreme anesthetic sensitivity and requires endocrine resuscitation.

options.text: Plan postoperative ventilation and ICU care.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan postoperative ventilation and ICU care. This is placed here because myxedema coma carries extreme anesthetic sensitivity and requires endocrine resuscitation.

management_logic: Myxedema coma carries extreme anesthetic sensitivity and requires endocrine resuscitation.
learning_point: In myxedema coma, small anesthetic doses can be large overdoses.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Severe hyperkalemia in ESRD before induction
topic: Severe hyperkalemia in ESRD before induction
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A dialysis patient for emergency surgery has peaked T waves and potassium 6.8 mmol/L.

options.text: Treat ECG-significant hyperkalemia immediately with IV calcium.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Treat ECG-significant hyperkalemia immediately with IV calcium. This is placed here because eCG changes make hyperkalemia an immediate pre-induction threat.

options.text: Shift potassium intracellularly with insulin-dextrose and beta-agonist according to protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Shift potassium intracellularly with insulin-dextrose and beta-agonist according to protocol. This is placed here because eCG changes make hyperkalemia an immediate pre-induction threat.

options.text: Avoid suxamethonium and potassium-containing fluids.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Avoid suxamethonium and potassium-containing fluids. This is placed here because eCG changes make hyperkalemia an immediate pre-induction threat.

options.text: Arrange urgent dialysis or definitive potassium removal if feasible.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Arrange urgent dialysis or definitive potassium removal if feasible. This is placed here because eCG changes make hyperkalemia an immediate pre-induction threat.

options.text: Monitor ECG and repeat potassium/ glucose frequently.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Monitor ECG and repeat potassium/ glucose frequently. This is placed here because eCG changes make hyperkalemia an immediate pre-induction threat.

options.text: Proceed with anesthesia only after risk is reduced or life-saving surgery mandates action.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Proceed with anesthesia only after risk is reduced or life-saving surgery mandates action. This is placed here because eCG changes make hyperkalemia an immediate pre-induction threat.

management_logic: ECG changes make hyperkalemia an immediate pre-induction threat.
learning_point: Calcium buys time; it does not remove potassium.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Hyponatremic seizure before surgery
topic: Hyponatremic seizure before surgery
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: A patient awaiting TURP has a generalized seizure; sodium returns at 112 mmol/L.

options.text: Protect airway, give oxygen, and stop the planned elective procedure.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Protect airway, give oxygen, and stop the planned elective procedure. This is placed here because seizure with severe hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline and cancellation of elective anesthesia.

options.text: Terminate seizure with benzodiazepine if ongoing.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Terminate seizure with benzodiazepine if ongoing. This is placed here because seizure with severe hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline and cancellation of elective anesthesia.

options.text: Give hypertonic saline according to severe symptomatic hyponatremia protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Give hypertonic saline according to severe symptomatic hyponatremia protocol. This is placed here because seizure with severe hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline and cancellation of elective anesthesia.

options.text: Check glucose, osmolality, urine studies, drugs, and chronicity.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Check glucose, osmolality, urine studies, drugs, and chronicity. This is placed here because seizure with severe hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline and cancellation of elective anesthesia.

options.text: Avoid overly rapid sodium correction and monitor sodium serially.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Avoid overly rapid sodium correction and monitor sodium serially. This is placed here because seizure with severe hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline and cancellation of elective anesthesia.

options.text: Transfer to ICU or HDU for controlled correction.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU or HDU for controlled correction. This is placed here because seizure with severe hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline and cancellation of elective anesthesia.

management_logic: Seizure with severe hyponatremia requires hypertonic saline and cancellation of elective anesthesia.
learning_point: The safe sodium correction rate matters after the seizure stops.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Citrate hypocalcemia during massive transfusion
topic: Citrate hypocalcemia during massive transfusion
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: During massive transfusion, the patient becomes hypotensive with prolonged QT and low ionized calcium.

options.text: Recognize transfusion-related hypocalcemia as a contributor to shock.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Recognize transfusion-related hypocalcemia as a contributor to shock. This is placed here because citrate in blood products binds calcium and can worsen hypotension and coagulopathy.

options.text: Give IV calcium according to institutional massive transfusion protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give IV calcium according to institutional massive transfusion protocol. This is placed here because citrate in blood products binds calcium and can worsen hypotension and coagulopathy.

options.text: Continue balanced blood product resuscitation and surgical control.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Continue balanced blood product resuscitation and surgical control. This is placed here because citrate in blood products binds calcium and can worsen hypotension and coagulopathy.

options.text: Monitor ionized calcium, potassium, temperature, pH, and coagulation repeatedly.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Monitor ionized calcium, potassium, temperature, pH, and coagulation repeatedly. This is placed here because citrate in blood products binds calcium and can worsen hypotension and coagulopathy.

options.text: Warm blood and patient to reduce coagulopathy.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Warm blood and patient to reduce coagulopathy. This is placed here because citrate in blood products binds calcium and can worsen hypotension and coagulopathy.

options.text: Document calcium replacement and handover ongoing monitoring.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document calcium replacement and handover ongoing monitoring. This is placed here because citrate in blood products binds calcium and can worsen hypotension and coagulopathy.

management_logic: Citrate in blood products binds calcium and can worsen hypotension and coagulopathy.
learning_point: During massive transfusion, ionized calcium is a resuscitation variable.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e

title: Postreperfusion syndrome in liver transplant
topic: Postreperfusion syndrome in liver transplant
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: Immediately after graft reperfusion, BP collapses with bradycardia, hyperkalemia, and acidosis.

options.text: Warn team at reperfusion and anticipate hemodynamic collapse.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Warn team at reperfusion and anticipate hemodynamic collapse. This is placed here because reperfusion can abruptly release acid, potassium, and vasoactive mediators causing cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Treat hypotension and bradycardia immediately with vasopressor, epinephrine, or inotrope as needed.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Treat hypotension and bradycardia immediately with vasopressor, epinephrine, or inotrope as needed. This is placed here because reperfusion can abruptly release acid, potassium, and vasoactive mediators causing cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Treat hyperkalemia with calcium and potassium-shifting therapy.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Treat hyperkalemia with calcium and potassium-shifting therapy. This is placed here because reperfusion can abruptly release acid, potassium, and vasoactive mediators causing cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Correct acidosis, hypocalcemia, and hypothermia.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Correct acidosis, hypocalcemia, and hypothermia. This is placed here because reperfusion can abruptly release acid, potassium, and vasoactive mediators causing cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Use TEE or hemodynamic data to assess RV/LV function and volume status.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use TEE or hemodynamic data to assess RV/LV function and volume status. This is placed here because reperfusion can abruptly release acid, potassium, and vasoactive mediators causing cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Continue ICU-level postoperative graft and organ support.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue ICU-level postoperative graft and organ support. This is placed here because reperfusion can abruptly release acid, potassium, and vasoactive mediators causing cardiovascular collapse.

management_logic: Reperfusion can abruptly release acid, potassium, and vasoactive mediators causing cardiovascular collapse.
learning_point: In liver transplant, reperfusion is a planned crisis.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Renal transplant reperfusion hyperkalemia
topic: Renal transplant reperfusion hyperkalemia
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After reperfusion of kidney graft, potassium rises and ECG shows widening QRS.

options.text: Give IV calcium immediately for ECG changes.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Give IV calcium immediately for ECG changes. This is placed here because hyperkalemia after reperfusion can cause arrest and needs ECG-directed treatment.

options.text: Shift potassium with insulin-dextrose and beta-agonist as appropriate.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Shift potassium with insulin-dextrose and beta-agonist as appropriate. This is placed here because hyperkalemia after reperfusion can cause arrest and needs ECG-directed treatment.

options.text: Increase ventilation if acidotic and correct metabolic acidosis if severe.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Increase ventilation if acidotic and correct metabolic acidosis if severe. This is placed here because hyperkalemia after reperfusion can cause arrest and needs ECG-directed treatment.

options.text: Avoid potassium-containing fluids and nephrotoxic insults.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Avoid potassium-containing fluids and nephrotoxic insults. This is placed here because hyperkalemia after reperfusion can cause arrest and needs ECG-directed treatment.

options.text: Coordinate with transplant surgeon and nephrology regarding graft perfusion and dialysis backup.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Coordinate with transplant surgeon and nephrology regarding graft perfusion and dialysis backup. This is placed here because hyperkalemia after reperfusion can cause arrest and needs ECG-directed treatment.

options.text: Monitor serial potassium, glucose, urine output, and ECG.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor serial potassium, glucose, urine output, and ECG. This is placed here because hyperkalemia after reperfusion can cause arrest and needs ECG-directed treatment.

management_logic: Hyperkalemia after reperfusion can cause arrest and needs ECG-directed treatment.
learning_point: A new kidney does not protect the patient from immediate hyperkalemia.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Cirrhosis patient bleeds during surgery
topic: Cirrhosis patient bleeds during surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A cirrhotic patient undergoing laparotomy develops diffuse oozing with low fibrinogen and thrombocytopenia.

options.text: Call for help and ask surgeon for local hemostatic control.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and ask surgeon for local hemostatic control. This is placed here because cirrhotic bleeding is multifactorial; fibrinogen and platelets are often key practical targets.

options.text: Activate blood product support guided by labs or viscoelastic testing.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Activate blood product support guided by labs or viscoelastic testing. This is placed here because cirrhotic bleeding is multifactorial; fibrinogen and platelets are often key practical targets.

options.text: Replace fibrinogen early if low during active bleeding.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Replace fibrinogen early if low during active bleeding. This is placed here because cirrhotic bleeding is multifactorial; fibrinogen and platelets are often key practical targets.

options.text: Transfuse platelets and plasma according to bleeding, tests, and protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Transfuse platelets and plasma according to bleeding, tests, and protocol. This is placed here because cirrhotic bleeding is multifactorial; fibrinogen and platelets are often key practical targets.

options.text: Correct hypothermia, acidosis, calcium, and anemia.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Correct hypothermia, acidosis, calcium, and anemia. This is placed here because cirrhotic bleeding is multifactorial; fibrinogen and platelets are often key practical targets.

options.text: Plan ICU care for rebleeding, encephalopathy, renal injury, and ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan ICU care for rebleeding, encephalopathy, renal injury, and ventilation. This is placed here because cirrhotic bleeding is multifactorial; fibrinogen and platelets are often key practical targets.

management_logic: Cirrhotic bleeding is multifactorial; fibrinogen and platelets are often key practical targets.
learning_point: In cirrhosis, INR alone is an incomplete bleeding map.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: Large ascites and aspiration risk during induction
topic: Large ascites and aspiration risk during induction
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: A cirrhotic patient with tense ascites needs emergency hernia repair and cannot lie flat comfortably.

options.text: Optimize position with head-up preoxygenation and suction ready.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Optimize position with head-up preoxygenation and suction ready. This is placed here because ascites reduces FRC and increases aspiration risk; positioning and airway plan matter.

options.text: Consider awake or modified RSI plan based on airway and oxygen reserve.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Consider awake or modified RSI plan based on airway and oxygen reserve. This is placed here because ascites reduces FRC and increases aspiration risk; positioning and airway plan matter.

options.text: Decompress stomach with NG tube if indicated and safe.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Decompress stomach with NG tube if indicated and safe. This is placed here because ascites reduces FRC and increases aspiration risk; positioning and airway plan matter.

options.text: Induce with aspiration precautions and experienced airway operator.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Induce with aspiration precautions and experienced airway operator. This is placed here because ascites reduces FRC and increases aspiration risk; positioning and airway plan matter.

options.text: Use lung-protective ventilation and avoid excessive PEEP compromising venous return.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use lung-protective ventilation and avoid excessive PEEP compromising venous return. This is placed here because ascites reduces FRC and increases aspiration risk; positioning and airway plan matter.

options.text: Plan postoperative monitoring for respiratory failure and renal decompensation.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan postoperative monitoring for respiratory failure and renal decompensation. This is placed here because ascites reduces FRC and increases aspiration risk; positioning and airway plan matter.

management_logic: Ascites reduces FRC and increases aspiration risk; positioning and airway plan matter.
learning_point: A patient who cannot lie flat will not become safer after induction.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Yao & Artusio 9e

title: AV fistula surgery with steal symptoms under block
topic: AV fistula surgery with steal symptoms under block
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023
presentation: During AV fistula creation under brachial plexus block, the hand becomes pale and pulseless after clamp release.

options.text: Alert surgeon immediately and stop assuming block-related numbness.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Alert surgeon immediately and stop assuming block-related numbness. This is placed here because regional anesthesia can mask ischemic pain; objective vascular checks are essential.

options.text: Assess color, capillary refill, Doppler signal, and contralateral limb.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Assess color, capillary refill, Doppler signal, and contralateral limb. This is placed here because regional anesthesia can mask ischemic pain; objective vascular checks are essential.

options.text: Maintain systemic blood pressure and warmth.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Maintain systemic blood pressure and warmth. This is placed here because regional anesthesia can mask ischemic pain; objective vascular checks are essential.

options.text: Ask surgeon to evaluate anastomosis, thrombosis, spasm, or steal urgently.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Ask surgeon to evaluate anastomosis, thrombosis, spasm, or steal urgently. This is placed here because regional anesthesia can mask ischemic pain; objective vascular checks are essential.

options.text: Avoid discharge until vascular status is clearly safe.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Avoid discharge until vascular status is clearly safe. This is placed here because regional anesthesia can mask ischemic pain; objective vascular checks are essential.

options.text: Document baseline and postoperative neurologic-vascular findings.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document baseline and postoperative neurologic-vascular findings. This is placed here because regional anesthesia can mask ischemic pain; objective vascular checks are essential.

management_logic: Regional anesthesia can mask ischemic pain; objective vascular checks are essential.
learning_point: After regional block, a pale pulseless hand is vascular until proved otherwise.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Advanced Anesthesia Review 2023

title: Failure to wean from cardiopulmonary bypass
topic: Failure to wean from cardiopulmonary bypass
source: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: After valve surgery, attempts to separate from CPB cause hypotension and rising pulmonary pressures.

options.text: Pause weaning and communicate structured failure-to-wean assessment.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Pause weaning and communicate structured failure-to-wean assessment. This is placed here because failure to wean from CPB demands TEE-guided diagnosis before escalating blindly.

options.text: Use TEE to assess volume, ventricular function, valves, air, tamponade, and regional ischemia.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Use TEE to assess volume, ventricular function, valves, air, tamponade, and regional ischemia. This is placed here because failure to wean from CPB demands TEE-guided diagnosis before escalating blindly.

options.text: Correct rhythm, electrolytes, temperature, acid-base, hemoglobin, and ventilation.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Correct rhythm, electrolytes, temperature, acid-base, hemoglobin, and ventilation. This is placed here because failure to wean from CPB demands TEE-guided diagnosis before escalating blindly.

options.text: Support RV with pulmonary vasodilator, inotrope, and systemic pressure strategy.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Support RV with pulmonary vasodilator, inotrope, and systemic pressure strategy. This is placed here because failure to wean from CPB demands TEE-guided diagnosis before escalating blindly.

options.text: Consider mechanical support if pharmacologic support fails.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Consider mechanical support if pharmacologic support fails. This is placed here because failure to wean from CPB demands TEE-guided diagnosis before escalating blindly.

options.text: Transfer to ICU only after stable separation plan and handover.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU only after stable separation plan and handover. This is placed here because failure to wean from CPB demands TEE-guided diagnosis before escalating blindly.

management_logic: Failure to wean from CPB demands TEE-guided diagnosis before escalating blindly.
learning_point: After CPB, treat the cause seen on TEE, not just the number on the arterial line.
evidence_basis: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Catastrophic protamine reaction
topic: Catastrophic protamine reaction
source: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: After protamine administration, pulmonary artery pressure surges, systemic BP collapses, and RV dilates.

options.text: Stop protamine and call for full cardiac team support.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop protamine and call for full cardiac team support. This is placed here because protamine can cause acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure requiring immediate cardiopulmonary rescue.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and support systemic pressure with vasopressor.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and support systemic pressure with vasopressor. This is placed here because protamine can cause acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure requiring immediate cardiopulmonary rescue.

options.text: Treat acute pulmonary hypertension and RV failure with pulmonary vasodilator and inotrope.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Treat acute pulmonary hypertension and RV failure with pulmonary vasodilator and inotrope. This is placed here because protamine can cause acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure requiring immediate cardiopulmonary rescue.

options.text: Return to CPB if collapse is refractory and cannulation is available.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Return to CPB if collapse is refractory and cannulation is available. This is placed here because protamine can cause acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure requiring immediate cardiopulmonary rescue.

options.text: Assess coagulation and residual heparin after stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Assess coagulation and residual heparin after stabilization. This is placed here because protamine can cause acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure requiring immediate cardiopulmonary rescue.

options.text: Document reaction and avoid further protamine unless risk-benefit is explicit.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document reaction and avoid further protamine unless risk-benefit is explicit. This is placed here because protamine can cause acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure requiring immediate cardiopulmonary rescue.

management_logic: Protamine can cause acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure requiring immediate cardiopulmonary rescue.
learning_point: A protamine reaction can be a right-heart catastrophe.
evidence_basis: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Cardiac tamponade in ICU after cardiac surgery
topic: Cardiac tamponade in ICU after cardiac surgery
source: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A postoperative cardiac surgery patient develops hypotension, rising CVP, narrowed pulse pressure, and falling chest drain output.

options.text: Call cardiac surgeon and prepare for emergency reopening.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call cardiac surgeon and prepare for emergency reopening. This is placed here because post-cardiac surgery tamponade may present with low drain output due to clot obstruction.

options.text: Support oxygenation and circulation with vasopressors while definitive treatment is mobilized.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Support oxygenation and circulation with vasopressors while definitive treatment is mobilized. This is placed here because post-cardiac surgery tamponade may present with low drain output due to clot obstruction.

options.text: Use bedside echo if immediately available but do not delay reopening in extremis.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Use bedside echo if immediately available but do not delay reopening in extremis. This is placed here because post-cardiac surgery tamponade may present with low drain output due to clot obstruction.

options.text: Check chest drains for blockage and strip only according to local policy.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Check chest drains for blockage and strip only according to local policy. This is placed here because post-cardiac surgery tamponade may present with low drain output due to clot obstruction.

options.text: Correct coagulopathy and prepare blood products.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Correct coagulopathy and prepare blood products. This is placed here because post-cardiac surgery tamponade may present with low drain output due to clot obstruction.

options.text: After decompression, reassess ventricular function and bleeding source.
options.rationale: Priority 6. After decompression, reassess ventricular function and bleeding source. This is placed here because post-cardiac surgery tamponade may present with low drain output due to clot obstruction.

management_logic: Post-cardiac surgery tamponade may present with low drain output due to clot obstruction.
learning_point: After cardiac surgery, a quiet drain can be the loudest warning.
evidence_basis: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Air in left heart after open cardiac procedure
topic: Air in left heart after open cardiac procedure
source: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: After valve surgery, TEE shows intracardiac air and ST changes occur during separation from CPB.

options.text: Alert surgeon and perfusionist; delay full separation from CPB.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Alert surgeon and perfusionist; delay full separation from CPB. This is placed here because intracardiac air can embolize coronaries or brain; TEE-guided de-airing is critical.

options.text: Position and de-air heart according to surgical plan.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Position and de-air heart according to surgical plan. This is placed here because intracardiac air can embolize coronaries or brain; TEE-guided de-airing is critical.

options.text: Maintain adequate perfusion pressure and avoid ejecting air into coronaries or brain.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Maintain adequate perfusion pressure and avoid ejecting air into coronaries or brain. This is placed here because intracardiac air can embolize coronaries or brain; TEE-guided de-airing is critical.

options.text: Use TEE to guide de-airing and confirm reduction of air.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Use TEE to guide de-airing and confirm reduction of air. This is placed here because intracardiac air can embolize coronaries or brain; TEE-guided de-airing is critical.

options.text: Treat ischemia and arrhythmias while supporting circulation.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Treat ischemia and arrhythmias while supporting circulation. This is placed here because intracardiac air can embolize coronaries or brain; TEE-guided de-airing is critical.

options.text: Continue postoperative neurologic and myocardial monitoring.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Continue postoperative neurologic and myocardial monitoring. This is placed here because intracardiac air can embolize coronaries or brain; TEE-guided de-airing is critical.

management_logic: Intracardiac air can embolize coronaries or brain; TEE-guided de-airing is critical.
learning_point: In cardiac surgery, tiny bubbles can cause large ischemia.
evidence_basis: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: TEE probe insertion risk in esophageal varices
topic: TEE probe insertion risk in esophageal varices
source: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: A cirrhotic patient with known large esophageal varices is scheduled for cardiac surgery where TEE is requested.

options.text: Identify varices as a major TEE risk before induction.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Identify varices as a major TEE risk before induction. This is placed here because tEE is valuable but not risk-free; esophageal pathology changes the monitoring plan.

options.text: Discuss risk-benefit with surgeon and cardiologist; consider alternatives or modified limited exam.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Discuss risk-benefit with surgeon and cardiologist; consider alternatives or modified limited exam. This is placed here because tEE is valuable but not risk-free; esophageal pathology changes the monitoring plan.

options.text: Ensure blood availability and plan for bleeding if TEE is essential.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ensure blood availability and plan for bleeding if TEE is essential. This is placed here because tEE is valuable but not risk-free; esophageal pathology changes the monitoring plan.

options.text: Avoid traumatic insertion and repeated manipulation if proceeding.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Avoid traumatic insertion and repeated manipulation if proceeding. This is placed here because tEE is valuable but not risk-free; esophageal pathology changes the monitoring plan.

options.text: Monitor for oropharyngeal or GI bleeding after insertion.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Monitor for oropharyngeal or GI bleeding after insertion. This is placed here because tEE is valuable but not risk-free; esophageal pathology changes the monitoring plan.

options.text: Document indication, risk discussion, and exam limitations.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Document indication, risk discussion, and exam limitations. This is placed here because tEE is valuable but not risk-free; esophageal pathology changes the monitoring plan.

management_logic: TEE is valuable but not risk-free; esophageal pathology changes the monitoring plan.
learning_point: A monitor that can harm the patient needs an indication.
evidence_basis: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e

title: Hemorrhagic shock trauma laparotomy
topic: Hemorrhagic shock trauma laparotomy
source: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: A polytrauma patient arrives hypotensive for damage-control laparotomy after positive FAST.

options.text: Activate trauma massive transfusion and call senior help before induction.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Activate trauma massive transfusion and call senior help before induction. This is placed here because trauma anesthesia is hemorrhage-control resuscitation with an airway plan.

options.text: Prioritize hemorrhage control and communicate damage-control plan with surgeon.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Prioritize hemorrhage control and communicate damage-control plan with surgeon. This is placed here because trauma anesthesia is hemorrhage-control resuscitation with an airway plan.

options.text: Use hemodynamically cautious RSI with vasopressor and blood ready.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Use hemodynamically cautious RSI with vasopressor and blood ready. This is placed here because trauma anesthesia is hemorrhage-control resuscitation with an airway plan.

options.text: Transfuse balanced blood products and treat calcium, temperature, and acidosis.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Transfuse balanced blood products and treat calcium, temperature, and acidosis. This is placed here because trauma anesthesia is hemorrhage-control resuscitation with an airway plan.

options.text: Avoid excessive crystalloid and permissive hypotension only if no severe TBI.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Avoid excessive crystalloid and permissive hypotension only if no severe TBI. This is placed here because trauma anesthesia is hemorrhage-control resuscitation with an airway plan.

options.text: Transfer to ICU with damage-control resuscitation plan.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU with damage-control resuscitation plan. This is placed here because trauma anesthesia is hemorrhage-control resuscitation with an airway plan.

management_logic: Trauma anesthesia is hemorrhage-control resuscitation with an airway plan.
learning_point: In bleeding trauma, induction is a controlled cardiovascular insult.
evidence_basis: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e

title: Severe head injury with aspiration risk
topic: Severe head injury with aspiration risk
source: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: A head-injured patient with vomiting and GCS 7 needs intubation before CT.

options.text: Call for skilled airway help and prepare suction, RSI drugs, and cervical spine stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for skilled airway help and prepare suction, RSI drugs, and cervical spine stabilization. This is placed here because tBI airway management prioritizes oxygenation, perfusion, aspiration prevention, and C-spine care.

options.text: Preoxygenate while maintaining cervical spine precautions and head-up position if possible.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Preoxygenate while maintaining cervical spine precautions and head-up position if possible. This is placed here because tBI airway management prioritizes oxygenation, perfusion, aspiration prevention, and C-spine care.

options.text: Perform RSI avoiding hypoxia, hypotension, and large ICP surges.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Perform RSI avoiding hypoxia, hypotension, and large ICP surges. This is placed here because tBI airway management prioritizes oxygenation, perfusion, aspiration prevention, and C-spine care.

options.text: Confirm tube with waveform capnography and secure cervical alignment.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Confirm tube with waveform capnography and secure cervical alignment. This is placed here because tBI airway management prioritizes oxygenation, perfusion, aspiration prevention, and C-spine care.

options.text: Ventilate to normocapnia and maintain blood pressure for cerebral perfusion.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Ventilate to normocapnia and maintain blood pressure for cerebral perfusion. This is placed here because tBI airway management prioritizes oxygenation, perfusion, aspiration prevention, and C-spine care.

options.text: Proceed to CT/OR with sedation, analgesia, and neuroprotective targets.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Proceed to CT/OR with sedation, analgesia, and neuroprotective targets. This is placed here because tBI airway management prioritizes oxygenation, perfusion, aspiration prevention, and C-spine care.

management_logic: TBI airway management prioritizes oxygenation, perfusion, aspiration prevention, and C-spine care.
learning_point: The injured brain is intolerant of hypoxia and hypotension during intubation.
evidence_basis: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e

title: Pregnant trauma patient for laparotomy
topic: Pregnant trauma patient for laparotomy
source: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: A 28-week pregnant trauma patient has hypotension and suspected intraabdominal bleeding.

options.text: Call trauma, obstetric, neonatal, anesthesia, and blood bank teams.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call trauma, obstetric, neonatal, anesthesia, and blood bank teams. This is placed here because maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation; left uterine displacement is immediate.

options.text: Apply left uterine displacement and give high-flow oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Apply left uterine displacement and give high-flow oxygen. This is placed here because maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation; left uterine displacement is immediate.

options.text: Activate massive transfusion and prepare damage-control laparotomy.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Activate massive transfusion and prepare damage-control laparotomy. This is placed here because maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation; left uterine displacement is immediate.

options.text: Induce with aspiration and difficult obstetric airway precautions after resuscitation begins.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Induce with aspiration and difficult obstetric airway precautions after resuscitation begins. This is placed here because maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation; left uterine displacement is immediate.

options.text: Monitor fetal status if feasible but prioritize maternal resuscitation.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Monitor fetal status if feasible but prioritize maternal resuscitation. This is placed here because maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation; left uterine displacement is immediate.

options.text: Plan postoperative ICU and obstetric monitoring.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan postoperative ICU and obstetric monitoring. This is placed here because maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation; left uterine displacement is immediate.

management_logic: Maternal resuscitation is fetal resuscitation; left uterine displacement is immediate.
learning_point: In pregnant trauma, save the mother to save the fetus.
evidence_basis: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e

title: Burn inhalation injury with progressive airway edema
topic: Burn inhalation injury with progressive airway edema
source: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: A burn patient with facial burns, soot in mouth, hoarseness, and stridor arrives for debridement.

options.text: Call experienced airway and surgical airway help early.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call experienced airway and surgical airway help early. This is placed here because inhalation injury airway can deteriorate rapidly; early intubation prevents impossible late airway.

options.text: Give high-flow oxygen and assess for carbon monoxide or cyanide toxicity.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give high-flow oxygen and assess for carbon monoxide or cyanide toxicity. This is placed here because inhalation injury airway can deteriorate rapidly; early intubation prevents impossible late airway.

options.text: Secure airway early with cuffed endotracheal tube before edema progresses.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Secure airway early with cuffed endotracheal tube before edema progresses. This is placed here because inhalation injury airway can deteriorate rapidly; early intubation prevents impossible late airway.

options.text: Use video/fiberoptic technique if safe; prepare front-of-neck access.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Use video/fiberoptic technique if safe; prepare front-of-neck access. This is placed here because inhalation injury airway can deteriorate rapidly; early intubation prevents impossible late airway.

options.text: Start burn resuscitation, warming, and analgesia.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Start burn resuscitation, warming, and analgesia. This is placed here because inhalation injury airway can deteriorate rapidly; early intubation prevents impossible late airway.

options.text: Plan ICU ventilation and bronchoscopy as indicated.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan ICU ventilation and bronchoscopy as indicated. This is placed here because inhalation injury airway can deteriorate rapidly; early intubation prevents impossible late airway.

management_logic: Inhalation injury airway can deteriorate rapidly; early intubation prevents impossible late airway.
learning_point: Hoarseness after facial burns is an airway warning.
evidence_basis: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e

title: Cervical spine injury airway for emergency fixation
topic: Cervical spine injury airway for emergency fixation
source: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: A patient with unstable cervical spine injury requires emergency surgery and has limited mouth opening.

options.text: Call skilled airway team and maintain manual in-line stabilization.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call skilled airway team and maintain manual in-line stabilization. This is placed here because airway management must minimize cervical movement and preserve spinal cord perfusion.

options.text: Choose awake or asleep technique based on cooperation, urgency, and airway assessment.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Choose awake or asleep technique based on cooperation, urgency, and airway assessment. This is placed here because airway management must minimize cervical movement and preserve spinal cord perfusion.

options.text: Avoid neck movement and prepare videolaryngoscope/fiberoptic and surgical airway backup.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Avoid neck movement and prepare videolaryngoscope/fiberoptic and surgical airway backup. This is placed here because airway management must minimize cervical movement and preserve spinal cord perfusion.

options.text: Maintain spinal cord perfusion pressure and avoid hypotension.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Maintain spinal cord perfusion pressure and avoid hypotension. This is placed here because airway management must minimize cervical movement and preserve spinal cord perfusion.

options.text: Document neurologic status before and after airway management.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Document neurologic status before and after airway management. This is placed here because airway management must minimize cervical movement and preserve spinal cord perfusion.

options.text: Use careful extubation plan with collar and swelling assessment.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Use careful extubation plan with collar and swelling assessment. This is placed here because airway management must minimize cervical movement and preserve spinal cord perfusion.

management_logic: Airway management must minimize cervical movement and preserve spinal cord perfusion.
learning_point: In C-spine injury, hypotension can harm the cord as much as movement.
evidence_basis: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e

title: MRI sedation desaturation in child
topic: MRI sedation desaturation in child
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e
presentation: A sedated child in MRI becomes desaturated and the airway cannot be accessed quickly.

options.text: Stop scan and call for help while initiating MRI-safe emergency process.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop scan and call for help while initiating MRI-safe emergency process. This is placed here because remote sedation emergencies require access to the patient before definitive airway maneuvers.

options.text: Remove patient from bore to accessible safe area.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Remove patient from bore to accessible safe area. This is placed here because remote sedation emergencies require access to the patient before definitive airway maneuvers.

options.text: Open airway, suction if needed, and provide 100% oxygen with MRI-compatible equipment.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Open airway, suction if needed, and provide 100% oxygen with MRI-compatible equipment. This is placed here because remote sedation emergencies require access to the patient before definitive airway maneuvers.

options.text: Support ventilation with bag-mask and place airway adjunct.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Support ventilation with bag-mask and place airway adjunct. This is placed here because remote sedation emergencies require access to the patient before definitive airway maneuvers.

options.text: Reverse or lighten sedation if appropriate and evaluate need for intubation.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Reverse or lighten sedation if appropriate and evaluate need for intubation. This is placed here because remote sedation emergencies require access to the patient before definitive airway maneuvers.

options.text: Do not restart scan until cause is corrected and monitoring plan is safe.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Do not restart scan until cause is corrected and monitoring plan is safe. This is placed here because remote sedation emergencies require access to the patient before definitive airway maneuvers.

management_logic: Remote sedation emergencies require access to the patient before definitive airway maneuvers.
learning_point: In MRI, the first airway maneuver may be getting the patient out of the bore.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e

title: Pneumothorax during CT-guided lung biopsy under sedation
topic: Pneumothorax during CT-guided lung biopsy under sedation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e
presentation: During CT-guided lung biopsy with sedation, the patient develops dyspnea, hypoxemia, and pleuritic pain.

options.text: Stop procedure and give high-flow oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop procedure and give high-flow oxygen. This is placed here because cT-guided lung biopsy can rapidly produce pneumothorax; tension signs demand treatment before formalities.

options.text: Call anesthesia, interventional radiology, and resuscitation help.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Call anesthesia, interventional radiology, and resuscitation help. This is placed here because cT-guided lung biopsy can rapidly produce pneumothorax; tension signs demand treatment before formalities.

options.text: Assess for pneumothorax clinically and with immediate imaging already available.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Assess for pneumothorax clinically and with immediate imaging already available. This is placed here because cT-guided lung biopsy can rapidly produce pneumothorax; tension signs demand treatment before formalities.

options.text: If tension physiology appears, decompress immediately without waiting.
options.rationale: Priority 4. If tension physiology appears, decompress immediately without waiting. This is placed here because cT-guided lung biopsy can rapidly produce pneumothorax; tension signs demand treatment before formalities.

options.text: Insert chest drain or aspiration catheter as directed by IR/surgery.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Insert chest drain or aspiration catheter as directed by IR/surgery. This is placed here because cT-guided lung biopsy can rapidly produce pneumothorax; tension signs demand treatment before formalities.

options.text: Monitor until respiratory stability is assured.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Monitor until respiratory stability is assured. This is placed here because cT-guided lung biopsy can rapidly produce pneumothorax; tension signs demand treatment before formalities.

management_logic: CT-guided lung biopsy can rapidly produce pneumothorax; tension signs demand treatment before formalities.
learning_point: A complication in NORA must be managed with OR-level urgency.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e

title: Endoscopy oversedation with hypoventilation
topic: Endoscopy oversedation with hypoventilation
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e
presentation: During upper GI endoscopy under propofol sedation, capnography disappears and SpO2 begins to fall.

options.text: Stop propofol and ask endoscopist to remove or pause scope.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Stop propofol and ask endoscopist to remove or pause scope. This is placed here because loss of capnography during sedation is apnea or obstruction until proved otherwise.

options.text: Open airway with jaw thrust and suction; give 100% oxygen.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Open airway with jaw thrust and suction; give 100% oxygen. This is placed here because loss of capnography during sedation is apnea or obstruction until proved otherwise.

options.text: Assist ventilation with bag-mask if capnography does not return.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Assist ventilation with bag-mask if capnography does not return. This is placed here because loss of capnography during sedation is apnea or obstruction until proved otherwise.

options.text: Insert airway adjunct or supraglottic airway if obstruction persists.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Insert airway adjunct or supraglottic airway if obstruction persists. This is placed here because loss of capnography during sedation is apnea or obstruction until proved otherwise.

options.text: Escalate to intubation if aspiration, laryngospasm, or persistent hypoventilation occurs.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Escalate to intubation if aspiration, laryngospasm, or persistent hypoventilation occurs. This is placed here because loss of capnography during sedation is apnea or obstruction until proved otherwise.

options.text: Review sedation dose, fasting, OSA risk, and recovery monitoring.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Review sedation dose, fasting, OSA risk, and recovery monitoring. This is placed here because loss of capnography during sedation is apnea or obstruction until proved otherwise.

management_logic: Loss of capnography during sedation is apnea or obstruction until proved otherwise.
learning_point: Capnography warns before pulse oximetry fails.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e

title: Pediatric radiotherapy anesthesia with fasting breach
topic: Pediatric radiotherapy anesthesia with fasting breach
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e
presentation: A child scheduled for daily radiotherapy anesthesia drank milk 2 hours ago, but the family requests proceeding.

options.text: Do not proceed with elective anesthesia after milk within unsafe fasting interval.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Do not proceed with elective anesthesia after milk within unsafe fasting interval. This is placed here because repeated short anesthesia still carries aspiration risk when fasting is inadequate.

options.text: Explain aspiration risk and institutional fasting policy to family.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Explain aspiration risk and institutional fasting policy to family. This is placed here because repeated short anesthesia still carries aspiration risk when fasting is inadequate.

options.text: Inform radiation team and reschedule within safe window if clinically acceptable.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Inform radiation team and reschedule within safe window if clinically acceptable. This is placed here because repeated short anesthesia still carries aspiration risk when fasting is inadequate.

options.text: Assess for urgent oncologic exception only with consultant-level decision.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Assess for urgent oncologic exception only with consultant-level decision. This is placed here because repeated short anesthesia still carries aspiration risk when fasting is inadequate.

options.text: Document fasting history and decision.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Document fasting history and decision. This is placed here because repeated short anesthesia still carries aspiration risk when fasting is inadequate.

options.text: Improve pre-procedure fasting communication for future fractions.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Improve pre-procedure fasting communication for future fractions. This is placed here because repeated short anesthesia still carries aspiration risk when fasting is inadequate.

management_logic: Repeated short anesthesia still carries aspiration risk when fasting is inadequate.
learning_point: A routine daily procedure is still anesthesia.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e

title: Contrast reaction in cath lab under anesthesia
topic: Contrast reaction in cath lab under anesthesia
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e
presentation: After contrast injection during interventional cardiology, the anesthetized patient develops hypotension, rash, and bronchospasm.

options.text: Declare anaphylaxis and stop contrast or culprit exposure.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Declare anaphylaxis and stop contrast or culprit exposure. This is placed here because contrast anaphylaxis in NORA follows the same epinephrine-first physiology as OR anaphylaxis.

options.text: Give 100% oxygen and support ventilation for bronchospasm.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Give 100% oxygen and support ventilation for bronchospasm. This is placed here because contrast anaphylaxis in NORA follows the same epinephrine-first physiology as OR anaphylaxis.

options.text: Administer epinephrine promptly according to severity.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Administer epinephrine promptly according to severity. This is placed here because contrast anaphylaxis in NORA follows the same epinephrine-first physiology as OR anaphylaxis.

options.text: Give rapid IV fluids and start vasopressor infusion if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Give rapid IV fluids and start vasopressor infusion if needed. This is placed here because contrast anaphylaxis in NORA follows the same epinephrine-first physiology as OR anaphylaxis.

options.text: Give adjunct antihistamine and steroid after stabilization; send tryptase if feasible.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Give adjunct antihistamine and steroid after stabilization; send tryptase if feasible. This is placed here because contrast anaphylaxis in NORA follows the same epinephrine-first physiology as OR anaphylaxis.

options.text: Plan monitored post-event care and document contrast reaction.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Plan monitored post-event care and document contrast reaction. This is placed here because contrast anaphylaxis in NORA follows the same epinephrine-first physiology as OR anaphylaxis.

management_logic: Contrast anaphylaxis in NORA follows the same epinephrine-first physiology as OR anaphylaxis.
learning_point: Location does not change the anaphylaxis algorithm.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e

title: Retroperitoneal bleed during prone IR procedure
topic: Retroperitoneal bleed during prone IR procedure
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e
presentation: During prone renal intervention, the patient becomes hypotensive and tachycardic with increasing abdominal distension.

options.text: Call for help and tell interventionalist to stop and assess for bleeding.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Call for help and tell interventionalist to stop and assess for bleeding. This is placed here because prone NORA bleeding combines access difficulty and hemorrhagic shock.

options.text: Increase oxygen, support circulation, and prepare vasopressor as bridge.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Increase oxygen, support circulation, and prepare vasopressor as bridge. This is placed here because prone NORA bleeding combines access difficulty and hemorrhagic shock.

options.text: Activate blood availability or massive transfusion if instability persists.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Activate blood availability or massive transfusion if instability persists. This is placed here because prone NORA bleeding combines access difficulty and hemorrhagic shock.

options.text: Decide whether airway must be secured before turning supine.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Decide whether airway must be secured before turning supine. This is placed here because prone NORA bleeding combines access difficulty and hemorrhagic shock.

options.text: Use imaging to identify bleeding and proceed to embolization or surgical control.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Use imaging to identify bleeding and proceed to embolization or surgical control. This is placed here because prone NORA bleeding combines access difficulty and hemorrhagic shock.

options.text: Transfer to ICU with hemorrhage and access-site handover.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Transfer to ICU with hemorrhage and access-site handover. This is placed here because prone NORA bleeding combines access difficulty and hemorrhagic shock.

management_logic: Prone NORA bleeding combines access difficulty and hemorrhagic shock.
learning_point: In prone procedures, airway and bleeding plans must move together.

title: Asystole during electroconvulsive therapy
topic: Asystole during electroconvulsive therapy
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e
presentation: After ECT stimulus, the patient develops prolonged asystole with no pulse.

options.text: Confirm pulselessness and call for resuscitation help.
options.rationale: Priority 1. Confirm pulselessness and call for resuscitation help. This is placed here because brief bradycardia can occur after ECT, but persistent pulseless asystole is cardiac arrest.

options.text: Start chest compressions if asystole persists beyond expected brief pause.
options.rationale: Priority 2. Start chest compressions if asystole persists beyond expected brief pause. This is placed here because brief bradycardia can occur after ECT, but persistent pulseless asystole is cardiac arrest.

options.text: Ventilate with 100% oxygen and secure airway if needed.
options.rationale: Priority 3. Ventilate with 100% oxygen and secure airway if needed. This is placed here because brief bradycardia can occur after ECT, but persistent pulseless asystole is cardiac arrest.

options.text: Give atropine or ACLS medications according to rhythm and protocol.
options.rationale: Priority 4. Give atropine or ACLS medications according to rhythm and protocol. This is placed here because brief bradycardia can occur after ECT, but persistent pulseless asystole is cardiac arrest.

options.text: Evaluate contributing drugs, hypoxia, electrolyte disorder, and cardiac disease.
options.rationale: Priority 5. Evaluate contributing drugs, hypoxia, electrolyte disorder, and cardiac disease. This is placed here because brief bradycardia can occur after ECT, but persistent pulseless asystole is cardiac arrest.

options.text: Cancel further ECT until cardiac review and modified plan are complete.
options.rationale: Priority 6. Cancel further ECT until cardiac review and modified plan are complete. This is placed here because brief bradycardia can occur after ECT, but persistent pulseless asystole is cardiac arrest.

management_logic: Brief bradycardia can occur after ECT, but persistent pulseless asystole is cardiac arrest.
learning_point: Expected physiology stops being expected when there is no pulse.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; Coté and Lerman 6e

title: Neutropenic septic shock after chemotherapy
topic: Neutropenic septic shock after chemotherapy
source: Oncologic Critical Care 2020; Oncologic Emergencies 2022; Febrile Neutropenia Review 2022; Stanford SHC Antimicrobial Dosing Guide 2026
presentation: Fever, confusion, cold peripheries, ANC 380/microL, lactate 5.8, no obvious source.

options.text: Declare sepsis or septic shock, call the ICU senior, and start a bedside sepsis huddle.
options.rationale: Recognition and team activation prevent delay in time-sensitive sepsis care.

options.text: Obtain blood cultures and focused cultures immediately if this does not delay antimicrobial therapy.
options.rationale: Cultures improve targeted treatment but must not delay therapy in unstable patients.

options.text: Administer empiric antimicrobials matched to likely source, host risk, renal function, and local policy.
options.rationale: Early appropriate antimicrobial therapy is the core disease-modifying intervention.

options.text: Support perfusion with reassessed balanced crystalloid and early norepinephrine if MAP remains low.
options.rationale: Shock requires active perfusion restoration while avoiding blind fluid overload.

options.text: Identify and execute source control with the relevant specialty team.
options.rationale: Antibiotics cannot substitute for drainage, debridement, device removal, or surgical control when indicated.

options.text: Reassess lactate, urine output, mental state, capillary refill, cultures, and de-escalation opportunities.
options.rationale: Sepsis care is iterative; response and stewardship require scheduled reassessment.

management_logic: Treat as probable septic shock with high-risk host while source is searched.
learning_point: In neutropenic shock, absence of a visible source is not a reason to wait.
evidence_basis: Oncologic Critical Care 2020; Oncologic Emergencies 2022; Febrile Neutropenia Review 2022; Stanford SHC Antimicrobial Dosing Guide 2026

title: Stable febrile neutropenia needing risk classification
topic: Stable febrile neutropenia needing risk classification
source: Oncologic Critical Care 2020; Oncologic Emergencies 2022; Febrile Neutropenia Review 2022; Stanford SHC Antimicrobial Dosing Guide 2026
presentation: Fever 38.5 C, ANC 700, alert, normotensive, lactate 1.7, no organ dysfunction.

options.text: Declare sepsis or septic shock, call the ICU senior, and start a bedside sepsis huddle.
options.rationale: Recognition and team activation prevent delay in time-sensitive sepsis care.

options.text: Obtain blood cultures and focused cultures immediately if this does not delay antimicrobial therapy.
options.rationale: Cultures improve targeted treatment but must not delay therapy in unstable patients.

options.text: Administer empiric antimicrobials matched to likely source, host risk, renal function, and local policy.
options.rationale: Early appropriate antimicrobial therapy is the core disease-modifying intervention.

options.text: Support perfusion with reassessed balanced crystalloid and early norepinephrine if MAP remains low.
options.rationale: Shock requires active perfusion restoration while avoiding blind fluid overload.

options.text: Identify and execute source control with the relevant specialty team.
options.rationale: Antibiotics cannot substitute for drainage, debridement, device removal, or surgical control when indicated.

options.rationale: Sepsis care is iterative; response and stewardship require scheduled reassessment.

management_logic: Preserve cultures and begin risk-appropriate empiric therapy while avoiding over-triage if truly stable.
learning_point: Febrile neutropenia is not automatically shock, but it is never casual fever.

title: Severe ARDS with PaO2 FiO2 78 on ventilator
topic: Severe ARDS with PaO2 FiO2 78 on ventilator
source: ESICM ARDS Guidelines 2023; Acute Respiratory Support Ventilation and ECMO 2025; ATS Ventilator Troubleshooting Guide
presentation: FiO2 0.9, PEEP 12, PaO2 70, plateau 32, driving pressure 18.

options.text: Call for help, give 100% oxygen, and immediately exclude tube, circuit, secretion, and pneumothorax causes.
options.rationale: Reversible mechanical causes must be excluded before labeling refractory lung disease.

options.text: Apply lung-protective ventilation with tidal volume near 4-6 mL/kg predicted body weight and safe plateau pressure.
options.rationale: Ventilator-induced lung injury worsens ARDS outcomes.

options.text: Individualize PEEP using oxygenation, compliance, driving pressure, and hemodynamic response.
options.rationale: PEEP is beneficial only when it recruits more than it overdistends or destabilizes.

options.text: Start early prone positioning for moderate to severe ARDS if no contraindication.
options.rationale: Prone ventilation is one of the few ARDS interventions with outcome benefit.

options.text: Use analgesia, sedation, and short neuromuscular blockade only if needed to achieve protective ventilation.
options.rationale: Synchrony matters, but paralysis should serve a clear physiologic goal.

options.text: Refer early for ECMO or advanced support if severe hypoxemia or unsafe pressures persist despite optimized care.
options.rationale: ECMO referral should occur before prolonged injurious ventilation or terminal hypoxemia.

management_logic: Start with troubleshooting and lung protection before rescue escalation.
learning_point: Do not rescue oxygenation by destroying the lung.
evidence_basis: ESICM ARDS Guidelines 2023; Acute Respiratory Support Ventilation and ECMO 2025; ATS Ventilator Troubleshooting Guide

title: Sudden desaturation with high airway pressure
topic: Sudden desaturation with high airway pressure
source: Textbook of Critical Care 8e; Marino ICU Book 5e
presentation: SpO2 suddenly 72%, PIP rises 24 to 48, EtCO2 falls.

options.text: Call for help, switch to 100% oxygen, disconnect from the ventilator, and hand ventilate.
options.rationale: This separates patient pathology from ventilator or circuit failure while restoring oxygen delivery.

options.text: Check the endotracheal tube for displacement, obstruction, cuff leak, kinking, biting, or secretions.
options.rationale: Tube problems are common, rapidly fatal, and immediately reversible.

options.text: Check the circuit, filter, valves, water trap, ventilator settings, and oxygen supply.
options.rationale: Equipment failure can mimic sudden patient deterioration.

options.text: Assess the patient for pneumothorax, bronchospasm, aspiration, atelectasis, pulmonary edema, or shock.
options.rationale: After tube and circuit checks, patient causes define treatment.

options.text: Treat the identified cause with suction, bronchodilator, decompression, tube exchange, recruitment, or hemodynamic support.
options.rationale: Specific therapy follows the branch diagnosis.

options.text: Obtain ABG, chest imaging, and prevention plan after immediate stabilization.
options.rationale: Investigations and prevention follow the first-minute rescue sequence.

management_logic: Use oxygenation plus tube-circuit-patient troubleshooting.
learning_point: First disconnect from the machine; then reconnect the diagnosis.
evidence_basis: Textbook of Critical Care 8e; Marino ICU Book 5e

source: ESICM ARDS Guidelines 2023; Acute Respiratory Support Ventilation and ECMO 2025; ATS Ventilator Troubleshooting Guide
presentation: ETT pulled outward 4 cm during nursing care; EtCO2 weak and SpO2 falling.

options.text: Call for help, give 100% oxygen, and immediately exclude tube, circuit, secretion, and pneumothorax causes.
options.rationale: Reversible mechanical causes must be excluded before labeling refractory lung disease.

options.text: Apply lung-protective ventilation with tidal volume near 4-6 mL/kg predicted body weight and safe plateau pressure.
options.rationale: Ventilator-induced lung injury worsens ARDS outcomes.

options.text: Individualize PEEP using oxygenation, compliance, driving pressure, and hemodynamic response.
options.rationale: PEEP is beneficial only when it recruits more than it overdistends or destabilizes.

options.text: Start early prone positioning for moderate to severe ARDS if no contraindication.
options.rationale: Prone ventilation is one of the few ARDS interventions with outcome benefit.

options.text: Use analgesia, sedation, and short neuromuscular blockade only if needed to achieve protective ventilation.
options.rationale: Synchrony matters, but paralysis should serve a clear physiologic goal.

options.text: Refer early for ECMO or advanced support if severe hypoxemia or unsafe pressures persist despite optimized care.
options.rationale: ECMO referral should occur before prolonged injurious ventilation or terminal hypoxemia.

management_logic: Prone airway displacement must be treated as a preplanned emergency.
learning_point: In prone ARDS, the tube is an organ support device.
evidence_basis: ESICM ARDS Guidelines 2023; Acute Respiratory Support Ventilation and ECMO 2025; ATS Ventilator Troubleshooting Guide
