title: Post-extubation stridor with retractions
topic: Post-extubation stridor with retractions
source: DAS/ICS/FICM/RCoA Guidelines for Tracheal Intubation in Critically Ill Adults 2018; Kirtania ICU Intubation Commentary 2026
presentation: Inspiratory stridor 30 minutes after extubation, SpO2 89%, suprasternal retractions.

options.text: Declare physiologically difficult ICU intubation and call the most experienced airway help available.
options.rationale: ICU intubation risk is physiology plus anatomy plus team performance.

options.text: Optimize oxygenation with HFNC, NIV, or two-person mask ventilation while suction and backup devices are prepared.
options.rationale: Preoxygenation and peroxygenation are central because desaturation is common and fast.

options.text: Optimize hemodynamics before induction with vasopressor ready or running and avoid unnecessary apnea.
options.rationale: Hypotension and apnea are major mechanisms of peri-intubation arrest.

options.text: Use a checklist, video laryngoscope if available, bougie, waveform capnography, and a predeclared rescue plan.
options.rationale: Shared mental model and first-pass success reduce complications.

options.text: After intubation, immediately confirm tube position and match ventilation to the patient’s acid-base and lung mechanics.
options.rationale: Post-intubation collapse often occurs after the tube is in, from shock, wrong ventilation, or displacement.

options.text: Document the airway plan, tube depth, fixation, sedation, vasopressor, and extubation risk.
options.rationale: Many ICU airway catastrophes occur after intubation due to displacement, blockage, or poor handover.

management_logic: Medical therapy may temporize, but early reintubation planning is central.
learning_point: A noisy airway can become a silent airway.
evidence_basis: DAS/ICS/FICM/RCoA Guidelines for Tracheal Intubation in Critically Ill Adults 2018; Kirtania ICU Intubation Commentary 2026

title: Massive aspiration in intubated ICU patient
topic: Massive aspiration in intubated ICU patient
source: Textbook of Critical Care 8e; Marino ICU Book 5e
presentation: Large-volume feed aspiration witnessed with copious material in ETT and desaturation.

options.text: Call for help, switch to 100% oxygen, disconnect from the ventilator, and hand ventilate.
options.rationale: This separates patient pathology from ventilator or circuit failure while restoring oxygen delivery.

options.text: Check the endotracheal tube for displacement, obstruction, cuff leak, kinking, biting, or secretions.
options.rationale: Tube problems are common, rapidly fatal, and immediately reversible.

options.text: Check the circuit, filter, valves, water trap, ventilator settings, and oxygen supply.
options.rationale: Equipment failure can mimic sudden patient deterioration.

options.text: Assess the patient for pneumothorax, bronchospasm, aspiration, atelectasis, pulmonary edema, or shock.
options.rationale: After tube and circuit checks, patient causes define treatment.

options.text: Treat the identified cause with suction, bronchodilator, decompression, tube exchange, recruitment, or hemodynamic support.
options.rationale: Specific therapy follows the branch diagnosis.

options.text: Obtain ABG, chest imaging, and prevention plan after immediate stabilization.
options.rationale: Investigations and prevention follow the first-minute rescue sequence.

management_logic: Airway clearance and oxygenation come before antibiotic debate.
learning_point: Suction and oxygen come before culture and debate.
evidence_basis: Textbook of Critical Care 8e; Marino ICU Book 5e

title: Septic AKI with refractory hyperkalemia
topic: Septic AKI with refractory hyperkalemia
source: CRRT Practical Guide 2024; Handbook of Dialysis 5e; SLED in ICU 2022; Blood Gases and Critical Care Testing 3e
presentation: K 6.9, peaked T waves, pH 7.14, urine 5 mL/h.

options.text: Treat immediate electrolyte, acid-base, or volume threat while calling ICU/nephrology support.
options.rationale: Life-threatening hyperkalemia, acidosis, or pulmonary edema cannot wait for full renal workup.

options.text: Stop nephrotoxins and ongoing potassium, acid, or fluid inputs; review drug dosing.
options.rationale: Stopping the injury and unsafe dosing prevents deterioration.

options.text: Assess AKI stage, urine output, volume status, obstruction, sepsis, rhabdomyolysis, and hemodynamics.
options.rationale: The cause and trajectory determine reversibility and RRT need.

options.text: Start temporizing medical therapy where appropriate while preparing definitive renal support.
options.rationale: Medical therapy buys time but may not remove the problem.

options.text: Choose CRRT, SLED, or IHD based on hemodynamic stability, cerebral risk, resources, and solute/volume target.
options.rationale: RRT modality is a physiologic decision, not a default machine choice.

options.text: Monitor electrolytes, acid-base, anticoagulation, drug dosing, circuit function, and nutrition during therapy.
options.rationale: Renal support changes drug clearance, electrolytes, and hemodynamics continuously.

management_logic: Hyperkalemia has a bridge phase and a removal phase.
learning_point: Calcium buys time; dialysis removes the problem.
evidence_basis: CRRT Practical Guide 2024; Handbook of Dialysis 5e; SLED in ICU 2022; Blood Gases and Critical Care Testing 3e

title: Hyperleukocytosis with leukostasis respiratory failure

source: Oncologic Critical Care 2020; Oncologic Emergencies 2022; Febrile Neutropenia Review 2022; Stanford SHC Antimicrobial Dosing Guide 2026
presentation: WBC 280,000, hypoxemia, confusion, retinal hemorrhages; blasts predominant.

options.text: Recognize high-risk oncology ICU presentation and call ICU, oncology/hematology, and infection support early.
options.rationale: Cancer patients deteriorate rapidly and often need disease-specific input.

options.text: Stabilize airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, and bleeding risk before definitive diagnostics.
options.rationale: Immediate physiology determines survival.

options.text: Obtain cultures or targeted tests promptly without delaying empiric treatment in unstable patients.
options.rationale: Microbiology is essential but subordinate to shock or airway stabilization.

options.text: Start empiric therapy appropriate to neutropenia, prior antibiotics, devices, renal function, and local resistance.
options.rationale: Oncology ICU antimicrobial errors are often errors of spectrum, dose, or timing.

options.text: Search for source or complication: catheter, mucositis, abdomen, lung, CNS, tumor lysis, leukostasis, effusion, or therapy toxicity.
options.rationale: Oncologic emergencies are often hidden behind generic sepsis or respiratory labels.

options.text: Reassess for escalation, de-escalation, organ support limits, and disease-directed feasibility with the treating oncology team.
options.rationale: Good oncology ICU care combines critical care reversibility with cancer trajectory.

management_logic: Leukostasis is a time-critical hematologic emergency requiring cytoreduction and organ support.
learning_point: The white count is not just a number when the lungs and brain fail.

options.text: Stabilize airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, and bleeding risk before definitive diagnostics.
options.rationale: Immediate physiology determines survival.

options.text: Obtain cultures or targeted tests promptly without delaying empiric treatment in unstable patients.
options.rationale: Microbiology is essential but subordinate to shock or airway stabilization.

options.text: Start empiric therapy appropriate to neutropenia, prior antibiotics, devices, renal function, and local resistance.
options.rationale: Oncology ICU antimicrobial errors are often errors of spectrum, dose, or timing.

options.text: Search for source or complication: catheter, mucositis, abdomen, lung, CNS, tumor lysis, leukostasis, effusion, or therapy toxicity.
options.rationale: Oncologic emergencies are often hidden behind generic sepsis or respiratory labels.

options.text: Reassess for escalation, de-escalation, organ support limits, and disease-directed feasibility with the treating oncology team.
options.rationale: Good oncology ICU care combines critical care reversibility with cancer trajectory.

management_logic: Support oxygenation and hemostasis while ruling infection and DAH causes.
learning_point: In thrombocytopenia, the lung can bleed silently until oxygenation collapses.
evidence_basis: Oncologic Critical Care 2020; Oncologic Emergencies 2022; Febrile Neutropenia Review 2022; Stanford SHC Antimicrobial Dosing Guide 2026

title: Massive GI bleed with shock
topic: Massive GI bleed with shock
source: Patient Blood Management Guideline for Adults with Critical Bleeding 2023/2025; PBM Critical Care Module 4; PBM Perioperative Module 2
presentation: Bright hematemesis, shock, Hb 5.6, INR 2.1, fibrinogen low.

options.text: Declare critical bleeding, call surgical/procedural, blood bank, anesthesia/ICU, and nursing support.
options.rationale: Major hemorrhage survival depends on coordinated response.

options.text: Control bleeding source immediately with compression, packing, surgery, endoscopy, interventional radiology, or obstetric measures.
options.rationale: Transfusion cannot replace hemorrhage control.

options.text: Activate major hemorrhage protocol and secure large-bore access, rapid infuser, warming, and monitoring.
options.rationale: Early balanced blood component delivery prevents lethal delay.

options.text: Measure and repeatedly correct hemoglobin, platelets, PT/INR, APTT, fibrinogen, ionized calcium, temperature, and acid-base status.
options.rationale: Coagulopathy, hypocalcemia, hypothermia, and acidosis perpetuate bleeding.

options.text: Use antifibrinolytic, fibrinogen, platelets, plasma, RBC, cell salvage, or VHA-guided therapy as indicated.
options.rationale: Hemostatic therapy should match the bleeding phenotype and guideline protocol.

options.text: Plan ICU handover with total blood loss, products, labs, ongoing bleeding risk, and next trigger thresholds.
options.rationale: Major hemorrhage continues after apparent control unless surveillance is explicit.

management_logic: Hemorrhage control and balanced resuscitation must run in parallel.
learning_point: Blood without control is only a temporary pause.
evidence_basis: Patient Blood Management Guideline for Adults with Critical Bleeding 2023/2025; PBM Critical Care Module 4; PBM Perioperative Module 2

title: VHA-guided correction in surgical bleeding
topic: VHA-guided correction in surgical bleeding
source: Patient Blood Management Guideline for Adults with Critical Bleeding 2023/2025; PBM Critical Care Module 4; PBM Perioperative Module 2
presentation: Ongoing oozing, conventional labs delayed, viscoelastic tracing suggests fibrinogen deficit.

options.text: Declare critical bleeding, call surgical/procedural, blood bank, anesthesia/ICU, and nursing support.
options.rationale: Major hemorrhage survival depends on coordinated response.

options.text: Control bleeding source immediately with compression, packing, surgery, endoscopy, interventional radiology, or obstetric measures.
options.rationale: Transfusion cannot replace hemorrhage control.

options.text: Activate major hemorrhage protocol and secure large-bore access, rapid infuser, warming, and monitoring.
options.rationale: Early balanced blood component delivery prevents lethal delay.

options.text: Measure and repeatedly correct hemoglobin, platelets, PT/INR, APTT, fibrinogen, ionized calcium, temperature, and acid-base status.
options.rationale: Coagulopathy, hypocalcemia, hypothermia, and acidosis perpetuate bleeding.

options.text: Use antifibrinolytic, fibrinogen, platelets, plasma, RBC, cell salvage, or VHA-guided therapy as indicated.
options.rationale: Hemostatic therapy should match the bleeding phenotype and guideline protocol.

options.text: Plan ICU handover with total blood loss, products, labs, ongoing bleeding risk, and next trigger thresholds.
options.rationale: Major hemorrhage continues after apparent control unless surveillance is explicit.

management_logic: Point-of-care coagulation can target components when time matters.
learning_point: Treat the coagulation defect you have, not the component you remember.
evidence_basis: Patient Blood Management Guideline for Adults with Critical Bleeding 2023/2025; PBM Critical Care Module 4; PBM Perioperative Module 2

title: VF arrest in ICU after hyperkalemia
topic: VF arrest in ICU after hyperkalemia
source: AHA Adult BLS, ACLS and Post-Cardiac-Arrest Care Guidelines 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e
presentation: Monitor shows VF; K 7.1 earlier; patient pulseless.

options.text: Confirm unresponsiveness and pulselessness, call code, and start high-quality CPR immediately.
options.rationale: Early compressions are the foundation of adult cardiac arrest survival.

options.text: Attach defibrillator/monitor and identify shockable versus non-shockable rhythm.
options.rationale: Rhythm classification determines defibrillation and drug sequence.

options.text: Defibrillate immediately for VF/pVT or continue CPR with epinephrine strategy for non-shockable rhythm.
options.rationale: The first rhythm-specific intervention should not be delayed.

options.text: Manage airway and ventilation without interrupting compressions; confirm advanced airway with waveform capnography.
options.rationale: Ventilation must support CPR without causing pauses or hyperventilation.

options.text: Search and treat reversible causes: hypoxia, hypovolemia, hydrogen ion, hypo/hyperkalemia, hypothermia, tension, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis.
options.rationale: Reversible causes are the route to ROSC in many ICU arrests.

options.text: After ROSC, implement post-arrest care: oxygen/ventilation targets, MAP support, ECG/cath decision, temperature strategy, glucose and neuroprognostication timing.
options.rationale: ROSC is the beginning of post-cardiac-arrest critical care, not the endpoint.

management_logic: Defibrillation and CPR run with hyperkalemia treatment.
learning_point: Shock the rhythm; treat the potassium.
evidence_basis: AHA Adult BLS, ACLS and Post-Cardiac-Arrest Care Guidelines 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e

title: Severe traumatic brain injury with hypotension
topic: Severe traumatic brain injury with hypotension
source: Textbook of Critical Care 8e; ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Trauma Guidelines index
presentation: GCS 6, unequal pupils, SBP 82, suspected head injury.

options.text: Stabilize airway, oxygenation, circulation, glucose, and seizures immediately.
options.rationale: Secondary brain injury begins with hypoxia, hypotension, hypoglycemia, and seizures.

options.text: Call neurocritical care/neurosurgery/neurology and define time of onset or injury.
options.rationale: Specialty treatment windows and surgical decisions are time-sensitive.

options.text: Obtain urgent imaging once the patient is safe for transport.
options.rationale: CT or vascular imaging defines hemorrhage, infarct, mass, hydrocephalus, or trauma.

options.text: Prevent secondary injury with head elevation, neutral neck, normoxia, normocapnia, temperature control, and adequate MAP/CPP.
options.rationale: Physiology control is brain treatment.

options.text: Initiate disease-specific therapy: thrombolysis pathway, reversal, osmotherapy, antiepileptic, antimicrobials, or surgery as indicated.
options.rationale: Definitive neurologic therapy depends on the lesion and timing.

options.text: Plan ICU monitoring, repeat imaging triggers, DVT/nutrition/airway strategy, and family communication.
options.rationale: Neurocritical trajectories change over hours and require explicit surveillance.

management_logic: Prevent secondary brain injury before and during imaging.
learning_point: A low BP is a second hit to the injured brain.
evidence_basis: Textbook of Critical Care 8e; ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Trauma Guidelines index

title: Eclampsia with pulmonary edema
topic: Eclampsia with pulmonary edema
source: Critical Care Obstetrics 7e; PBM Obstetrics and Maternity Module 5; IJA Recent Advances in Obstetric Anaesthesia and Critical Care 2023
presentation: Generalized seizure, BP 190/120, pulmonary edema, SpO2 86%.

options.text: Call obstetric, anesthesia, ICU, neonatology, blood bank, and senior nursing help immediately.
options.rationale: Maternal critical illness requires a coordinated peripartum team.

options.text: Stabilize maternal airway, breathing, circulation, left uterine displacement, and oxygenation first.
options.rationale: Fetal outcome follows maternal oxygen delivery and perfusion.

options.text: Identify the obstetric emergency phenotype: hemorrhage, sepsis, preeclampsia/eclampsia, embolism, cardiac disease, or airway failure.
options.rationale: The diagnosis determines definitive treatment and delivery decision.

options.text: Start disease-specific treatment: uterotonics/MTP, magnesium and BP control, antibiotics/source control, anticoagulation, or cardiac support.
options.rationale: Obstetric physiology modifies but does not replace critical care priorities.

options.text: Decide delivery, procedure, IR, or ICU transfer with obstetric and neonatal teams.
options.rationale: Timing of delivery is a clinical intervention, not only an obstetric endpoint.

options.text: Continue postpartum/ICU surveillance for recurrent bleeding, pulmonary edema, coagulopathy, renal injury, and neurologic complications.
options.rationale: Maternal deterioration often continues after delivery or apparent control.

management_logic: Maternal stabilization, magnesium, BP control and delivery planning must be coordinated.
learning_point: Fetal rescue starts with maternal oxygenation.
evidence_basis: Critical Care Obstetrics 7e; PBM Obstetrics and Maternity Module 5; IJA Recent Advances in Obstetric Anaesthesia and Critical Care 2023

options.rationale: Maternal critical illness requires a coordinated peripartum team.

options.text: Stabilize maternal airway, breathing, circulation, left uterine displacement, and oxygenation first.
options.rationale: Fetal outcome follows maternal oxygen delivery and perfusion.

options.text: Identify the obstetric emergency phenotype: hemorrhage, sepsis, preeclampsia/eclampsia, embolism, cardiac disease, or airway failure.
options.rationale: The diagnosis determines definitive treatment and delivery decision.

options.text: Start disease-specific treatment: uterotonics/MTP, magnesium and BP control, antibiotics/source control, anticoagulation, or cardiac support.
options.rationale: Obstetric physiology modifies but does not replace critical care priorities.

options.text: Decide delivery, procedure, IR, or ICU transfer with obstetric and neonatal teams.
options.rationale: Timing of delivery is a clinical intervention, not only an obstetric endpoint.

options.text: Continue postpartum/ICU surveillance for recurrent bleeding, pulmonary edema, coagulopathy, renal injury, and neurologic complications.
options.rationale: Maternal deterioration often continues after delivery or apparent control.

management_logic: Treat as cardiopulmonary collapse with hemorrhagic coagulopathy.
learning_point: AFE is managed as physiology, not as a lab diagnosis.
evidence_basis: Critical Care Obstetrics 7e; PBM Obstetrics and Maternity Module 5; IJA Recent Advances in Obstetric Anaesthesia and Critical Care 2023

title: Maternal sepsis with fetal distress
topic: Maternal sepsis with fetal distress
source: Critical Care Obstetrics 7e; PBM Obstetrics and Maternity Module 5; IJA Recent Advances in Obstetric Anaesthesia and Critical Care 2023
presentation: Fever, hypotension, pyelonephritis, fetal tachycardia.

options.text: Call obstetric, anesthesia, ICU, neonatology, blood bank, and senior nursing help immediately.
options.rationale: Maternal critical illness requires a coordinated peripartum team.

options.text: Stabilize maternal airway, breathing, circulation, left uterine displacement, and oxygenation first.
options.rationale: Fetal outcome follows maternal oxygen delivery and perfusion.

options.text: Identify the obstetric emergency phenotype: hemorrhage, sepsis, preeclampsia/eclampsia, embolism, cardiac disease, or airway failure.
options.rationale: The diagnosis determines definitive treatment and delivery decision.

options.text: Start disease-specific treatment: uterotonics/MTP, magnesium and BP control, antibiotics/source control, anticoagulation, or cardiac support.
options.rationale: Obstetric physiology modifies but does not replace critical care priorities.

options.text: Decide delivery, procedure, IR, or ICU transfer with obstetric and neonatal teams.
options.rationale: Timing of delivery is a clinical intervention, not only an obstetric endpoint.

options.text: Continue postpartum/ICU surveillance for recurrent bleeding, pulmonary edema, coagulopathy, renal injury, and neurologic complications.
options.rationale: Maternal deterioration often continues after delivery or apparent control.

management_logic: Maternal sepsis bundle precedes delivery decision unless obstetric emergency mandates delivery.
learning_point: The fetus is treated through maternal perfusion first.
evidence_basis: Critical Care Obstetrics 7e; PBM Obstetrics and Maternity Module 5; IJA Recent Advances in Obstetric Anaesthesia and Critical Care 2023

title: Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy with shock
topic: Pulmonary hypertension in pregnancy with shock
source: Critical Care Obstetrics 7e; PBM Obstetrics and Maternity Module 5; IJA Recent Advances in Obstetric Anaesthesia and Critical Care 2023
presentation: Dyspnea, cyanosis, hypotension, 30 weeks pregnant.

options.text: Call obstetric, anesthesia, ICU, neonatology, blood bank, and senior nursing help immediately.
options.rationale: Maternal critical illness requires a coordinated peripartum team.

options.text: Stabilize maternal airway, breathing, circulation, left uterine displacement, and oxygenation first.
options.rationale: Fetal outcome follows maternal oxygen delivery and perfusion.

options.text: Identify the obstetric emergency phenotype: hemorrhage, sepsis, preeclampsia/eclampsia, embolism, cardiac disease, or airway failure.
options.rationale: The diagnosis determines definitive treatment and delivery decision.

options.text: Start disease-specific treatment: uterotonics/MTP, magnesium and BP control, antibiotics/source control, anticoagulation, or cardiac support.
options.rationale: Obstetric physiology modifies but does not replace critical care priorities.

options.text: Decide delivery, procedure, IR, or ICU transfer with obstetric and neonatal teams.
options.rationale: Timing of delivery is a clinical intervention, not only an obstetric endpoint.

options.text: Continue postpartum/ICU surveillance for recurrent bleeding, pulmonary edema, coagulopathy, renal injury, and neurologic complications.
options.rationale: Maternal deterioration often continues after delivery or apparent control.

management_logic: Avoid drops in SVR and rises in PVR; call cardiac-obstetric ICU team.
learning_point: In pulmonary hypertension pregnancy, small physiologic errors are large events.

title: Pediatric septic shock with cold extremities
topic: Pediatric septic shock with cold extremities
source: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e
presentation: Fever, lethargy, CRT 5 seconds, weak pulses, ANC 200.

options.text: Call pediatric ICU help and use weight-based pediatric emergency dosing and equipment.
options.rationale: Children need size-specific equipment, doses, and physiology-aware decisions.

options.text: Stabilize airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, temperature, and seizures.
options.rationale: Pediatric deterioration often presents late and progresses quickly.

options.text: Obtain vascular or intraosseous access early and start condition-specific resuscitation.
options.rationale: Access delay is a common pediatric emergency bottleneck.

options.text: Treat the likely cause: sepsis, bronchiolitis/asthma, pARDS, DKA, trauma, shock, arrhythmia, or metabolic crisis.
options.rationale: Generic stabilization must transition to disease-specific therapy.

options.text: Escalate respiratory, vasoactive, renal, or neurologic support with PICU transfer planning.
options.rationale: Children may require early transfer before maximal deterioration.

options.text: Communicate clearly with caregivers and document weights, doses, response, and escalation triggers.
options.rationale: Pediatric safety depends on accurate dosing, trend documentation, and family communication.

management_logic: Pediatric sepsis needs weight-based access, antibiotics and vasoactive escalation.
learning_point: Children compensate until they stop compensating.
evidence_basis: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e

title: Severe bronchiolitis failing HFNC
topic: Severe bronchiolitis failing HFNC
source: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e
presentation: HFNC at high flow, apnea episodes, rising CO2, poor feeding.

options.text: Call pediatric ICU help and use weight-based pediatric emergency dosing and equipment.
options.rationale: Children need size-specific equipment, doses, and physiology-aware decisions.

options.text: Stabilize airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, temperature, and seizures.
options.rationale: Pediatric deterioration often presents late and progresses quickly.

options.text: Obtain vascular or intraosseous access early and start condition-specific resuscitation.
options.rationale: Access delay is a common pediatric emergency bottleneck.

options.text: Treat the likely cause: sepsis, bronchiolitis/asthma, pARDS, DKA, trauma, shock, arrhythmia, or metabolic crisis.
options.rationale: Generic stabilization must transition to disease-specific therapy.

options.text: Escalate respiratory, vasoactive, renal, or neurologic support with PICU transfer planning.
options.rationale: Children may require early transfer before maximal deterioration.

options.text: Communicate clearly with caregivers and document weights, doses, response, and escalation triggers.
options.rationale: Pediatric safety depends on accurate dosing, trend documentation, and family communication.

management_logic: Apnea and hypercapnia are failure signs even if saturation intermittently improves.
learning_point: A tired infant may look quieter, not better.
evidence_basis: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e

title: Pediatric DKA with cerebral edema warning
topic: Pediatric DKA with cerebral edema warning
source: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e
presentation: Headache, bradycardia, falling GCS during DKA treatment.

options.text: Call pediatric ICU help and use weight-based pediatric emergency dosing and equipment.
options.rationale: Children need size-specific equipment, doses, and physiology-aware decisions.

options.text: Stabilize airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, temperature, and seizures.
options.rationale: Pediatric deterioration often presents late and progresses quickly.

options.text: Obtain vascular or intraosseous access early and start condition-specific resuscitation.
options.rationale: Access delay is a common pediatric emergency bottleneck.

options.text: Treat the likely cause: sepsis, bronchiolitis/asthma, pARDS, DKA, trauma, shock, arrhythmia, or metabolic crisis.
options.rationale: Generic stabilization must transition to disease-specific therapy.

options.text: Escalate respiratory, vasoactive, renal, or neurologic support with PICU transfer planning.
options.rationale: Children may require early transfer before maximal deterioration.

options.text: Communicate clearly with caregivers and document weights, doses, response, and escalation triggers.
options.rationale: Pediatric safety depends on accurate dosing, trend documentation, and family communication.

management_logic: Cerebral edema signs require immediate neuroprotective therapy and fluid review.
learning_point: In pediatric DKA, neurologic change is the red alarm.
evidence_basis: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e

title: Pediatric supraventricular tachycardia with shock
topic: Pediatric supraventricular tachycardia with shock
source: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e
presentation: Narrow-complex tachycardia 260/min, lethargy, poor pulses.

options.text: Call pediatric ICU help and use weight-based pediatric emergency dosing and equipment.
options.rationale: Children need size-specific equipment, doses, and physiology-aware decisions.

options.text: Stabilize airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, temperature, and seizures.
options.rationale: Pediatric deterioration often presents late and progresses quickly.

options.text: Obtain vascular or intraosseous access early and start condition-specific resuscitation.
options.rationale: Access delay is a common pediatric emergency bottleneck.

options.text: Treat the likely cause: sepsis, bronchiolitis/asthma, pARDS, DKA, trauma, shock, arrhythmia, or metabolic crisis.
options.rationale: Generic stabilization must transition to disease-specific therapy.

options.text: Escalate respiratory, vasoactive, renal, or neurologic support with PICU transfer planning.
options.rationale: Children may require early transfer before maximal deterioration.

options.text: Communicate clearly with caregivers and document weights, doses, response, and escalation triggers.
options.rationale: Pediatric safety depends on accurate dosing, trend documentation, and family communication.

management_logic: Unstable pediatric SVT requires synchronized cardioversion, not prolonged vagal attempts.
learning_point: A child with SVT and shock has no time for rituals.
evidence_basis: ESPNIC Children’s Intensive Care Textbook 2025; Textbook of Critical Care 8e

title: Early ICU nutrition in vasopressor-dependent shock
topic: Early ICU nutrition in vasopressor-dependent shock
source: BMJ Nutritional Support in the ICU 2024; Textbook of Critical Care 8e
presentation: High-dose norepinephrine, lactate rising, enteral feed order for full calories.

options.text: Assess phase of critical illness, nutrition risk, shock status, gut function, and aspiration risk.
options.rationale: Nutrition strategy differs between acute shock and recovery.

options.text: Avoid aggressive early full feeding during unstable acute critical illness.
options.rationale: Recent evidence does not support one-size-fits-all early aggressive macronutrients.

options.text: Start enteral nutrition when hemodynamically acceptable and gut access is safe.
options.rationale: Enteral feeding supports gut function when shock and aspiration risk are controlled.

options.text: Set protein and calorie targets according to trajectory, renal replacement therapy, obesity, wounds, and rehabilitation plan.
options.rationale: Needs vary substantially by patient phenotype and ICU phase.

options.text: Monitor intolerance, refeeding risk, glucose, electrolytes, triglycerides, bowel function, and interruptions.
options.rationale: Nutrition complications can be clinically important.

options.text: Integrate nutrition with mobilization, delirium reduction, and recovery planning.
options.rationale: Nutrition alone does not restore muscle without rehabilitation and recovery support.

management_logic: Nutrition should be phase-aware and not aggressive during unstable shock.
learning_point: Calories are not resuscitation fluid.
evidence_basis: BMJ Nutritional Support in the ICU 2024; Textbook of Critical Care 8e

title: Severe pain after laparotomy despite prescribed analgesia
topic: Severe pain after laparotomy despite prescribed analgesia
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: She reports 9/10 incisional pain, refuses deep breathing, and has not mobilised. Regular paracetamol was charted but last dose was missed; only one rescue opioid dose was given overnight.
constraints: Pain at movement is limiting recovery

options.text: Assess pain at rest and movement using NRS plus functional impact
options.rationale: Functional pain scoring identifies whether analgesia is adequate for breathing, coughing, physiotherapy, and mobilisation.

options.text: Review administered analgesics, missed doses, contraindications, and side effects over the last 24 hours
options.rationale: A prescription cannot be judged without checking what was actually administered.

options.text: Reinstate regular paracetamol and NSAID/COX-2 inhibitor if safe
options.rationale: Simple analgesics are the backbone of multimodal analgesia and reduce opioid requirement.

options.text: Give titrated immediate rescue opioid with monitoring until she can cough and breathe deeply
options.rationale: Severe pain needs prompt rescue analgesia while definitive regular analgesia is corrected.

options.text: Escalate to APS if pain remains severe or opioid requirement rises
options.rationale: Persistent severe pain after basic correction suggests need for specialist review or regional technique.

options.text: Document a functional analgesia goal and reassess after intervention
options.rationale: Pain relief should be tied to recovery targets, not only a number.

management_logic: Initial acute pain review starts with assessment, medication reconciliation, and restoration of missed multimodal analgesia before assuming treatment failure.
learning_point: A high pain score after surgery is often a system failure: missed regular doses, no functional assessment, and delayed rescue analgesia.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Pain score low at rest but unable to cough
topic: Pain score low at rest but unable to cough
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: He says pain is 3/10 lying still but becomes 9/10 on coughing. He avoids spirometry and has basal atelectasis on morning review.
constraints: Resting pain score underestimates clinical risk

options.text: Assess dynamic pain during cough, deep breathing, sitting, and mobilisation
options.rationale: Functional pain assessment detects analgesic failure hidden by acceptable resting scores.

options.text: Check respiratory status and exclude evolving pulmonary complication
options.rationale: Pain-limited ventilation can cause atelectasis and pneumonia, but hypoxemia also needs assessment.

options.text: Optimize regular multimodal analgesia and time doses before physiotherapy
options.rationale: Analgesia should be planned around activity, not delivered only after pain becomes severe.

options.text: Consider regional or neuraxial rescue if systemic analgesia is inadequate or respiratory risk is high
options.rationale: Upper abdominal pain limiting ventilation is a strong indication to consider technique escalation.

options.text: Coordinate with physiotherapy and nursing for a reassessment after analgesic peak effect
options.rationale: The intervention is successful only if it restores cough and mobilisation.

options.text: Record rest and movement scores separately in the pain chart
options.rationale: Separating rest and dynamic pain prevents false reassurance.

management_logic: Dynamic pain, not resting pain alone, determines postoperative respiratory and mobility risk.
learning_point: Ask whether the patient can cough and move; the answer may matter more than a resting pain score.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Sudden new severe calf pain after tibial fixation
topic: Sudden new severe calf pain after tibial fixation
source: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
demographics: 29M after intramedullary nail for tibial fracture
presentation: Six hours postoperatively he develops escalating deep calf pain despite opioids. Pain is worse on passive toe stretch. The leg feels tense.

options.text: Treat this as suspected acute compartment syndrome and call the operating surgeon immediately
options.rationale: Escalating pain out of proportion with passive stretch is limb-threatening until proved otherwise.

options.text: Do a focused neurovascular and compartment assessment without delaying surgical review
options.rationale: Pulses may remain present; clinical suspicion matters.

options.text: Avoid masking the diagnosis with escalating sedative opioid boluses alone
options.rationale: Analgesia should not obscure progressive compartment syndrome.

options.text: Prepare for urgent compartment pressure measurement or fasciotomy according to local pathway
options.rationale: Definitive management is surgical decompression when suspected/confirmed.

options.text: Provide monitored titrated analgesia while diagnostic and surgical steps proceed
options.rationale: Severe pain still deserves treatment, but not at the cost of delayed fasciotomy.

options.text: Document time of symptom recognition, surgeon notification, and serial findings
options.rationale: Timing is critical for limb salvage and medicolegal audit.

management_logic: Severe escalating limb pain after fracture fixation is a surgical emergency first and a pain problem second.
learning_point: Pain out of proportion after limb trauma is compartment syndrome until proved otherwise.
evidence_basis: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Dense leg weakness with epidural infusion
topic: Dense leg weakness with epidural infusion
source: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
demographics: 57F POD1 after open colorectal surgery with thoracic epidural
presentation: Nurse reports increasing bilateral leg weakness and numbness. Pain is well controlled. She received LMWH prophylaxis last evening.

options.text: Stop the epidural infusion immediately and assess motor block level
options.rationale: New dense motor block with epidural requires urgent evaluation.

options.text: Call anesthesia/APS urgently and perform documented neurological examination
options.rationale: Time-critical differentiation of high block, drug effect, hematoma, or abscess is needed.

options.text: Check anticoagulation timing, platelet count, coagulation profile, fever, back pain, and bladder function
options.rationale: Risk factors and associated findings guide urgency and imaging.

options.text: Arrange urgent MRI spine and neurosurgical discussion if weakness persists or red flags exist
options.rationale: Epidural hematoma or abscess requires rapid diagnosis and decompression.

options.text: Provide alternative systemic analgesia while epidural is stopped
options.rationale: Analgesia must continue without worsening the suspected neuraxial complication.

options.text: Do serial neurological observations until block resolves or definitive diagnosis is made
options.rationale: Improvement after stopping infusion supports drug effect; non-resolution is dangerous.

management_logic: A painless patient with new motor block can be more urgent than a patient in pain.
learning_point: New motor weakness with an epidural is an emergency, not an acceptable side effect.
evidence_basis: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Postoperative pain with hypotension and abdominal distension
topic: Postoperative pain with hypotension and abdominal distension
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: He complains of severe abdominal pain despite epidural analgesia. Abdomen is distended, drain output is increasing, and BP is falling.

options.text: Call surgeon and activate immediate postoperative complication assessment
options.rationale: Pain plus shock after abdominal surgery suggests bleeding or leak until proved otherwise.

options.text: Assess ABC, perfusion, drain output, abdomen, urine output, and mental state
options.rationale: Physiology must be assessed before increasing analgesics.

options.text: Start resuscitation with oxygen, IV access, fluids/blood as indicated, and urgent labs
options.rationale: Shock treatment should run while diagnosis is clarified.

options.text: Pause or reduce neuraxial sympatholytic infusion if contributing to hypotension
options.rationale: Epidural-induced vasodilation can worsen shock physiology.

options.text: Give carefully titrated IV analgesia only after life-threatening causes are being addressed
options.rationale: Analgesia is appropriate but should not delay source diagnosis.

options.text: Document pain pattern and escalation timeline for surgical handover
options.rationale: Trend and timing are key to early recognition of complications.

management_logic: In postoperative pain, a change in physiology changes the problem from analgesia to diagnosis and resuscitation.
learning_point: Do not treat pain in isolation when hypotension, distension, or bleeding is present.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Inadequate pain assessment in delirious elderly patient
topic: Inadequate pain assessment in delirious elderly patient
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management
presentation: She is confused, grimacing, tachycardic, and refusing physiotherapy. She cannot reliably use a numeric pain score.

options.text: Use an observational pain tool and assess behaviour during movement
options.rationale: Validated observational scales are needed when self-report is unreliable.

options.text: Look for reversible contributors: urinary retention, pressure injury, tight dressing, fracture complication, infection, hypoxia
options.rationale: Agitation in an older patient may reflect pain or another treatable problem.

options.text: Give regular simple analgesia if safe and avoid leaving analgesia only as PRN
options.rationale: Confused patients may not request analgesia reliably.

options.text: Use low-dose titrated opioid only if needed with sedation and respiratory monitoring
options.rationale: Older patients require cautious dosing and close observation.

options.text: Coordinate analgesia timing with mobilisation and nursing care
options.rationale: Function-based treatment reduces immobility complications.

options.text: Review daily for de-escalation and delirium-minimizing strategy
options.rationale: Pain treatment should support cognition as well as mobility.

management_logic: Pain assessment must adapt to the patient; inability to self-score is not absence of pain.
learning_point: In delirium, behaviour may be the pain score.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Painful dressing with no procedural analgesia plan
topic: Painful dressing with no procedural analgesia plan
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: Daily dressing causes severe pain and panic. Nurses report he refuses dressing changes after a previous traumatic experience.

options.text: Assess baseline pain, procedural pain, anxiety, and previous analgesic response
options.rationale: Procedure pain needs separate assessment from background pain.

options.text: Plan pre-emptive analgesia with timing matched to drug onset before dressing starts
options.rationale: Analgesia after the procedure begins is too late.

options.text: Use non-pharmacologic measures: explanation, consent, positioning, pause signals, and gentle technique
options.rationale: Patient control and communication reduce fear-driven amplification.

options.text: Add short-acting opioid or regional/local technique if simple measures are inadequate
options.rationale: Severe repeated procedural pain needs escalation.

options.text: Coordinate wound team, nurse, and prescriber so the same plan is used daily
options.rationale: Reliability prevents repeated trauma.

options.text: Record an individualized dressing analgesia protocol
options.rationale: The next team should not reinvent or omit the plan.

management_logic: Background and procedural pain are different entities; repeated dressing pain deserves a timed protocol.
learning_point: A painful procedure should have an analgesic prescription, not just sympathy.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Pain score 10 after day-care surgery discharge call
topic: Pain score 10 after day-care surgery discharge call
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: She calls the ward at night with severe shoulder-tip and abdominal pain, repeated vomiting, and inability to keep oral tablets down.

options.text: Advise immediate clinical reassessment rather than remote opioid escalation
options.rationale: Severe pain with vomiting and tachycardia after discharge may indicate complication.

options.text: Screen for red flags: fever, syncope, bleeding, distension, dyspnea, chest pain, reduced urine
options.rationale: Remote triage must identify danger before analgesic advice.

options.text: Direct patient to emergency/return pathway and inform surgical team
options.rationale: Clear pathway avoids delay.

options.text: When assessed, treat dehydration, nausea, and pain with monitored parenteral therapy as needed
options.rationale: Vomiting prevents oral analgesia and may worsen physiology.

options.text: Review discharge analgesic plan and rescue instructions after complication is excluded
options.rationale: The outpatient plan may need adjustment.

options.text: Document the call, advice, red flags, and escalation time
options.rationale: Telephone pain triage is clinically and legally important.

management_logic: Severe pain after discharge is not automatically undertreatment; it may be a surgical or medical complication.
learning_point: Do not prescribe stronger opioids remotely for severe postoperative pain with systemic symptoms.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Pain worsens after initial improvement
topic: Pain worsens after initial improvement
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: Pain was improving but today is worse, diffuse, and associated with fever and tachycardia. He asks for stronger analgesia.

options.text: Treat change in pain trajectory as possible complication and call surgical review
options.rationale: Pain that worsens after improvement is a red flag for infection, leak, bleeding, or ischemia.

options.text: Assess abdomen, wound, drain, bowel function, urine output, and sepsis markers
options.rationale: A focused postoperative complication screen is mandatory.

options.text: Start sepsis/complication workup and resuscitation according to findings
options.rationale: Analgesia escalation alone can delay diagnosis.

options.text: Provide titrated analgesia while diagnostic workup proceeds
options.rationale: Complication assessment and pain relief should run together.

options.text: Avoid NSAID escalation until renal function, bleeding, and anastomotic concerns are reviewed
options.rationale: NSAIDs may be unsafe in selected postoperative complications.

options.text: Document baseline trend and the exact change that triggered escalation
options.rationale: Trend recognition is a core acute pain safety skill.

management_logic: A new pain pattern after surgery is diagnostic data.
learning_point: Do not ignore a broken recovery trajectory.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Setting a realistic pain goal after surgery
topic: Setting a realistic pain goal after surgery
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: She says she expects zero pain and wants to avoid all movement until pain disappears.

options.text: Explain that the goal is tolerable pain enabling breathing, feeding, walking, and infant care
options.rationale: Functional goals are safer and more realistic than zero pain.

options.text: Discuss planned multimodal analgesia and expected timeline of pain improvement
options.rationale: Education improves cooperation and reduces fear.

options.text: Screen for risk factors such as chronic pain, anxiety, opioid use, OSA, and drug allergies
options.rationale: Risk factors should change the plan before pain becomes severe.

options.text: Discuss neuraxial opioid or regional block options according to anesthetic plan
options.rationale: Shared decision-making improves consent and expectation.

options.text: Provide written instructions for rescue analgesia and red-flag symptoms
options.rationale: Safe use after discharge depends on clear advice.

options.text: Document counselling and agreed analgesic plan
options.rationale: The plan should be visible to the perioperative team.

management_logic: Patient education is part of analgesia, especially when pain control must support function.
learning_point: The best pain target is recovery, not numbness.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Severe pain after thoracotomy without epidural
topic: Severe pain after thoracotomy without epidural
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: He cannot cough, has splinting, and systemic opioids cause sedation before adequate analgesia.

options.text: Reassess pain phenotype, function, sedation, respiratory risk, and surgical red flags
options.rationale: Treatment must match nociceptive, neuropathic, visceral, or complication-related pain.

options.text: Correct regular multimodal analgesia unless contraindicated
options.rationale: Paracetamol plus NSAID/COX-2 inhibitor where safe reduces opioid burden.

options.text: Give monitored rescue analgesia titrated to function, not unconsciousness
options.rationale: The goal is cough, mobilisation, and participation in care.

options.text: Consider procedure-specific regional or neuraxial analgesia if systemic drugs are inadequate or unsafe
options.rationale: Regional techniques are valuable when pain is severe or opioids cause harm.

options.text: Address adverse effects and barriers: nausea, constipation, sedation, fear, splinting, or physiotherapy timing
options.rationale: Pain relief fails when side effects or logistics are ignored.

options.text: Set a reassessment time and document the functional target
options.rationale: A pain plan needs a feedback loop.

management_logic: Postoperative pain management should be procedure-specific, multimodal, functional, and repeatedly reassessed.
learning_point: Treat the patient’s ability to breathe, cough, move, and sleep—not the pain number alone.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Rebound pain after single-shot interscalene block
topic: Rebound pain after single-shot interscalene block
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: Excellent analgesia ended suddenly at 2 AM with severe shoulder pain and panic.

options.text: Reassess pain phenotype, function, sedation, respiratory risk, and surgical red flags
options.rationale: Treatment must match nociceptive, neuropathic, visceral, or complication-related pain.

options.text: Correct regular multimodal analgesia unless contraindicated
options.rationale: Paracetamol plus NSAID/COX-2 inhibitor where safe reduces opioid burden.

options.text: Give monitored rescue analgesia titrated to function, not unconsciousness
options.rationale: The goal is cough, mobilisation, and participation in care.

options.text: Consider procedure-specific regional or neuraxial analgesia if systemic drugs are inadequate or unsafe
options.rationale: Regional techniques are valuable when pain is severe or opioids cause harm.

options.text: Address adverse effects and barriers: nausea, constipation, sedation, fear, splinting, or physiotherapy timing
options.rationale: Pain relief fails when side effects or logistics are ignored.

title: Opioid-induced respiratory depression after morphine boluses
topic: Opioid-induced respiratory depression after morphine boluses
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: She received repeated IV morphine for pain. Now difficult to rouse with shallow breathing.

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, sedation score, and call for urgent help
options.rationale: Opioid toxicity is first an airway and ventilation problem.

options.text: Stop opioid delivery or remove unsafe opioid source
options.rationale: Continuing opioid exposure worsens respiratory depression.

options.text: Give oxygen, stimulate, support ventilation, and prepare naloxone titration
options.rationale: Ventilation and cautious reversal are lifesaving.

options.text: Identify contributing risks: renal failure, OSA, neuraxial opioid, basal infusion, fever, or co-sedatives
options.rationale: Cause determines recurrence risk.

options.text: Restart analgesia with opioid-sparing and safer opioid strategy after stabilization
options.rationale: Reversal without a pain plan causes distress and sympathetic stress.

options.text: Increase monitoring and document event and prescription changes
options.rationale: Delayed recurrence is possible after naloxone or long-acting opioids.

management_logic: Opioid safety depends on matching drug, dose, route, renal function, sleep-disordered breathing, and monitoring.
learning_point: Opioids rescue pain but can create the emergency; sedation and respiratory rate are vital signs.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: PCA button pressed by family member
topic: PCA button pressed by family member
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
presentation: Family presses PCA while she sleeps because they think she looks uncomfortable. She is now drowsy.

options.text: Reassess pain, opioid exposure, opioid tolerance, side effects, and red flags
options.rationale: Escalation requires a full safety review.

options.text: Continue baseline opioid if dependent and prevent withdrawal
options.rationale: Stopping chronic opioids abruptly worsens pain and physiology.

options.text: Optimize non-opioid multimodal analgesia and regional options
options.rationale: Opioid-sparing strategies are central in tolerant or high-risk patients.

options.text: Use titrated short-acting opioid with monitoring when necessary
options.rationale: Rescue opioids should be individualized and reassessed.

options.text: Avoid unsafe opioid choices for the context, such as codeine in breastfeeding or tramadol with serotonergic drugs
options.rationale: Drug selection must account for metabolism and interactions.

options.text: Create discharge/de-escalation plan and involve APS for complex use
options.rationale: Prevention of persistent opioid harm starts before discharge.

management_logic: Opioid safety depends on matching drug, dose, route, renal function, sleep-disordered breathing, and monitoring.
learning_point: Opioids rescue pain but can create the emergency; sedation and respiratory rate are vital signs.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

options.rationale: Escalation requires a full safety review.

options.text: Continue baseline opioid if dependent and prevent withdrawal
options.rationale: Stopping chronic opioids abruptly worsens pain and physiology.

options.text: Optimize non-opioid multimodal analgesia and regional options
options.rationale: Opioid-sparing strategies are central in tolerant or high-risk patients.

options.text: Use titrated short-acting opioid with monitoring when necessary
options.rationale: Rescue opioids should be individualized and reassessed.

options.text: Avoid unsafe opioid choices for the context, such as codeine in breastfeeding or tramadol with serotonergic drugs
options.rationale: Drug selection must account for metabolism and interactions.

options.text: Create discharge/de-escalation plan and involve APS for complex use
options.rationale: Prevention of persistent opioid harm starts before discharge.

management_logic: Opioid safety depends on matching drug, dose, route, renal function, sleep-disordered breathing, and monitoring.
learning_point: Opioids rescue pain but can create the emergency; sedation and respiratory rate are vital signs.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: PCA excessive sedation with basal infusion
topic: PCA excessive sedation with basal infusion
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
demographics: 66M OSA on PCA with basal infusion
presentation: He is snoring, hard to wake, and has repeated desaturation alarms overnight.

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, sedation score, and call for urgent help
options.rationale: Opioid toxicity is first an airway and ventilation problem.

options.text: Stop opioid delivery or remove unsafe opioid source
options.rationale: Continuing opioid exposure worsens respiratory depression.

options.text: Give oxygen, stimulate, support ventilation, and prepare naloxone titration
options.rationale: Ventilation and cautious reversal are lifesaving.

options.text: Identify contributing risks: renal failure, OSA, neuraxial opioid, basal infusion, fever, or co-sedatives
options.rationale: Cause determines recurrence risk.

options.text: Restart analgesia with opioid-sparing and safer opioid strategy after stabilization
options.rationale: Reversal without a pain plan causes distress and sympathetic stress.

options.text: Increase monitoring and document event and prescription changes
options.rationale: Delayed recurrence is possible after naloxone or long-acting opioids.

management_logic: Opioid safety depends on matching drug, dose, route, renal function, sleep-disordered breathing, and monitoring.
learning_point: Opioids rescue pain but can create the emergency; sedation and respiratory rate are vital signs.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: LAST after TAP block plus wound infiltration
topic: LAST after TAP block plus wound infiltration
source: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
demographics: 44F after abdominal wall block and surgical infiltration
presentation: Soon after block she has tinnitus, metallic taste, agitation, then seizure.

options.text: Recognize the regional analgesia safety issue and assess ABCs, block level, motor function, and pain pattern
options.rationale: Regional techniques can fail, spread, infect, bleed, or cause toxicity.

options.text: Stop or reduce the infusion/injection if toxicity, excessive block, infection, or neurologic complication is suspected
options.rationale: Removing the ongoing cause is the first safety step.

options.text: Call APS/anesthesia and the relevant surgical team early
options.rationale: Regional complications require shared decision-making and timely escalation.

options.text: Treat the immediate physiology or complication: oxygen, vasopressor, lipid protocol, imaging, or surgical review as indicated
options.rationale: The treatment depends on the hazard, not only on the pain score.

options.text: Provide alternative analgesia that does not worsen the suspected complication
options.rationale: Stopping a catheter should not mean abandoning pain relief.

options.text: Document neurological findings, catheter status, drug dose, and reassessment plan
options.rationale: Regional analgesia safety depends on serial documentation.

management_logic: Regional analgesia improves recovery only when catheter function, local anesthetic dose, motor effect, infection, bleeding risk, and red flags are monitored.
learning_point: A working block is not automatically a safe block.
evidence_basis: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

source: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
presentation: Somatic pain improved but deep cramping visceral pain persists.

options.text: Recognize the regional analgesia safety issue and assess ABCs, block level, motor function, and pain pattern
options.rationale: Regional techniques can fail, spread, infect, bleed, or cause toxicity.

options.text: Stop or reduce the infusion/injection if toxicity, excessive block, infection, or neurologic complication is suspected
options.rationale: Removing the ongoing cause is the first safety step.

options.text: Call APS/anesthesia and the relevant surgical team early
options.rationale: Regional complications require shared decision-making and timely escalation.

options.text: Treat the immediate physiology or complication: oxygen, vasopressor, lipid protocol, imaging, or surgical review as indicated
options.rationale: The treatment depends on the hazard, not only on the pain score.

options.text: Provide alternative analgesia that does not worsen the suspected complication
options.rationale: Stopping a catheter should not mean abandoning pain relief.

options.text: Document neurological findings, catheter status, drug dose, and reassessment plan
options.rationale: Regional analgesia safety depends on serial documentation.

management_logic: Regional analgesia improves recovery only when catheter function, local anesthetic dose, motor effect, infection, bleeding risk, and red flags are monitored.
learning_point: A working block is not automatically a safe block.
evidence_basis: Hadzic’s Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management 2019; Waldman Atlas of Pain Management Injection Techniques 4e; Management of acute pain 2025
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Acute postoperative pain in severe OSA
topic: Acute postoperative pain in severe OSA
source: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management
presentation: Pain is severe but oxygen saturation repeatedly drops after opioid doses.

options.text: Identify the population-specific risk before choosing analgesia
options.rationale: Age, pregnancy, OSA, renal disease, communication, tolerance, and addiction alter safety.

options.text: Use the most reliable pain assessment method for this patient
options.rationale: Self-report, faces scale, FLACC, observational tools, or interpreter support may be needed.

options.text: Optimize non-opioid and regional options that are safe for the condition
options.rationale: Multimodal treatment reduces opioid harm but must respect contraindications.

options.text: Use opioids only when needed, titrated to effect with enhanced monitoring or tolerance-adjusted dosing
options.rationale: Both undertreatment and overdose are common in special populations.

options.text: Involve APS, obstetric, pediatric, addiction, renal, or respiratory specialists when risk is high
options.rationale: Complex pain requires the right team.

options.text: Create a written plan for monitoring, rescue doses, side effects, and discharge
options.rationale: Special populations need continuity, not one-time orders.

management_logic: Special populations require individualized analgesia rather than routine postoperative orders.
learning_point: The same pain prescription can be too weak, too toxic, or inappropriate depending on the patient.
evidence_basis: Management of acute pain, Surgery 2025; Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

options.text: Identify the population-specific risk before choosing analgesia
options.rationale: Age, pregnancy, OSA, renal disease, communication, tolerance, and addiction alter safety.

options.text: Use the most reliable pain assessment method for this patient
options.rationale: Self-report, faces scale, FLACC, observational tools, or interpreter support may be needed.

options.text: Optimize non-opioid and regional options that are safe for the condition
options.rationale: Multimodal treatment reduces opioid harm but must respect contraindications.

options.text: Use opioids only when needed, titrated to effect with enhanced monitoring or tolerance-adjusted dosing
options.rationale: Both undertreatment and overdose are common in special populations.

options.text: Involve APS, obstetric, pediatric, addiction, renal, or respiratory specialists when risk is high
options.rationale: Complex pain requires the right team.

options.text: Create a written plan for monitoring, rescue doses, side effects, and discharge
options.rationale: Special populations need continuity, not one-time orders.

management_logic: Special populations require individualized analgesia rather than routine postoperative orders.
learning_point: The same pain prescription can be too weak, too toxic, or inappropriate depending on the patient.

title: Cancer bone pain with impending cord compression
topic: Cancer bone pain with impending cord compression
source: Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management
presentation: New severe thoracic back pain, leg heaviness, and urinary hesitancy.

options.text: Screen for oncologic emergency or reversible cause before analgesic escalation
options.rationale: Cancer pain may signal cord compression, fracture, obstruction, sepsis, hypercalcemia, or airway risk.

options.text: Treat immediate suffering with route-appropriate analgesia and antiemetic/sedation support as needed
options.rationale: Severe cancer pain requires rapid relief using an available route.

options.text: Match analgesic to phenotype: nociceptive, neuropathic, visceral colic, mucositis, or breakthrough pain
options.rationale: Phenotype determines whether opioid alone is inadequate.

options.text: Call oncology/palliative/surgical/radiology team according to the suspected emergency
options.rationale: Definitive treatment may be steroids, imaging, fixation, radiotherapy, drainage, or surgery.

options.text: Review opioid toxicity, renal function, bowel regimen, hydration, and adjuvants
options.rationale: Cancer pain management fails when adverse effects and organ function are ignored.

options.text: Discuss goals of care and document a rescue plan for recurrent pain
options.rationale: The plan must match disease stage and patient priorities.

management_logic: Cancer pain is both a symptom-relief problem and a diagnostic signal for oncologic emergencies.
learning_point: In cancer pain, ask what dangerous process the pain is announcing.
evidence_basis: Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e; Pain Management Secrets 4e; Oxford Handbook of Pain Management
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Thunderclap headache labelled migraine
topic: Thunderclap headache labelled migraine
source: Bonica’s Management of Pain 5e; Waldman Pain Review 2e; Waldman Atlas of Common Pain Syndromes 4e
presentation: Instant worst-ever headache during exertion, vomiting, photophobia, neck stiffness.

options.text: Identify red flags and perform focused neurological/ocular/systemic assessment
options.rationale: Some pain syndromes are emergencies despite appearing as pain clinic diagnoses.

options.text: Escalate immediately to ED/neurology/ophthalmology/spine/infection pathway when red flags are present
options.rationale: Definitive diagnosis prevents blindness, stroke, hemorrhage, paralysis, or suicide.

options.text: Provide phenotype-specific acute analgesia without delaying diagnostic workup
options.rationale: Pain relief and diagnosis should proceed in parallel.

options.text: Avoid inappropriate opioid-only escalation for primarily neuropathic pain
options.rationale: Neuropathic pain often needs adjuvant or disease-specific treatment.

options.text: Plan disease-specific treatment and follow-up after dangerous causes are addressed
options.rationale: Long-term control requires correct syndrome identification.

options.text: Document trigger features, neurological findings, and safety-net advice
options.rationale: Pain pattern and red flags guide future care.

title: Awake fibreoptic plan failing in obstructing supraglottic tumour
topic: Awake fibreoptic plan failing in obstructing supraglottic tumour
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; DAS airway principles; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
demographics: 62M, carcinoma supraglottis, stridor at rest, sitting position
presentation: Topicalized awake fibreoptic intubation attempted. Patient becomes panicky, coughs blood-stained secretions, SpO2 falls to 86 percent, scope view lost. He refuses to lie flat.
constraints: Do not induce paralysis without a rescue airway immediately available

options.text: Announce airway emergency, call for help, stop nonessential activity, and give 100 percent oxygen
options.rationale: Airway crises deteriorate quickly; the first task is team activation and restoration of oxygen delivery.

options.text: Reposition, suction, apply two-person mask technique, and insert oral or nasal airway as appropriate
options.rationale: Most immediate airway failures improve with basic airway optimization before more complex rescue.

options.text: Address the case-specific cause: stop instrumentation, restore spontaneous breathing oxygenation in sitting position, suction blood, and move to awake tracheostomy or inhalational backup plan with ENT scrubbed
options.rationale: The correct rescue step depends on the presenting modifier rather than a fixed airway script.

options.text: Move to the next best airway plan with the highest first-pass likelihood and limit repeated attempts
options.rationale: Repeated attempts worsen bleeding, edema, hypoxemia, and loss of options.

options.text: Confirm ventilation or tracheal placement with sustained waveform capnography and bilateral chest movement
options.rationale: Clinical appearance alone is unreliable during airway rescue.

options.text: Create a written post-event airway plan including extubation strategy, ICU need, and future warning documentation
options.rationale: Many airway deaths occur after the initial rescue when the exit plan is vague.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Airway. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on stop instrumentation, restore spontaneous breathing oxygenation in sitting position, suction blood, and move to awake tracheostomy or inhalational backup plan with ent scrubbed, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Airway, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; DAS airway principles; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

options.text: Move to the next best airway plan with the highest first-pass likelihood and limit repeated attempts
options.rationale: Repeated attempts worsen bleeding, edema, hypoxemia, and loss of options.

options.text: Confirm ventilation or tracheal placement with sustained waveform capnography and bilateral chest movement
options.rationale: Clinical appearance alone is unreliable during airway rescue.

options.text: Create a written post-event airway plan including extubation strategy, ICU need, and future warning documentation
options.rationale: Many airway deaths occur after the initial rescue when the exit plan is vague.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Airway. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on suction stimulus, jaw thrust with cpap, deepen anesthesia, and give succinylcholine early if complete obstruction persists, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.

title: Severe dynamic hyperinflation in COPD after induction
topic: Severe dynamic hyperinflation in COPD after induction
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
demographics: 66M, severe COPD for emergency bowel surgery
presentation: After intubation, PIP 44, expiratory flow does not reach baseline, hypotension develops, EtCO2 rises slowly.

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 100 percent, hand ventilate, and check pulse, chest movement, and capnography
options.rationale: Manual ventilation separates ventilator/circuit failure from patient physiology.

options.text: Inspect the circuit, tube, filter, valve, soda lime, and sampling line for obstruction, leak, or disconnection
options.rationale: Equipment and tube faults are common reversible causes of sudden ventilation failure.

options.text: Treat the dominant physiological problem: disconnect briefly if peri-arrest from auto-PEEP, reduce rate, extend expiratory time, and accept permissive hypercapnia
options.rationale: The pattern of pressure, compliance, ETCO2, and SpO2 determines the correct intervention.

options.text: Ask the surgeon to pause the provoking step, release pneumoperitoneum or stop insufflation if relevant
options.rationale: Some ventilation failures are surgical-mechanical problems that drugs cannot correct.

options.text: Obtain ABG and lung assessment, then reset ventilator strategy to lung-protective and disease-specific targets
options.rationale: Stabilization must be followed by objective reassessment and safer ventilation settings.

options.text: Plan postoperative respiratory monitoring, ICU/HDU care, and handover of the event and residual risk
options.rationale: Late respiratory failure can follow an apparently corrected intraoperative event.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Ventilation. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on disconnect briefly if peri-arrest from auto-peep, reduce rate, extend expiratory time, and accept permissive hypercapnia, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Ventilation, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

title: Profound hypotension after cementing hip arthroplasty
topic: Profound hypotension after cementing hip arthroplasty
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: During cement insertion BP collapses, SpO2 falls, EtCO2 drops, surgeon reports cement pressurization.

options.text: Declare hemodynamic crisis, verify pulse and arterial waveform, call for help, and give 100 percent oxygen
options.rationale: The first response is confirmation of true circulatory collapse and team mobilization.

options.text: Stop or correct the likely trigger and ask the surgeon to pause or control the field when applicable
options.rationale: Persistent trigger exposure makes vasopressors and fluids ineffective.

options.text: Deliver the case-specific circulation treatment: treat bone cement implantation syndrome with 100 percent oxygen, vasopressors/inotrope, volume support, and stop further pressurization
options.rationale: The key therapy must match the mechanism of shock or instability.

options.text: Support perfusion with appropriate vasopressor, inotrope, fluid, or blood while definitive control proceeds
options.rationale: Bridging support preserves coronary, cerebral, and renal perfusion.

options.text: Use ECG, ABG, electrolytes, hemoglobin, lactate, ultrasound or TEE as available to refine diagnosis
options.rationale: Objective data prevents anchoring on the wrong cause of shock.

options.text: Arrange postoperative ICU/HDU transfer and communicate the event, therapy, and unresolved risks
options.rationale: Hemodynamic crises often recur after drapes come down unless the receiving team is prepared.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Hemodynamics. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on treat bone cement implantation syndrome with 100 percent oxygen, vasopressors/inotrope, volume support, and stop further pressurization, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Hemodynamics, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Yao & Artusio 9e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania
