title: Rocuronium anaphylaxis immediately after induction
topic: Rocuronium anaphylaxis immediately after induction
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: Two minutes after rocuronium, BP collapses, wheeze, rash and facial edema appear. Capnography low.

options.text: Stop the suspected agent or infusion, declare the drug-related crisis, and call for help
options.rationale: Early trigger removal is the single most important step in drug reaction management.

options.text: Secure airway, breathing, and circulation with 100 percent oxygen and invasive-level monitoring if needed
options.rationale: Drug crises often combine respiratory and circulatory collapse.

options.text: Give the specific antidote or first-line treatment: epinephrine IV titrated to severity, rapid fluids, bronchodilator, and tryptase sampling after stabilization
options.rationale: Supportive care alone is insufficient when a specific reversal or antidote is required.

options.text: Treat secondary problems such as bronchospasm, acidosis, arrhythmia, hypotension, hyperkalemia, or seizures
options.rationale: Complications may become the immediate cause of arrest even after the trigger is removed.

options.text: Send relevant blood tests and preserve timing details for diagnosis, allergy referral, or audit
options.rationale: Correct labeling prevents future exposure and supports medico-legal documentation.

options.text: Document the reaction, inform patient and team, and update future anesthesia precautions
options.rationale: A drug crisis becomes a recurrent hazard when the allergy or adverse reaction is poorly recorded.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Drug Reactions. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on epinephrine iv titrated to severity, rapid fluids, bronchodilator, and tryptase sampling after stabilization, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Drug Reactions, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.

options.rationale: Maternal oxygenation and perfusion are the primary fetal resuscitation.

options.rationale: Obstetric emergencies require parallel maternal resuscitation and source-specific treatment.

options.text: Use pregnancy-appropriate drugs, airway strategy, aspiration protection, and blood-product support
options.rationale: Pregnancy changes airway, circulation, aspiration risk, and transfusion priorities.

options.text: Coordinate delivery, uterine/surgical control, neonatal plan, and ICU escalation according to response
options.rationale: Definitive obstetric decisions must occur during resuscitation, not after collapse.

options.text: Debrief and document maternal event, fetal status, drugs, blood loss, and postoperative surveillance plan
options.rationale: Obstetric crises require precise handover and family communication.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Obstetric Anesthesia. title: Infant apnea after pyloromyotomy extubation
topic: Infant apnea after pyloromyotomy extubation
source: Smith's Anesthesia for Infants and Children 10e; Coté and Lerman 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: After extubation, recurrent apnea and desaturation occur despite appearing awake. Temperature 35.6.

options.text: Call pediatric anesthesia help, give 100 percent oxygen, and verify weight-based drug doses
options.rationale: Children desaturate rapidly and dosing errors are common during panic.

options.text: Correct the immediate airway, breathing, or circulation problem using age-appropriate equipment
options.rationale: Pediatric crises are often resolved by simple correctly sized interventions.

options.text: Apply the case-specific pediatric treatment: support ventilation, check glucose/electrolytes/temperature, and admit for apnea monitoring
options.rationale: The definitive maneuver must match the child’s physiology and disease.

options.text: Avoid repeated traumatic attempts; escalate early to the most experienced operator and best device
options.rationale: Small airways swell and bleed quickly after repeated instrumentation.

options.text: Reassess glucose, temperature, electrolytes, hemoglobin, blood gas, and hydration after stabilization
options.rationale: Children compensate well until sudden collapse; objective reassessment is mandatory.

options.text: Plan PACU, HDU, or ICU observation and explain the event to parents with written safety advice
options.rationale: Late airway obstruction, apnea, pain, or hypoglycemia may occur after an initial recovery.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Pediatric Anesthesia. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on support ventilation, check glucose/electrolytes/temperature, and admit for apnea monitoring, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.

options.text: Avoid repeated traumatic attempts; escalate early to the most experienced operator and best device

title: Loss of motor evoked potentials during spine surgery
topic: Loss of motor evoked potentials during spine surgery
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Yao & Artusio 9e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: MEPs suddenly lost bilaterally after rod placement. MAP is 58. Hb 8.2. Surgeon asks to wait.

options.text: Protect oxygenation, ventilation, cerebral perfusion, and call for neurosurgical or ENT-specific help
options.rationale: Brain and airway outcomes depend on preventing secondary hypoxia, hypercarbia, and hypotension.

options.text: Check head and neck position, venous drainage, airway security, EtCO2, blood pressure, and anesthetic depth
options.rationale: Basic physiological errors can mimic or worsen neurological crisis.

options.text: Perform the case-specific neuro/ENT step: raise MAP, correct anemia/oxygenation/anesthetic depth, reverse recent surgical maneuver if signals do not recover
options.rationale: The key action depends on whether the crisis is pressure, bleeding, airway contamination, or neurological injury.

options.text: Use targeted pharmacology and ventilation while avoiding hypotension and excessive anesthetic depth
options.rationale: Neuroanesthesia requires correction without sacrificing cerebral perfusion.

options.text: Request imaging, blood tests, evoked-potential review, bronchoscopy, or endoscopy as indicated
options.rationale: Objective localization prevents blind treatment of a neurological or airway event.

options.text: Plan postoperative neurological examination, ICU care, and explicit handover of baseline versus new deficits
options.rationale: Detection of new deficits depends on a documented baseline and planned reassessment.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Neuroanesthesia. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on raise map, correct anemia/oxygenation/anesthetic depth, reverse recent surgical maneuver if signals do not recover, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Neuroanesthesia, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.

title: MRI anesthesia with airway obstruction inside scanner
topic: MRI anesthesia with airway obstruction inside scanner
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; DAS airway principles; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: In scanner bore, capnography becomes obstructed, SpO2 falls to 84. Access to airway is delayed by table position.

options.text: Announce airway emergency, call for help, stop nonessential activity, and give 100 percent oxygen
options.rationale: Airway crises deteriorate quickly; the first task is team activation and restoration of oxygen delivery.

options.text: Reposition, suction, apply two-person mask technique, and insert oral or nasal airway as appropriate
options.rationale: Most immediate airway failures improve with basic airway optimization before more complex rescue.

options.text: Address the case-specific cause: stop scan, remove patient from bore, open airway and ventilate before adjusting sedative infusion
options.rationale: The correct rescue step depends on the presenting modifier rather than a fixed airway script.

options.text: Move to the next best airway plan with the highest first-pass likelihood and limit repeated attempts
options.rationale: Repeated attempts worsen bleeding, edema, hypoxemia, and loss of options.

options.text: Confirm ventilation or tracheal placement with sustained waveform capnography and bilateral chest movement
options.rationale: Clinical appearance alone is unreliable during airway rescue.

options.text: Create a written post-event airway plan including extubation strategy, ICU need, and future warning documentation
options.rationale: Many airway deaths occur after the initial rescue when the exit plan is vague.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in NORA. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on stop scan, remove patient from bore, open airway and ventilate before adjusting sedative infusion, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.

title: Raised ICP with Cushing response before induction
topic: Raised ICP with Cushing response before induction
source: Essentials of Trauma Anesthesia 2e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: Before induction BP 210/110, HR 44, unequal pupils and vomiting. GCS 6.

options.text: Protect oxygenation, ventilation, cerebral perfusion, and call for neurosurgical or ENT-specific help
options.rationale: Brain and airway outcomes depend on preventing secondary hypoxia, hypercarbia, and hypotension.

options.text: Check head and neck position, venous drainage, airway security, EtCO2, blood pressure, and anesthetic depth
options.rationale: Basic physiological errors can mimic or worsen neurological crisis.

options.text: Perform the case-specific neuro/ENT step: maintain cerebral perfusion and avoid hypotension during RSI; hyperosmolar therapy and controlled ventilation after airway secured
options.rationale: The key action depends on whether the crisis is pressure, bleeding, airway contamination, or neurological injury.

options.text: Use targeted pharmacology and ventilation while avoiding hypotension and excessive anesthetic depth
options.rationale: Neuroanesthesia requires correction without sacrificing cerebral perfusion.

options.text: Request imaging, blood tests, evoked-potential review, bronchoscopy, or endoscopy as indicated
options.rationale: Objective localization prevents blind treatment of a neurological or airway event.

options.text: Plan postoperative neurological examination, ICU care, and explicit handover of baseline versus new deficits
options.rationale: Detection of new deficits depends on a documented baseline and planned reassessment.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Neuroanesthesia. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on maintain cerebral perfusion and avoid hypotension during rsi, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Neuroanesthesia, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.

title: Sedation disaster in dental procedure with OSA
topic: Sedation disaster in dental procedure with OSA
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; DAS airway principles; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: After midazolam and fentanyl, airway obstruction and desaturation occur in dental chair. Mouth full of blood and instruments.

options.text: Announce airway emergency, call for help, stop nonessential activity, and give 100 percent oxygen
options.rationale: Airway crises deteriorate quickly; the first task is team activation and restoration of oxygen delivery.

options.text: Reposition, suction, apply two-person mask technique, and insert oral or nasal airway as appropriate
options.rationale: Most immediate airway failures improve with basic airway optimization before more complex rescue.

options.text: Address the case-specific cause: stop procedure, remove instruments, suction, jaw thrust/CPAP, reverse sedation if needed, and transfer to monitored care
options.rationale: The correct rescue step depends on the presenting modifier rather than a fixed airway script.

options.text: Move to the next best airway plan with the highest first-pass likelihood and limit repeated attempts
options.rationale: Repeated attempts worsen bleeding, edema, hypoxemia, and loss of options.

options.text: Confirm ventilation or tracheal placement with sustained waveform capnography and bilateral chest movement
options.rationale: Clinical appearance alone is unreliable during airway rescue.

options.text: Create a written post-event airway plan including extubation strategy, ICU need, and future warning documentation
options.rationale: Many airway deaths occur after the initial rescue when the exit plan is vague.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in ENT/NORA. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on stop procedure, remove instruments, suction, jaw thrust/cpap, reverse sedation if needed, and transfer to monitored care, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In ENT/NORA, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.
evidence_basis: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; DAS airway principles; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

options.text: Announce cardiovascular emergency, verify rhythm and pulse, call for help, and ensure defibrillator availability
options.rationale: Cardiac anesthesia crises can deteriorate into arrest within seconds.

options.text: Stop the provoking stimulus or surgical step and optimize oxygenation, ventilation, preload, and anesthetic depth
options.rationale: Treatable physiological stressors often drive arrhythmia or ischemia.

options.text: Apply the case-specific cardiac intervention: withdraw guidewire, assess pulse, and perform synchronized cardioversion if unstable
options.rationale: Rhythm, device, valve, ventricular function, and ischemia each require different first therapy.

options.text: Use vasoactive support, pacing, cardioversion, defibrillation, or antiarrhythmic therapy according to stability
options.rationale: Electrical or pharmacologic rescue is dictated by perfusion, not by rhythm name alone.

options.text: Use arterial pressure, ECG, ABG, electrolytes, hemoglobin, TEE/echo, and device interrogation when relevant
options.rationale: Cardiac diagnoses need objective confirmation during and after initial rescue.

options.text: Arrange ICU/CCU handover, troponin/ECG follow-up, device review, and documentation of the trigger
options.rationale: Postoperative recurrence and silent myocardial injury require planned surveillance.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Cardiac Anesthesia. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on withdraw guidewire, assess pulse, and perform synchronized cardioversion if unstable, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.

title: Clamp release hypotension after aortic surgery
topic: Clamp release hypotension after aortic surgery
source: Kaplan's Cardiac Anesthesia 8e; Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e
presentation: On unclamping, MAP falls to 38, acidosis and hyperkalemia appear, bleeding controlled.

options.text: Declare hemodynamic crisis, verify pulse and arterial waveform, call for help, and give 100 percent oxygen
options.rationale: The first response is confirmation of true circulatory collapse and team mobilization.

options.text: Stop or correct the likely trigger and ask the surgeon to pause or control the field when applicable
options.rationale: Persistent trigger exposure makes vasopressors and fluids ineffective.

options.text: Deliver the case-specific circulation treatment: anticipate unclamp shock, support with vasopressor, volume/blood, calcium and correction of acidosis/hyperkalemia
options.rationale: The key therapy must match the mechanism of shock or instability.

options.text: Support perfusion with appropriate vasopressor, inotrope, fluid, or blood while definitive control proceeds
options.rationale: Bridging support preserves coronary, cerebral, and renal perfusion.

options.text: Use ECG, ABG, electrolytes, hemoglobin, lactate, ultrasound or TEE as available to refine diagnosis
options.rationale: Objective data prevents anchoring on the wrong cause of shock.

options.text: Arrange postoperative ICU/HDU transfer and communicate the event, therapy, and unresolved risks
options.rationale: Hemodynamic crises often recur after drapes come down unless the receiving team is prepared.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Vascular Anesthesia. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on anticipate unclamp shock, support with vasopressor, volume/blood, calcium and correction of acidosis/hyperkalemia, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Vascular Anesthesia, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.

title: Double-lumen tube malposition after lateral positioning
topic: Double-lumen tube malposition after lateral positioning
source: Hensley's Practical Approach to Cardiothoracic Anesthesia 6e; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Yao & Artusio 9e
presentation: After turning lateral, ventilation pressure rises and SpO2 falls. Bronchoscope confirms bronchial cuff migrated.

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 100 percent, hand ventilate, and confirm lung isolation, tube position, and circuit integrity
options.rationale: Thoracic crises require simultaneous oxygenation and verification of the lung-isolation device.

options.text: Communicate with surgeon and pause the thoracic step that is worsening oxygenation or hemodynamics
options.rationale: Shared-airway and open-chest problems cannot be solved by anesthesia alone.

options.text: Apply the case-specific thoracic intervention: reconfirm and reposition DLT with bronchoscopy after positioning before one-lung ventilation continues
options.rationale: The correct rescue differs for malposition, hypoxemia, hemorrhage, fistula, pneumothorax, and mediastinal compression.

options.text: Use bronchoscopy, two-lung ventilation, CPAP/PEEP, decompression, or isolation strategy according to the mechanism
options.rationale: Thoracic anesthesia rescue depends on rapidly restoring the best available gas exchange.

options.text: Check ABG, airway pressures, EtCO2, hemodynamics, and imaging or ultrasound if diagnosis remains uncertain
options.rationale: Objective reassessment prevents persistence with the wrong lung strategy.

options.text: Plan ICU or HDU care and hand over tube position, lung isolation issues, bleeding, air leak, and oxygenation trend
options.rationale: Thoracic events commonly recur after transfer unless airway and lung details are explicit.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Thoracic Anesthesia. title: Epidural hematoma after anticoagulated catheter removal
topic: Epidural hematoma after anticoagulated catheter removal
source: Hadzic Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
demographics: 68F, epidural after colorectal surgery, LMWH given early
presentation: Six hours after epidural removal, severe back pain and new leg weakness occur.

options.text: Stop the injection or infusion, call for help, and assess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurological status
options.rationale: Regional complications can present as seizure, motor deficit, collapse, or evolving neuraxial compression.

options.text: Remove reversible contributors such as excess local anesthetic, hypotension, anticoagulant timing, or catheter error
options.rationale: Ongoing exposure or unsafe catheter management worsens the complication.

options.text: Give the specific regional-anesthesia treatment: urgent MRI and neurosurgical decompression pathway; treat as epidural hematoma until proven otherwise
options.rationale: The same numb limb may represent expected block, LAST, hematoma, abscess, or compartment syndrome.

options.text: Escalate early to acute pain, surgical, neurology, radiology, or ICU teams as indicated
options.rationale: Time-to-imaging or decompression determines neurological outcome in several complications.

options.text: Document block level, motor power, sensory findings, anticoagulation, drug dose, and serial examinations
options.rationale: Trend documentation differentiates resolving block from new pathology.

options.text: Explain the complication, stop unsafe discharge, and create a follow-up and warning-sign plan
options.rationale: Unclear advice after regional anesthesia can delay treatment of serious complications.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Regional Anesthesia. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on urgent mri and neurosurgical decompression pathway, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Regional Anesthesia, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.
evidence_basis: Hadzic Regional Anesthesia and Acute Pain Management; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

options.text: Give the specific regional-anesthesia treatment: urgent anesthetic review, neurological assessment, and epidural blood patch pathway while excluding alternate causes
options.rationale: The same numb limb may represent expected block, LAST, hematoma, abscess, or compartment syndrome.

options.text: Escalate early to acute pain, surgical, neurology, radiology, or ICU teams as indicated

options.rationale: Unclear advice after regional anesthesia can delay treatment of serious complications.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Regional Anesthesia. title: DKA discovered after induction
topic: DKA discovered after induction
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: After induction, ABG shows pH 7.12, glucose 480, K 5.8, ketones positive. Patient was vomiting preop.

options.text: Verify the abnormal value clinically and repeat bedside testing if the result is incompatible with physiology
options.rationale: Treatment of a spurious value can harm the patient; critical values still need immediate verification.

options.text: Secure ABCs, check ECG, blood gas, glucose, electrolytes, temperature, and urine output
options.rationale: Metabolic crises often present through rhythm, ventilation, or mental-status changes.

options.text: Start the specific metabolic correction: stabilize DKA with insulin, fluids, potassium-guided correction and consider delaying surgery if source control can wait
options.rationale: Definitive treatment depends on the exact biochemical abnormality and rate of change.

options.text: Stop precipitating drugs or fluids and adjust anesthetic, ventilation, and renal/hepatic dosing
options.rationale: Continuing the precipitant defeats correction.

options.text: Repeat objective measurements and watch for overcorrection, rebound, or procedure-related recurrence
options.rationale: Metabolic correction is a process, not a single bolus.

options.text: Plan postoperative monitored care with written electrolyte, glucose, renal, or endocrine follow-up orders
options.rationale: Unsafe ward transfer after a metabolic crisis leads to delayed recurrence recognition.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Metabolic/Endocrine. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on stabilize dka with insulin, fluids, potassium-guided correction and consider delaying surgery if source control can wait, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.

title: Tumor lysis syndrome before emergency laparotomy
topic: Tumor lysis syndrome before emergency laparotomy
source: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
presentation: K 6.8, uric acid high, calcium low, creatinine rising. Needs urgent decompression.

options.text: Call anesthesia, surgeon, ICU, blood bank, and oncology support early; protect oxygenation and circulation
options.rationale: Cancer patients often have narrow reserve from anemia, infection, mass effect, or treatment toxicity.

options.text: Identify the oncology-specific modifier: airway compression, cytopenia, sepsis, cardiotoxicity, metabolic crisis, or bleeding
options.rationale: The cancer diagnosis is less important than the immediate mechanism threatening life.

options.text: Apply the case-specific oncology anesthesia step: stabilize hyperkalemia and arrhythmia risk, hydrate, rasburicase pathway, and coordinate ICU/nephrology before anesthesia
options.rationale: The definitive anesthetic plan must match the oncologic complication rather than follow routine elective sequencing.

options.text: Correct anemia, thrombocytopenia, coagulopathy, infection, electrolytes, or organ dysfunction before or during induction as urgency permits
options.rationale: Peri-induction collapse is common when optimization is skipped in oncology emergencies.

options.text: Use invasive monitoring, ABG, crossmatch, imaging, echo, and specialty consultation according to the dominant risk
options.rationale: Objective risk mapping prevents underestimation of treatment-related disease.

options.text: Plan ICU/HDU care and document the cancer-related anesthesia risks for future procedures
options.rationale: Oncology patients often return for repeated anesthetics; the learning must follow the patient.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Onco-anesthesia. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on stabilize hyperkalemia and arrhythmia risk, hydrate, rasburicase pathway, and coordinate icu/nephrology before anesthesia, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Onco-anesthesia, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.
evidence_basis: Stoelting's Anesthesia and Co-Existing Disease; Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
author: Prof. Dr. Jyotirmay Kirtania

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, consciousness, pain, temperature, glucose, and surgical site immediately
options.rationale: PACU crises often hide behind agitation, pain, or delayed awakening.

options.text: Give oxygen, call anesthesia help, and reverse or remove the most likely immediate threat

options.text: Treat the case-specific PACU problem: support ventilation and check ABG before labeling delayed emergence as residual anesthetic alone

options.rationale: Multiple reversible factors often coexist after anesthesia.

options.text: Delay discharge until objective recovery criteria, pain control, and observation needs are met
options.rationale: A normal-looking patient can deteriorate after early transfer.

options.text: Document event, treatment, response, and future anesthesia precautions
options.rationale: PACU events are key predictors of future anesthetic risk.

title: Discharge decision after difficult airway event

source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e; DAS airway principles; Morgan & Mikhail 7e
demographics: 49M, day-care surgery, grade 4 airway rescued with SGA
presentation: Surgery completed uneventfully. Patient awake and wants discharge. No airway alert document yet.

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, consciousness, pain, temperature, glucose, and surgical site immediately
options.rationale: PACU crises often hide behind agitation, pain, or delayed awakening.

options.text: Give oxygen, call anesthesia help, and reverse or remove the most likely immediate threat
options.rationale: First-minute stabilization prevents progression to respiratory or circulatory arrest.

options.text: Treat the case-specific PACU problem: withhold routine discharge until airway risk is documented, patient informed, and future airway plan provided
options.rationale: Postoperative rescue must target the cause rather than simply sedating or restraining the patient.

options.text: Search for residual anesthetic, opioid, neuromuscular block, bleeding, hypothermia, sepsis, or metabolic cause
options.rationale: Multiple reversible factors often coexist after anesthesia.

options.text: Delay discharge until objective recovery criteria, pain control, and observation needs are met
options.rationale: A normal-looking patient can deteriorate after early transfer.

options.text: Document event, treatment, response, and future anesthesia precautions
options.rationale: PACU events are key predictors of future anesthetic risk.

presentation: GCS 8, SBP 70, SpO2 92, active bleeding. Team prepares standard propofol-suxamethonium RSI.

options.text: Activate trauma/anesthesia help, protect cervical spine, give 100 percent oxygen, and assess massive bleeding
options.rationale: Trauma anesthesia begins with simultaneous airway, spine, breathing, and circulation priorities.

options.text: Choose induction and airway plan that preserves oxygenation and perfusion under full-stomach conditions
options.rationale: Physiological collapse during trauma RSI is often caused by pre-induction shock and hypoxemia.

options.text: Perform the trauma-specific lifesaving step: resuscitate during airway preparation, use hemodynamically appropriate induction, blood ready, and avoid post-induction arrest
options.rationale: The immediate threat may be hemorrhage, TBI physiology, chest injury, burns, or intoxication.

options.text: Use ABG, lactate, hemoglobin, coagulation, FAST/POCUS, imaging, and surgical feedback to guide next steps
options.rationale: Repeated reassessment prevents fixation on the initial injury pattern.

options.text: Handover to ICU with airway, spine, bleeding, analgesia, antibiotics, tetanus, and imaging plan
options.rationale: Trauma outcomes depend on continuity across OT, radiology, and ICU.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Trauma Anesthesia. title: Pipeline oxygen failure during maintenance
topic: Pipeline oxygen failure during maintenance
source: Miller's Anesthesia 10e; Morgan & Mikhail 7e; Oxford Handbook of Anaesthesia 5e
presentation: Oxygen analyzer falls, ventilator still cycling, central alarm sounds. Patient SpO2 96 initially.

options.text: Switch to manual control or backup device, give 100 percent oxygen, and check the patient first
options.rationale: When a machine alarms, patient oxygenation and circulation come before screen interpretation.

options.text: Check connections, gas supply, circuit, vaporizers, valves, scavenging, infusion lines, and power source
options.rationale: Most equipment crises are simple disconnections, wrong settings, or supply failures.

options.text: Correct the specific equipment or monitoring failure: switch to cylinder oxygen, verify FiO2 with analyzer, disconnect pipeline source and ventilate manually if needed
options.rationale: The safe workaround depends on the failed component and available backup.

options.text: Inform surgeon and pause the procedure if monitoring or ventilation is below safe minimum standard
options.rationale: Continuing surgery with unsafe monitoring converts a fixable fault into patient harm.

options.text: Replace the failed device, document readings from alternate monitors, and verify drug delivery independently
options.rationale: Single-monitor reliance is dangerous during equipment failure.

options.text: Remove faulty equipment from service and complete biomedical incident reporting
options.rationale: Future patients remain at risk until the device is quarantined and investigated.

management_logic: This case tests first-minute prioritisation in Equipment/Safety. The learner must identify the dominant threat, act on switch to cylinder oxygen, verify fio2 with analyzer, disconnect pipeline source and ventilate manually if needed, and avoid generic sequencing when the clinical modifier changes the order.
learning_point: In Equipment/Safety, the correct first sequence is dictated by the immediate physiological threat, not by the final diagnosis label.
